questions.description: Fictitious Directive 2021/777 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 31 October 2021 on the harmonisation of g…
questions.text: Fictitious Directive 2021/777 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 31 October 2021 on the harmonisation of gambling services includes amongst its main provisions:- Article 3, which requires setting a total maximum betting daily limit of €500 per person, applicable in all EU Member States for both in-shop and online gambling; and Article 4, which obliges EU Member States to charge a levy of 10% on all individual bets over €200 (as a form of deterrent on gamblers placing excessive stakes). These provisions were to be transposed into the national laws of all EU Member States by 31st January 2023. The Oireachtas has amended several pieces of domestic legislation, including the (fictitious) Gaming and Lotteries Act 1986, the Gambling Control Act 2012 and the Betting Act 1990 to reflect the new requirements set by Fictitious Directive 2021/777 on foot of Government-proposed bills. However, given the complexity of current Irish law on gambling, none of these amendments will come into effect until January 31st 2025. On May 12th 2023, Billy went into his local privately-owned betting shop and placed three separate bets of €300 each on three greyhound races. No levy was charged. He also staked another €225 with a Maltese-registered online gambling website on the outcome of a football match. He won on none of these bets. Over the following week he gambled another €4,500 to try and recoup his losses. Only one bet came good, giving him winnings of €250 and a net loss for that week of €4,250. Billy's gambling in May 2023 has used up all of his savings and placed him in debt. He cannot now afford to pay the mortgage on his home and is threatened with house repossession. Billy has just found out about the existence of Directive 2021/777 on the harmonisation of gambling services. He comes to you as his solicitor for advice on whether he may have any grounds under EU law on: direct effect; and/or state liability for taking an action against either the betting shops and/or Ireland, to try and recoup his gambling losses. Referring to relevant CJEU case-law throughout, advise Billy accordingly.

questions.description: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has chan…
questions.text: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has changed within the EU legal order since its foundation in the 1950s and how the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights has, since it became legally binding, now become the main basis upon which the CJEU ensures that human rights are observed in the Union legal order.

questions.description: Has the introduction of the ordinary legislative procedure under Article 289 TFEU set the type of role for the European …
questions.text: Has the introduction of the ordinary legislative procedure under Article 289 TFEU set the type of role for the European Parliament in the legislative process that should satisfactorily address any outstanding concerns that may have existed before the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon about a lack of democratic decision-making in the creation European Union laws?

questions.description: On 25 January 2020, Carlos, an Argentinian national, married Carmen, a Spanish national. Carmen has never exercised her …
questions.text: On 25 January 2020, Carlos, an Argentinian national, married Carmen, a Spanish national. Carmen has never exercised her right to move freely within the European Union — she has always lived and worked in Spain (mostly part-time on minimum wage). Carlos and Carmen have a daughter, Camila, who is a Spanish national. Camila was born in Spain on 15 March 2014. Carlos has made an application for a residence permit in Spain on the basis that he is a family member of a Union citizen. Carlos has had his application for the residence permit refused by the Spanish authorities. It was refused on two grounds: (1) that Carlos has a criminal record in Spain (he has a criminal conviction for driving under the influence of drugs; another conviction for driving without a licence; and a third conviction for driving without any insurance — these convictions all resulted from the same incident when Carlos was pulled over by police while driving erratically, although he had not crashed the car nor had he injured himself or any other person); and (2) that Carmen does not have sufficient financial resources to support herself and her family members. Carlos has now appealed the decision to refuse to grant him a residency permit on the basis that he, Carmen and Camila could be forced to leave the European Union because of the relationship of dependency between them. Referring to relevant provisions of the TFEU, EU secondary legislation and CJEU case law, advise Carlos on the strength of his appeal against the decision to refuse to grant him a residency permit in Spain as the family member of Union citizen(s).

questions.description: The Minister for Health in Ireland has recently introduced a (fictitious) set of regulations designed to help combat the…
questions.text: The Minister for Health in Ireland has recently introduced a (fictitious) set of regulations designed to help combat the significant detrimental health implications caused by the hazardous consumption of alcohol. Included in these (fictitious) Public Health (Alcohol Consumption Reduction) Regulations 2024 is a requirement that all beer sold in supermarkets and shops in Ireland must have a maximum alcohol content of 4% by volume. Beer sold in pubs and restaurants can have an alcohol content by volume above 4%. The Wild Atlantic Brewery Ltd., based in Westport, County Mayo, produces a very popular pale ale, with an alcohol by volume content of 6% and a very popular lager, with an alcohol by volume content of 7%. Both products are only sold in shops and are not available in pubs or restaurants. The Wild Atlantic Brewery Ltd. currently only supply their products to the Irish market. Many Irish breweries have already indicated that they may be willing to alter or reformulate their products for shop and supermarket sale, reducing the alcohol content of their products to 4% by volume or below. The Wild Atlantic Brewery Ltd. maintain that reducing the alcohol content of their most popular beers, given that these have a relatively high alcohol content, would remove some of their main characteristics and that the modified product would not appeal to as many consumers. The Wild Atlantic Brewery Ltd. have come to you for advice on whether these new (fictitious) Public Health (Alcohol Consumption Reduction) Regulations 2024, which are due to come into effect in February 2025, are compatible with EU law on the free movement of goods. Referring to CJEU case-law on Articles 34 TFEU and 36 TFEU throughout, advise the Wild Atlantic Brewery Ltd. On the compatibility or otherwise of the 2024 Irish Regulations with EU law.

questions.description: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with rega…
questions.text: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with regard to certain Union acts; and (ii) judicial attempts that have been made to reform the test for individual concern in particular, analyse critically whether it has become any easier in 2024 for non-privileged applicants to seek judicial review of EU acts than it was when Advocate General Jacobs delivered his opinion on case C-50/00, UPA v Council [2002] ECR 1-6677. Your answer should include an analysis of the case-law of the Union judicature interpreting Article 263(4) TFEU.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each casenote: (a) the core …
questions.text: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each casenote: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU; and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: Case C-438/05, International Transport Workers Federation and Finnish Seamen's Union v Viking Line ABP and OCI Viking Line Eesti [2007] ECR 1-10779; Case C-384/93, Alpine Investments BV v Minister van Financién [1995] ECR I-1141; Case 140/79, Chemial Farmaceutici v DAF SpA [1981] ECR 1; Cases T-778/16, Ireland v Commission and T-892/16, Apple Sales International and Apple Operations Europe v Commission Case 46/76, Bauhuis v Netherlands [1977] ECR 5; Case 106/77, Amministrazione delle Finanze dello Stato v Simmenthal SpA [1978] ECR 629; Case 262/88, Barber v Guardian Royal Exchange Assurance Group [1990] ECR 1889; Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Siochäna v Workplace Relations Commission ECLI:EU:C:2018:979.

questions.description: Explain, by reference to relevant CJEU case-law, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted practice" in Art…
questions.text: Explain, by reference to relevant CJEU case-law, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted practice" in Article 101 (1) TFEU. Also explain, by reference to relevant CJEU case-law, how a "dominant position" can be held by "one or more undertakings" under Article 102 TFEU.

questions.description: On 10 May 2023, Directive 2023/970 of the European Parliament and of the Council was published in the Official Journal o…
questions.text:
On 10 May 2023, Directive 2023/970 of the European Parliament and of the Council was published in the Official Journal of the EU. The Directive is designed to strengthen the application of the principle of equal pay for equal work or work of equal value between men and women. The Directive entered into force on 6 June 2023 and all EU Member States will have to implement all of the provisions of the Directive by 7 June 2026. The Directive includes several provisions designed to guarantee pay transparency in both the public and the private sectors. Article 5(2) of the Directive specifically provides that: an employer shall not ask applicants about their pay history during their current or 
previous employment relationships. The Irish Minister for Enterprise, Trade and Employment has issued a [fictional] press statement stating that Article 5(2) of Directive 2023/970 will not be transposed into Irish Law as knowing about the pay history of a successful job applicant is crucial to negotiating and determining what salary level should be set in a public sector post and is essential to ensuring that public funds are protected. A private employers' group has also expressed concerns about Article 5(2) of the Directive, stating that it should not apply in the private sector either as it will lead to wage inflation across all sectors. A [fictional] trade union, the Irish Worker Solidarity Association (IWSA), has 80,000 members in Ireland across both the public and private sectors. The IWSA has come to you for advice on whether the Irish Government may be compelled to transpose all provisions of Directive 2023/970, including Article 5(2) thereof, in full by 7 June 2026. Referring to relevant CJEU case-law on the Treaty obligations of Member States to transpose Union directives and on the capacity for direct effect of such directives throughout your answer, advise the Irish Worker Solidarity Association on this point.

questions.description: In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLI:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that: [... ] it should be borne in mind …
questions.text:
In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLI:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that: [... ] it should be borne in mind that the fundamental rights guaranteed by the Charter are applicable in all situations governed by EU law and that they must, therefore, be complied with inter alia where national legislation falls within the scope of EU law. [para. 63] In this Judgment, the CJEU was addressing issues related to Article 17 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union on the right to property. With reference to the case-law of the CJEU concerning the Charter throughout your answer, assess how the CJEU in that case-law, since the Charter was declared in December 2000, but 
especially since it came into force on 1 December 2009 along with the Treaty of Lisbon, has 
allowed provisions of the Charter to be used to challenge a wide range of both EU and national legislative measures in the manner described in Commission v Hungary (above).

questions.description: Outline, by reference to relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of the Union judicature, the rol…
questions.text: Outline, by reference to relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of the Union judicature, the roles of each of: (i) the European Parliament; (ii) the Commission; and (iii) the Council in establishing new or amending legislative, delegated and implementing acts.

questions.description: Paolo is an Italian national who is married to an Irish national, Pauline. The couple left Italy in 2016 to move to Irel…
questions.text: Paolo is an Italian national who is married to an Irish national, Pauline. The couple left Italy in 2016 to move to Ireland as Pauline was offered a job in Dublin. Paolo has never worked in Ireland. They have two children, Paul and Paula, who were both born in Italy in 2013 and who also resided in Italy until 2016. The children are both Italian/Irish dual nationals. Shortly after the family moved to Ireland, Paolo and Pauline separated. They divorced in 2021. Paolo, who still resides with Pauline, Paul and Paula in Ireland to be near his children as they grow up, has become almost deaf in his left ear and his hearing is also deteriorating in his right ear. The condition is inoperable, and Paolo was informed by his audiologist in March 2023 that the only solution to help his worsening hearing is the use of hearing aids. Paolo, who is still not working, cannot afford to pay for the hearing aids, which in his case will cost at least €1,900, has now applied to get a financial grant towards this cost under the Treatment Benefit Scheme, which is run in Ireland by the Department of Social Protection. The grant, however, is only available to insured workers, the self-employed and retired people who have the required number of social insurance (PRSI) contributions. Under the Scheme, the Department will pay a grant once every four years of up to €1,000 towards a pair of hearing aids if required. While Paolo has not worked in Ireland, a dependant spouse, civil partner or cohabitant can still qualify for treatment benefit on the spouse's Social Insurance record. Pauline has made sufficient Social Insurance contributions since 2016. Paolo has applied to the Department of Social Protection to become affiliated to the Treatment Benefit Scheme. This request was refused in April 2023 on the ground that he was neither employed nor self-employed nor retired in Ireland, and he was no longer the spouse of a worker who qualified for the Treatment Benefit Scheme. Paolo appealed this decision to an appeal panel at the Department of Social Protection. The appeal was dismissed in July 2023. Paolo has since been engaged in illegal activity in a bid to fund the cost of his new hearing aids. He was caught driving off a car ferry in Dublin Port in late July 2023 with a consignment of 20,000 counterfeit cigarettes, which were destined for the Irish black market. He also resisted arrest at the time, including punching the ribs of a member of An Garda Síochána who had arrived to assist. Paolo received a suspended sentence of two years' imprisonment when he was before the Circuit Court last month, as this was just his second offence — he was also convicted of shoplifting when he was on holidays in Ireland in 2012, for which he was fined €300. However, on this occasion, the Minister for Justice has also ordered Paolo's deportation from Ireland as part of a concerted Government campaign against counterfeit cigarette smuggling. Paolo now wants to challenge: (i) the deportation order made against him; and (ii) the refusal to accept him on to the Treatment Benefit Scheme. He claims that as an EU citizen, with children residing in Ireland, and having been married to a worker through whom he should be qualified for the Treatment Benefit Scheme, that he is entitled to remain in Ireland and to receive the hearing aid grant. Referring to relevant CJEU case-law and TFEU Treaty provisions and EU legislation throughout, advise Paolo about whether EU law on free movement and citizenship may help his challenges against both the deportation order and the refusal to include him in the Treatment Benefit Scheme.

questions.description: The French Government has recently introduced a [fictional] law, No. 23-77, that all new light fittings sold in France a…
questions.text: The French Government has recently introduced a [fictional] law, No. 23-77, that all new light fittings sold in France after 31 December 2023 must be automatic — switching on when someone enters a room or other lit space and then switching themselves off 90 seconds after everyone has left a room or lit space. The French Government has deemed that this is a necessary part of reducing overall energy consumption in France as part of national energy security policy and to contribute to reducing the country's carbon emissions. Brillo Bulbs Ltd. is a company based in Blessington, County Wicklow, which manufactures light fittings, mostly for export. France is the company's second biggest market (34% of all of its sales) after Germany (40% of all of its sales). No Brillo Bulbs Ltd. products are automatic. All require a manual switching on and a manual switching off. Brillo Bulbs Ltd. wants to complain to the EU Commission about this new [fictional] French law. Brillo Bulbs Ltd. insists that the operation of lights, manual and automatic combined, accounts for less than 2% of France's overall energy consumption. Referring to relevant TFEU provisions and case law of the CJEU throughout, advise Brillo Bulbs Ltd. on the compatibility, or otherwise, of [fictional] French Law No. 23-77 with EU rules on the free movement of goods.

questions.description: The Irish Government has become concerned about the bad publicity that Ireland has been receiving as a destination for t…
questions.text:
The Irish Government has become concerned about the bad publicity that Ireland has been receiving as a destination for tourists due to a number of stories that have appeared in the 
international media about physical attacks on foreign tourists and also about how expensive hotel rooms in Ireland are and about the consistently bad weather during the summer months. As a response, the Irish Minister for Tourism, Culture, Arts, Gaeltacht, Sport and Media has proposed a number of [fictional] measures designed to make Ireland a more attractive holiday destination for overseas travellers. These proposals include: - (i) a further cut to the Value Added Tax (VAT) rate for the hospitality and tourism sectors from the standard rate of 13.5%, currently already reduced to 9%, but now to be further reduced to 5%;  a subsidy to be paid to all Irish-owned airlines of €5.70 per passenger travelling on all inbound flights with these Irish-owned airlines to airports in Ireland; (iii) an interest-free loan scheme that can be used by tourist attractions to assist with their public liability insurance costs. A [fictional] Government Department for Tourism, Culture, Arts, Gaeltacht, Sport and Media 
report, published in early September 2023, and based on interviews with overseas travellers to Ireland during the summer of 2023, found that incoming tourist numbers would likely decline by 3-5% during the summer of 2024, primarily due to the high cost of hotel accommodation, poor weather and embassy advice about the frequency of physical attacks on overseas tourists. Several operators, including a German airline, a French hotel group and a private body which represents the hospitality sector in Spain, have raised concerns regarding the proposals listed above with the EU Commission, claiming that, if implemented, they would amount to unlawful State Aid. Referring to relevant legal sources throughout, write a report for the EU Commission, assessing whether or not these Irish Ministerial proposals may amount to State aid under Article 107 TFEU and, if so, whether they may, nonetheless, be considered thereunder, as well as under relevant EU legislative provisions on State aid as interpreted in the relevant case-law of the Union judicature and by reference to any applicable EU Commission notices applying those provisions, to be compatible with the internal market.

questions.description:
Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in 
each case-note: (a) the cor…
questions.text:
Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in 
each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU; and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs- og Selskabsstyre/sen [1999] ECR 1 1459 [MAR 22, MAR 21, MAR 20, OCT 19]; c-352/98,  Laboratoires  Pharmaceutiques   Bergaderm v Commission 
ECLI:EU:C:2000:361 [MAR 23, MAR 20]; Case 33/74, Van Binsbergen v Bestuur van de Bedrijfsvereniging voor de Metaalnijverheid [1974] ECR 1299 [OCT 22]; case 140/79, Chemial Farmaceutici v DAF SpA [1981] ECR 1 [MAR 20, OCT 19]; Case 87/75, Bresciani v Amministrazione Italiana del/e Finanze [1976] ECR 129 
[OCT 22, OCT 20]; 6/64, Costa v ENEL ECLI:EU:C:1964:66 [MAR 23, OCR 22]; Case C-236/09, Association belge des Consommateurs Test-Achats v Conseil des 
ministres EU:C:2011:100 [OCT 22, MAR 22, MAR 20]; Cases 56 and 58/64, Établissements Consten SARL and Grundig-Verkaufs-GmbH v Commission [1966] ECR 299 [OCT 20].

questions.description: A [fictional] AgriSci report published in October 2022 concluded that the consumption of the [fictional] food additive E…
questions.text:
A [fictional] AgriSci report published in October 2022 concluded that the consumption of the [fictional] food additive E1111, which is a flavouring that is primarily used in raspberry cream biscuits, has a number of side effects in children. This includes causing hormonal changes in male teenagers and hyperactivity in younger children. The EU Commission have acted on the basis of these conclusions and published a proposal for a new [fictional] EU Regulation 5555/2023, which will ban the use of E1111 at any level in food production. This Regulation 5555/2023 is due to come into full effect by 31 October 2025. AgriSci is a very well-respected and independent food science organisation, with a reputation for only employing the very best scientists. Belters Ltd. is an Irish food-additives manufacturer. In September 2023, it produced 88% of all E1111 used in the manufacture of products containing this additive across the EU. Given the popularity of raspberry cream biscuits, the sale of E1111 by Belters Ltd. accounts for an average of 65% of its total annual revenue over the past five years. Belters Ltd. has come to you for advice on how it could challenge the introduction of EU 
Regulation 5555/2023. In particular, it is concerned that it may have no legal standing even to bring a challenge before the Union judicature on the introduction of Regulation 5555/2023. It has commissioned its own expert report, which has come to different conclusions to those presented in the AgriSci report of October 2022. The Belters Ltd. report has concluded that, while there may be some side effects to consuming E1111, young persons would have to consume unrealistically copious quantities of this additive (the equivalent of that contained in at least one hundred raspberry cream biscuits per day, every day) before they could possibly suffer any detrimental impact to their health. Advise Belters Ltd. on whether it would have locus standi to bring a challenge to the legality of Regulation 5555/2023 before the General Court. Your answer should include a detailed analysis of the relevant case-law of the Union judicature interpreting Article 263(4) TFEU and how it relates to the admissibility of an action like that being considered by Belters Ltd.

questions.description: (Fictional) Directive (EU) 2021/7777 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 25 April 2021 on measures for a hi…
questions.text: (Fictional) Directive (EU) 2021/7777 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 25 April 2021 on measures for a high common level of cybersecurity across the Union provides, amongst other things, the following:- Article 7: ‘Each Member State shall adopt a national cybersecurity strategu with a view to achieving and maintaining a high level of cybersecurity [including] a plan to enhance the general level of cybersecurity awareness among citizens’ and Article 44: ‘By 26 June 2022, Member States shall adopt and publish the measures necessary to comply with this Directive. They shall immediately inform the Commission thereof. They shall apply those measures from 27 June 2022. Ireland has not published or adopted a national cybersecurity strategy by March 2023. There is also currently no plan published that is aimed at enhancing the general level of cybersecurity awareness campaign run by CyberTrust in the national media since May 2022 designed to highlight the most commonly experienced cybersecurity issues. CyberTrust are a private organisation who are co-funded equally by a group of private computer manufacturers based in Ireland and the governmental Department of the Environment, Climate and Communications. The Minister also appoints the Executive Director of CyberTrust, who in turn has to report back to the Minister on the activities of CyberTrust on an annual basis. Harry is a 77 year old man who lives in Galway and has only started using computers and the internet in recent months. Last month, he was sent an email about a lottery win in Canada, which he could claim by sending in his credit card details – which he did. The email was fraudulent and Harry’s credit card was subsequently used by the fraudsters to make purchases amounting to €4,800. Harry is licensed. He claims to have known nothing about the potential for email to be used to phish for his credit card details in this way and insists that the State should be doing more to protec vulnerable and inexperienced internet users from this type of activity. Referring to relevant caselaw of the CJEU throughout your answer, advise Harry on whether he can possibly use the provisions set out in Articles 7 and 44 of the fictional Directive (EU) 2021/7777 to assist his claim for compensation from the State for the financial losses that he has suffered.

questions.description: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has chan…
questions.text: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has changed within the EU legal order since its foundation in the 1950s and how the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights has, since it became legally binding, now become the main basis upon which the CJEU ensures that human rights are observed in the Union legal order.

questions.description: Outline, by reference to relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of the Union judicature, the rol…
questions.text: Outline, by reference to relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of the Union judicature, the roles of each of: (i) the European Parliament; (ii) the Commission; and (iii) the Council in establishing new or amending legislative, delegated and implementing acts. Assess also whether the changes made to legislative procedures by the Lisbon Treaty amendments have improved the democratic legitimacy of decision-making within the European Union.

questions.description: There is a shortage of second-hand cars available in Ireland at present. As a result, second-hand cars are now imported …
questions.text: There is a shortage of second-hand cars available in Ireland at present. As a result, second-hand cars are now imported from other EU Member States at record high levels — rising from an average of 1,000 imported EU cars per year in 2021 up to 15,000 imported EU cars in 2022. Before exportation, these cars are adapted from right sided driving to left sided driving specifically for the Irish market. The Irish Revenue Commissioners have adapted the method used to calculate vehicle registration tax (VRT) on these imported EU cars during 2022. They have added the cost of adapting the vehicles for the Irish market and they have added a new carbon emission cost account for the work required to make these modifications to the imported EU cars. As a result, imports of second-hand cars from other EU Member States have become significantly more expensive in Ireland. For example, the average VRT on a 2015 Renault Clio 1.4 which has been driven fo approximately 100,000 kms was €1,600 in 2021. The same car, imported from another EU Member State is now charged a VRT on importation of €1,500, plus 35% of the modification cost (35% of €2,000 = €700) plus the new carbon emission cost of carrying out these modifications (€300), raising the VRT due in 2023 to a total of €2,500. A group of both Irish importers and EU exporters have come together to raise this issue with the EU Commission, claiming that the new system. of calculating these vehicle registration taxes on modified second-hand EU cars breaches TFEU rules on taxation, as set out in Article 110 TFEU. Referring to relevant CJEU case-law throughout your answer, advise the EU Commission on whether it should consider bringing legal action against Ireland due to these changes to the Revenue Commissioners’ methods and rates of calculating VRT in this way. Your advice should also address the pre-litigation steps that the EU Commission would have to follow prior to initiating an action under Article 258 TFEU against Ireland.

questions.description: Sylvester is a French national who moved to Dublin in March 2019. Initially, he was not successful in securing employmen…
questions.text: Sylvester is a French national who moved to Dublin in March 2019. Initially, he was not successful in securing employment in Ireland but during this period he lived with his brother who did not charge him any rent or for any utility bills and he lived off his own modest savings for nearly a full year. In March 2020, Sylvester did obtain some casual work in a launderette, where he still works. There are no guaranteed hours of work. Some weeks Sylvester does not get any work at all, but most weeks he at least gets some hours of paid work, usually in a range between 8 and 14 hours for which he is currently paid at the national minimum wage of €11.30 per hour. He has continued to live with his brother and now makes some financial contributions towards utility bills. In August 2022 Sylvester applied for a place on a two-year college course in animation, starting in September of that year in Art College Dublin. The enrolment fees for both Irish and EU students are €1,500 per year. Irish students earning below €300 per week at the time of their application are also  entitled to apply for a State-provided maintenance grant towards their living costs of €3,000 per year plus payment of 50% of their enrolment fees. Sylvester has applied for the State-provided maintenance grant and enrolment fee reduction. His application has been denied because the scheme is currently only open to Irish applicants. Sylvester has now been sent a bill for the full €1,500 enrolment fee, payable immediately at the risk of being expelled from the College. He has also had to cut back on his hours of work at the launderette since starting the course in animation due to his college commitments related to full-time attendance requirements and coursework completion. Referring to relevant CJEU case-law and EU legislation throughout your answer, advise Sylvester on whether he can bring a legal challenge about the compatibility of this student mainte:iance and fee-reduction scheme with relevant aspects of EU law.

questions.description: With reference to relevant CJEU case-law and Commission decisions, analyse critically the extent to which Article 106 TF…
questions.text: With reference to relevant CJEU case-law and Commission decisions, analyse critically the extent to which Article 106 TFEU provides for an exception to the application of EU competition law, particularly Articles 101 and 102 TFEU, and the scope and limits to any such exception as regards public undertakings, undertakings to which Member States have granted special or exclusive rights and undertakings entrusted by Member States with the operation of services of general economic interest.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU; and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU (d) its judgment: C-267/91, Keck and Mithouare ECLI:E:L:C:1993:905; C-352/98, Laboratoires Pharmaceutiques Bergaderm v Commission ECLI:EU:C:2000:361; C-384/93, Alpine Investments v Minister van Financien ECLI:EU:C:1995:126; 46/76, Bauhuis v, Netherlands ECLI:EU:C:1977:6; 6/64, Costco v ENEL  ECLI:EU:C:1964:66; 96/85, Jenkins v Kingsgate ECLI:EU:C:1981:80; C-.200/02, Zhu and Chen v Secretary of State for Home Dept. ECLI:EU:C:2004:639; C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Commissioner of An Garda Siochana v Workplace Relations Commission ECLI:EU:C:2018:979.

questions.description: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with rega…
questions.text: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with regard to certain Union acts; and (ii) judicial attempts that have been made to reform the test for individual concern in particular, analyse critically whether it has become any easier in 2023 for non-privileged applicants to seek judicial review of EU acts than it was when Advocate General Jacobs delivered his opinion on Case C-50/00, UPA v Council [2002] ECR 1-6677. Your answer should include an analysis of the CJEU case-law interpreting Article 263(4) TFEU.

questions.description: In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLl:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that: [... ] it should be borne in mind …
questions.text: In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLl:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that: [... ] it should be borne in mind that the fundamental rights guaranteed by the Charter are applicable in all situations governed by EU law and that they must, therefore, be complied with inter alia where national legislation falls within the scope of EU law. [para. 63] In this Judgment, the CJEU was addressing issues related to Article 17 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union on the right to property. With reference to the case law of the CJEU concerning the Charter throughout your answer, assess how the CJEU in that case law, since the Charter was declared in December 2000, but especially since it came into force in December 2009 along with the Treaty of Lisbon, allowed provisions of the Charter to be used to challenge a wide range of both EU and national legislative measures in the manner described in Commission v Hungary (above).

questions.description: Has the introduction of the ordinary legislative procedure under Article 289 TFEU set the type of role for the European …
questions.text: Has the introduction of the ordinary legislative procedure under Article 289 TFEU set the type of role for the European Parliament in the legislative process that should satisfactorily address any outstanding concerns that may have existed before the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon about a lack of democratic decision-making in the creation of European Union laws?

questions.description: Fictitious Directive 2020/777 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 30 June 2020 on the harmonisation of gamb…
questions.text: Fictitious Directive 2020/777 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 30 June 2020 on the harmonisation of gambling services includes amongst its main provisions: Article 3, which requires setting a total maximum betting daily limit of €500 per person, applicable in all EU Member States for both in-shop and online gambling; and Article 4, which obliges EU Member States to charge of levy of 10% on all individual bets over €200 (as a form of deterrent on gamblers placing excessive stakes). These provisions were to be transposed into the national laws of all EU Member States by 31st December 2021. The Irish Government have amended several pieces of domestic legislation, including the (fictitious) Gaming and Lotteries Act 1986, the Gambling Control Act 2012 and the Betting Act 1990 to reflect the new requirements set by Fictitious Directive 2020/777. However, given the complexity of current Irish law on gambling, none of these amendments will come into effect until January 31st 2023. On May 12th 2022 Billy went into his local privately-owned betting shop and placed  three separate bets of €300 each on three greyhound races. He also staked another €225 with a French-registered online gambling website on the outcome of a football match. He won on none of these bets. Over the following week he gambled another €4,500 to try and recoup his losses. Only one bet came good, giving him winnings of €250 and a net loss for that week of €4,250. Billy's gambling in May 2022 has used up all of his savings and placed him in debt. He cannot now afford to pay the mortgage on his home and is threatened with house repossession. Billy has just found out about the existence of Directive 2020/777 on the harmonisation of gambling services. He comes to you as his solicitor for advice on whether he may have any grounds under EU law for taking an action against either the betting shops and/or the Irish Government. Referring to relevant CJEU case law throughout, advise Billy.

questions.description:
When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on Case C- 
50/2000, UPA v Council, that: "[. ..] an…
questions.text:
When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on Case C- 
50/2000, UPA v Council, that: "[. ..] an individual should be regarded as individually concerned within the meaning of the fourth paragraph of Article 230 EC [now Article 263 TFEU] by a Community measure where, by reason of his particular circumstances, the measure has, or is liable to have, a substantial adverse effect on his interests" He was clearly articulating that there was at that time a lack of effective judicial protection for non-privileged applicants who sought a judicial review of acts of the European Union's institutions. Firstly, explain briefly, by reference to relevant contemporaneous case-law, the concerns that the Advocate General was addressing here. Secondly, make an assessment of whether these concerns have been adequately addressed by the 
changes that were made to Article 230 EC by what is now contained in Article 263 TFEU and relevant case-law of the Union judicature interpreting those changes.

questions.description:
Answer Part (A) OR Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. 
 
Part (A) The Irish Minister of the Environment, Climate and C…
questions.text:
Answer Part (A) OR Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. 
 
Part (A) The Irish Minister of the Environment, Climate and Communications has launched a public consultation as part of the process of introducing new, and updating existing, national measures designed to reduce the consumption of fossil fuels in Ireland. Amongst these are proposals to: - introduce an additional charge of €0.50 per kilogram of soap produced from  petroleum but reduce charges per kilogram of soap produced from rapeseed oil from €0.40 to €0.05; and prohibit the use of domestic electric car chargers where the electricity used to charge these cars is produced from fossil fuels. You have been approached by two companies - Sudds Ltd., an Irish manufacturer of soaps which currently uses petroleum imported from other EU Member States to make its products (rapeseed oil is widely manufactured in Ireland whereas petroleum is not); and ltalycity S.p.A., who manufacture domestic electric car chargers and fear that their sales in Ireland may be detrimentally impacted if the proposals come to fruition. Referring to relevant case law of the CJEU throughout, advise both Sudds Ltd. and ltalycity S.p.A. on the compatibility of these proposals with EU law on the free movement of goods. 
  
Part (B) Write a legal opinion, addressing relevant Treaty provisions and CJEU case-law, on whether the introduction by the Irish Government of an additional €2 tax on the sale of every bottle of wine from off-licences and restaurants may be contrary to Article 110 TFEU. You may presume that Ireland produces very little wine when compared to other alcoholic beverages that are not subject to the new additional €2 specific tax.

questions.description: Caroline is a French national who moved to Ireland in March 2016 to join a commune based in Connemara. Caroline resided …
questions.text: Caroline is a French national who moved to Ireland in March 2016 to join a commune based in Connemara. Caroline resided with the commune from March 2016, where she was provided with a room and board in return for making pottery in the workshop. All pottery was sold to the public and the revenue generated from this was used to finance the activities of the commune. In May 2022, Caroline fell out with one of her fellow commune potters who had accused her of stealing some pottery that she had made and later deliberately breaking it. Caroline denied the accusations and felt so aggrieved at the lack of support from her fellow commune residents that she felt that she had to leave the commune, even though she had nowhere else to go. After sleeping on the streets of Clifden for the first few nights after leaving the commune, Caroline obtained some casual work on a fruit farm where she was paid according to the amount of fruit that she picked. On an average day, Caroline would be paid €35 - €40 for eight hours of work. The minimum wage in Ireland is €10.50 per hour and the living wage is estimated to be €12.17 per hour in 2022. During this time, Caroline started squatting in the holiday home of a partner in a large Dublin law firm who, when he found out and saw the extensive and costly damage that she had done to his house, reported it to An Garcia Siochana. Caroline was charged and convicted of criminal damage, being sentenced to eighteen months in prison in July 2022. As Caroline's solicitor, you have been advised that the Department of Justice is seeking to deport Caroline back to France as soon as her sentence is served. Caroline does not want to go back to France but now wants to return to the commune in Connemara if they accept that she did not deliberately steal or break any other potter's work. Referring to relevant Treaty provisions, legislation and CJEU case-law throughout, advise Caroline on whether she may be protected from deportation under EU law.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the Union judicature, setting out in each case-note: …
questions.text: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the Union judicature, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the Union judicature, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgement: Case C-34/09, Gerardo Ruiz Zambrano v ONEm [2011] ECR 1-1177; Case 33/74, JHM van Binsbergen v Bestuur van de Bedrijfsvereniging voor de Metaalnijverheid [1974] ECR 1299; Cases T-778/16, Ireland v Commission, and T-892/16, Apple Sales International and Apple Operations Europe v Commission ECLl:EU:T:2020:338; Case 87/75, CO Bresciani v Amministrazione ltaliana de/le Finanze [1976] ECR 129; Case C-236/09, Association Beige des Consommateurs Test-Achats ASBL and others v Conseil des ministres [2011] ECR 1-773; Case 6/64, Flaminio Costa v ENEL [1964] ECR 585; Case C-415/93, Union Royale Beige des Societes de Football Association ASBL v Jean-Marc Bosman [1995] ECR 1-4921; Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Siochana v Workplace Relations Commission ECLl:EU:C:2018:979;

questions.description: Explain, by reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concert…
questions.text: Explain, by reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted practice" in Article 101(1) TFEU. In addition, explain separately, by reference to relevant case-law, how a "dominant position" can be held by "one or more undettakings" under Article 102 TFEU.

questions.description: Answer Part (a) or Part (b) of Question One. Do not answer both parts of this question. (a) What are the general princip…
questions.text: Answer Part (a) or Part (b) of Question One. Do not answer both parts of this question. (a) What are the general principles of EU law and, using examples from the case-law of the CJEU, discuss how are they used by the CJEU to protect individuals and to ensure that the European Union institutions, and the Member States when implementing EU law, act in accordance with the rule of law? OR (b) In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLI:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that:- [...] it should be borne in mind that the fundamental rights guaranteed by the Charter are applicable in all situations governed by EU law and that they must, therefore, be complied with inter alia where national legislation falls within the scope of EU law. [para. 63] In this Judgment, the CJEU was addressing issues related to Article 17 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union on the right to property. With reference to the case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, assess how the CJEU has allowed the Charter of Fundamental Rights to be used to challenge a wide range of EU and national legislative measures in the manner described in Commission v Hungary (above).

questions.description:
Outline, by reference to relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of 
the Union judicature, the ro…
questions.text:
Outline, by reference to relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of 
the Union judicature, the roles of (i) the European Parliament; (ii) the Commission; and (iii) 
the Council in establishing new or amending legislative, delegated and implementing acts. Assess also whether the changes made to legislative procedures by the Lisbon Treaty 
amendments have improved the democratic legitimacy of decision-making within the 
European Union.

questions.description: The EU Commission introduced (fictional) Directive (EU) No 2020/2020 on 29 February 2020, setting a series of harmonisin…
questions.text: The EU Commission introduced (fictional) Directive (EU) No 2020/2020 on 29 February 2020, setting a series of harmonising provisions on the use and disposal of single-use plastic. Article 12 of the directive set the date for implementation as 1 April 2021. Article 4 of the EU Directive provides that certain products should no longer be made with single use plastic. This list is currently limited to plastic forks, plastic knives and plastic straws (but can be added to using delegated legislation). Producers could continue to sell off any single use plastic products that they had already produced by 1 April 2021, but they were no longer permitted to make these products after this date. Article 5 of the Directive provides that single use plastic products should never be disposed by incineration due to the potential release of toxic fumes from this. The (fictional) European Union (Single Use Plastics) Regulations 2021, SI No. 987/2021, were introduced on 30 November 2021 to transpose EU Directive 2020/2020 into Irish Law. They came into effect on the date of publication. Portmarnock Plastics Ltd. have been producing plastic products in Dublin since 2005, including plastic cutlery, both knives and forks. As they knew that they would no longer be able sell these products in Ireland under the terms of the Directive and the Irish Regulations, they switched to only using recyclable plastic in their products by the end of March 2021. Recyclable plastic is more expensive to use in production. Portlaoise Plastics plc continued to manufacture single-use plastic knives and forks right up until the end of November 2021 and have since been continuing to sell off this remaining stock that they produced. Portmarnock Plastics Ltd lost out on a lucrative contract to supply disposable cutlery to a large Irish supermarket chain in late December 2021. They had a contract to supply these knives and forks to the supermar ket chain on an ongoing basis since soon after they went into business in 2005. However, on this occasion they were not able to compete on price with what Portlaoise Plastics were charging for their single-use stock as Portmarnock Plastics had sold off the last of their single-use stock by August 2021. Roger is a resident of Ringsend. He has chronic asthma, which he claims has been made worse by the toxic fumes emitted from the incinerator beside his home. The incinerator is still used in March 2022 for the burning of single use plastics. The (fictional) European Union (Single Use Plastics) Regulations 2021 contained no provision reflecting what was set out in Article 5 of EU Directive 2020/2020 prohibiting this practice. Referring to CJEU case-law throughout, advise both (i) Portmarnock Plastics Ltd.; and (ii) Roger on any remedies that they may have under EU law arising out of the way that EU Directive 2020/2020 has been transposed into Irish Law.

questions.description: Has the seminal decision of the CJEU in Case 25/62 Plaumann v Commission continued to make it 'literally impossible' for…
questions.text: Has the seminal decision of the CJEU in Case 25/62 Plaumann v Commission continued to make it 'literally impossible' for a non-privileged applicant to 'institute proceedings against an act' of the European Union under Article 263(4) TFEU? In your opinion, refer to relevant case-law, legislation and to attempts that may have been made (particularly by the amendment introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon), to make it easier for non- privileged applicants to use Article 263 TFEU to review the legality of acts of the EU institutions. Consider also how any such attempts have been interpreted by the Union judicature in its relevant case-law.

questions.description: Following a series of scandals surrounding the contamination of powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula produced …
questions.text: Following a series of scandals surrounding the contamination of powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula produced in France, China and Brazil, the Irish Government, through the Department of Health, wants to establish a (fictional) scheme for the sampling and analysing of all such products, regardless of whether they are manufactured in Ireland, in other EU Member States, or in any third country. The (fictional) EU Directive 2017/678 on powdered baby milk and follow-on infant formula allows EU Member States to carry out sampling and analysis of these products, but it does not oblige any Member State to do so. According to the Irish Government’s proposal, the costs of operating this scheme will be met by the imposition of a 10% value added taxon all imported powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula. The products of Irish producers will not be taxed in this way, but will, instead, have to be certified regarding the sampling and analysis undertaken and detailing the results of these investigations. Iris producers will have to pay for these tests and the related certification themselves. It is estimated that this will add an additional production cost to the Irish producers of powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula of between 8-12%. You have been approached to advise the Irish Department of Health about the compatibility of the proposed scheme with EU law. In your opinion, referring to relevant case law of the CJEU and having regard to the (fictional) EU Directive 2017/678, consider the compatibility of the proposed scheme with the TFEU provisions on charges of equivalent effect to customs duties and internal taxation.

questions.description: Betty is a 77 year old woman who has lived for all of her life in Belmullet, County Mayo. She represented the county cam…
questions.text: Betty is a 77 year old woman who has lived for all of her life in Belmullet, County Mayo. She represented the county camogie team for eleven years, from 1967-1978, when injuries to her right leg and lower back forced her to retire from the sport. Caused in part by a resultant limp when she walks, Betty developed problems with her left hip in the early 2000s. This has got progressively worse. When she visited her GP about the pain in 2008, she (the GP) referred Betty on to an orthopaedic consultant who told her that she would eventually need a hip replacement operation, but probably not for a few more years. The pain and discomfort in Betty's left hip continued to get worse, so much so that she has required the support of a walking stick since 2012.She visited a different orthopaedic consultant in 2016, who indicated that due to the high level of discomfort and deterioration she would need to have the surgery as soon as possible. It was later scheduled to take place in November 2019, but Betty had to postpone the surgery at that time due do a bad case of influenza. It was rescheduled for March 2020, but was cancelled by the public hospital due to the suspension of all elective surgeries at that time due to the covid pandemic. By January 2022, and despite numerous enquiries made by Betty, the hip replacement operation had still not been rescheduled. She was in a considerable amount of discomfort and could not walk for more than 50 meters at a time, even with a walking stick. She found that both her discomfort and her lack of mobility had taken a toll on her mental health as well. She became quite reclusive and her appetite waned. Her daughter advised her to travel to Germany to a private hospital to have the hip replacement operation there, which she did at the start of February 2022. The German hospital charged Betty €21,500 for the operation and 7 nights residence in a semi-private room in the hospital. She also paid €1,200 for an additional five nights in a local hotel in Germany as she was advised not to travel back to Ireland for an additional week after her hospital discharge. The return flights to Germany cost €225. With subsistence and other travel costs €1,100 per month and her life savings amount to €21,000. Due to the pressure that this operation has put on her financial situation, Betty has approached you for advice on whether there is any provision in EU law that would enable her to recoup some of this money from the Irish Health Service Executive (HSE). Under the (fictional) HSE Treatment (EU) Scheme, reimbursement for medical procedures will only be available for public patients where they have first had an application approved under the scheme (at least 9 months prior to travel for the operation) and up to a maximum payment of €9,000. Betty made no application to the HSE for this type of support before the operation because she was unaware of the scheme's existence. The standard cost across the EU for a hip operation replacement and hospital recovery is €22,000. Referring to relevant CJEU case-law and EU legislation throughout, advise Betty on whether she may still be able to make a claim for reimbursement for her operation from the HSE and on any potential difficulties that she may encounter in making this claim.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core…
questions.text:
Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: Cases T-778/16, Ireland v Commission, and T-892/16, Apple Sales International and Apple Operations Europe V Commission ECLI:EU:T:2020:338; Case 120/78, Rewe-Zentral AG v Bundermonopolverwalting fur Branntwein (Cassis de Dijon) [1979] ECR 649; Case C-341/05, Laval un Partneri Ltd v Svenska Byggnadsarbetareforbundet ea 
[2007] ECR I-11767; Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs-og Selskabsstyrelsen [1999] ECR 1459; Cases 41, 44 and 45/69, ACF Chemiefarma NV v Commission (Quinine cartel) 
[1970] ECR 661; Case C-236/09, Association Belge des Consommateurs Test-Achats ASBL and 
others v Conseil des ministres[2011]ECRI-773; Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the the Garda Siochana v 
Workplace Relations Commission ECLI:EU:C:2018:979; Case 174/82, Officier van Justitie v Sandoz BV [1983] ECR 2445.

questions.description: With reference to relevant case-law and Commission decisions, analyse extent to which Article 106 TFEU provides for an e…
questions.text: With reference to relevant case-law and Commission decisions, analyse extent to which Article 106 TFEU provides for an exception to the competition law, particularly Articles 101 and 102 TFEU, and the scope and such exception as regards public undertakings, undertakings to which have granted special or exclusive rights and undertakings entrusted by with the operation of services of general economic interest.

questions.description: The European Union and all of its Member States became Members of the (fictional) International Organisation for the Pro…
questions.text: The European Union and all of its Member States became Members of the (fictional) International Organisation for the Protection of Animal Welfare (IOPAW) when they signed the (fictional) International Agreement on Animal Rights (IAAR) in 2005. Article 10 of the IAAR provides that Members of the IOPAW agree to be bound by the terms of the Convention. Article 11 of the IAAR provides that Members must refrain from entering into trade agreements for animal products with States that breach the provisions of the IAAR or where the other State is not a Member of the IAAR and has not signed up to IOPAW obligations. Article 12 of the IAAR establishes a Standing Panel of five members who can investigate claims of breaches of the terms of the IAAR. If the Panel finds that a State has breached any of the provisions of the IAAR then they can issue fines of up to USD$10million. If the fine is not paid within two months, or if the offending State continues to breach its IOPAW or IAAR obligations then they can be served with six months’ notice of termination of their IOPAW membership. They would then cease to be able to enter into agreements to trade in all animal products with all continuing IOPAW Members. There are currently 187 IOPAW Member States, including the United States of America, China, Japan, Canada, the UK and Australia. The European Union signed a (fictional) trade agreement with the (fictional) South American country Costa Lotta in 2019. Under the terms of this Agreement, the EU allows 12 million pairs of leather shoes manufactured in Costa Lotta to enter the Union without any tariffs every year until 2030, when the Agreement with Costa Lotta will be renegotiated. The Agreement with Costa Lotta was ratified and entered into EU Law under EU Council Decision No 1718/19. The Italian Shoemakers Association (ISA) has made a complaint to the European Commission that EU Council Decision No 1718/19 should never have been introduced into EU law because they claim that it is incompatible with Italy’s and the EU’s obligations under the IOPAW and the IAAR. This claim is based on the fact that animal welfare standards applicable to the cattle used to make the leather for shoes in Costa Lotta are far beneath those expected by the terms of the IAAR. For example, branding, tethering and the use of crates for cattle are all prohibited by the Annex to the IAAR, but these practices are widespread in Costa Lotta. Costa Lotta used to be a Member State of the IOPAW but this was terminated by the IOPAW in 2017 due its failure to pay a USD$2.7million fine imposed by the IOPAW for breaching the IAAR. The Commission is bringing a case to the CJEU against the Council seeking the annulment of Council Decision 1718/19 because of its alleged incompatibility with the EU’s international legal obligations. Referring to relevant CJEU case law throughout your answer, make an assessment of whether an International Agreement, in this case the IAAR, may produce the type of legal effect in the EU that leaves Decision 1718/19 open to a potentially successful action for annulment like this.

questions.description: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has chan…
questions.text: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has changed within the EJ legal order since its foundation in the 1950s and how the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights has, since it became legally binding, now become the main basis upon which the CJEU ensures that human rights are observed in the Union legal order.

questions.description: The EU has introduced (fictional) Directive 2020/100 on technical specifications for electric vehicles, including electr…
questions.text: The EU has introduced (fictional) Directive 2020/100 on technical specifications for electric vehicles, including electric scooters. Article 2(4) of the Directive states that for any electric scooter to be marketable in the EU it must have a maximum motor size of 250 watts. Member States are permitted, under the terms of Article 6 of Directive 2020/100, to inspect electric scooters being imported into their territory from any other Member State or third country to ensure that they meet this maximum motor size requirement. Customs Officers at Irish ports and airports have been rigorously inspecting all imported electric scooters coming into the country since a spate of accidents involving powerful electric scooters since the end of 2020. Under the transposing Irish legislation, the (fictional) Electric Vehicles (Safety and Inspection) Regulations 2021, importers can be charged a fee of €25 per imported scooter to cover the costs of these inspections. It is estimated that it costs around €250 to €300 to inspect a batch of 12 electric scooters. A Belgian company, Staden Scooters, is the largest manufacturer of electric scooters in the EU. They currently supply around 55% of the Irish market for these products. All of the scooters that they supply to Ireland are manufactured with a regulation motor of below 250 watts, but they are still subjected to the import inspections and they are charged the full price for this. Staden Scooters have complained about this to the Belgian Government, who in turn have made a complaint about the Irish inspections and charges to the EU Commission. Referring to relevant CJEU case law throughout your answer, advise the EU Commission on the compatibility of the Irish inspections and charges with EU rules on the free movement of goods.

questions.description: Paolo is an Italian national who is married to an Irish national, Pauline. The couple left Italy   in 2016 to move to Ir…
questions.text:
Paolo is an Italian national who is married to an Irish national, Pauline. The couple left Italy   in 2016 to move to Ireland as Pauline was offered a job in Dublin. Paolo has never worked in Ireland. They have two children, Paul and Paula, who were both born in Italy in 2013 and who also resided in Italy until 2016. The children are both Italian/lrish dual nationals. Shortly after the family moved to Ireland, Paolo and Pauline separated. They divorced in 2020. Paolo, who still resides with Pauline, Paul and Paula in Ireland to be near his children as they grow up, has become almost deaf in his left ear and his hearing is also deteriorating in his right ear. The condition is inoperable, and Paolo was informed by his audiologist in March 2021 that the only solution to help his worsening hearing is the use of hearing aids. Paolo, who is still not working, cannot afford to pay for the hearing aids, which in his case will cost at least €1 ,900, so he has now applied to get a financial grant towards this cost under the Treatment Benefit Scheme, which is run in Ireland by the Department of Social Protection. The grant, however, is only available to insured workers, the self-employed and retired people who have the required number of social insurance (PRSI) contributions. Under the Scheme, the Department will pay a grant once every four years of up to €1 ,000 towards a pair of hearing aids if required. While Paolo has not worked in Ireland, a dependant spouse, civil partner or cohabitant can still qualify for treatment benefit on the spouse’s Social Insurance record. Pauline has made sufficient Social Insurance contributions since 2016. Paolo has applied to the Department of Social Protection to become affiliated to the Treatment Benefit Scheme. This request was refused in April 2021 on the ground that he was neither employed nor self-employed nor retired in Ireland, and he was no longer the spouse of a worker who qualified for the Treatment Benefit Scheme. Paolo appealed this decision to the Department of Social Protection. The appeal was dismissed in June 2021. Paolo has since been engaged in illegal activity in a bid to fund the cost of his new hearing aids. He was caught driving off a car ferry in Dublin Port in June 2021 with a consignment of 20,000 counterfeit cigarettes, which were destined for the Irish black market. He also resisted arrest at the time and threatened to cough on one of the Dublin Harbour Police officers, claiming that he had tested positive for Covid-19 Coronavirus, and he also punched a member of An Garda Síochána who had arrived to assist. Paolo received a suspended sentence of two years’ imprisonment when he was before the Circuit Court last month, as this was just his second offence — he was also convicted of shoplifting when he was on holidays in Ireland in 2012, for which he was fined €300. However, on this occasion, the 
Minister for Justice has also ordered Paolo’s deportation from Ireland as part of a concerted 
Government campaign against counterfeit cigarette smuggling. Paolo now wants to appeal: (i) the deportation order made against him; and (ii) the refusal to accept him on to the Treatment Benefit Scheme. He claims that as an EU citizen, with children residing in Ireland, and having been married to a worker whom through he should be qualified for the Treatment Benefit Scheme, that he is entitled to remain in Ireland and to receive the hearing aid grant. Referring to relevant CJEU case law and EU legislation throughout, advise Paolo about whether EU law on free movement and citizenship may help his appeals against both the deportation order and the refusal to include him in the Treatment Benefit Scheme.

questions.description: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with rega…
questions.text: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with regard to certain Union acts; and (ii) judicial attempts that have been made to reform the test for individual concern in particular, analyse critically whether it has become any easier in 2021 for non-privileged applicants to seek judicial review of EU acts than it was when Advocate General Jacobs delivered his opinion on Case C-50/00, UPA v Council [2002] ECR 1-6677. Your answer should include an analysis of the CJEU case-law interpreting Article 263(4) TFEU.

questions.description: Explain, by reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU, the concepts of “agreement”, “decision” and “concerted practice”…
questions.text: Explain, by reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU, the concepts of “agreement”, “decision” and “concerted practice” in Article 101 (1) TFEU. In addition, explain separately, by reference to relevant case-law, how a “dominant position” can be held by “one or more undertaking” under Article 102 TFEU.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU; and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: Case C-55/94, Reinhard Gebhard v Consiglio dell'Ordine degli Avvocati e Procurator/ di Milano [1995] ECR 1-4165; Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Síochána v Workplace Relations Commission ECLI:EU:C:2018:979; Case 96/80, Jenkins v Kingsgate (Clothing Productions) Ltd. [1981] ECR 911; Case C-91/92, Paola Faccini Dori v Recreb Srl. [1994] ECR I-3325 Case C-341/05, Laval un Patneri Ltd v Svenska Byggnadsarbetareförbundet ea [2007] ECR I-11767; Case C-44/93, Namur — Les Assurances du Crédit SA v Office National du Ducroire and Belgian State [1994] ECR 1-3829; Case C-413/06, Bertelsmann AG and Sony Corporation of America v Impala [2008] ECR I-4951; Cases C-46/93 and C-48/93, Brasserie du Pécheur SA v Bundesrepublik Deutschland and The Queen v Secretary of State for Transport, ex pane: Factortame Ltd and others [1996] ECR I-1029,

questions.description: In 2019, the Irish Department of Health undertook a number of (fictional) studies into the safety of DIY and home improv…
questions.text: In 2019, the Irish Department of Health undertook a number of (fictional) studies into the safety of DIY and home improvement substances. Two of these studies identified that the inhalation of the substance ‘B-xylene212’ may contribute to causing cancer. The study was not, however, definitive. It also concluded that further research into the risks associated with the use of B-xylene212 would have to carried out. B-xylene212 is used by many paint manufacturers as a thinning substance for their products. In March 2020, the Irish Minister for Health signed the (fictional) Decorative Paints (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2020, Regulation 3(2)(k) of which prohibits the use of Bxylene212 in paints used for domestic decoration. Regulation 6(4) further provides that any of the prohibitions or restrictions set out in these regulations can be retracted, or strengthened, if new, and reliable scientific studies suggest that any of the substances covered by the 2020 Irish Regulations, including B-xylene212, are, or are not, in fact toxic or dangerous in any way. In February 2021, the International Association for Research and Investigation into Cancer (IARIC) and the European Environment Agency both published findings stating that there is currently insufficient information to determine whether or not B-xylene212 causes cancer. They both declared it a ‘low risk’ substance. Other ‘low risk’ products used to manufacture paints, such as C-Glyc0155, were not included in the Decorative Paints (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2020, or in any other similar Irish regulations. Pforzheim Paints are a German company who have manufactured paints and paint products containing B-xylene212 since 2015. They have been exporting nearly 40% of their entire output of products to Ireland since 2016. The use of B-xylene212 in the production of paints for domestic use is permitted in Germany. However, products which contain B-xylene212 and are sold in Germany must state clearly and conspicuously on the front of the tin of paint that this substance has been used in manufacture and it may be toxic. All Pforzheim Paints containing Bexylene212 also comply with this requirement. Pforzheim Paints have had to withdraw all of their products from the Irish market after the introduction of the Decorative Paints (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2020 into Irish law. Pforzheim Paints have lodged a complaint with the EU Commission about the Irish restrictions on the sale of products containing B-xylene212. Pforzheim Paints are considering using C-Glyc0155 as a thinning substance for their products instead, but the cost of C-Glyc0155 is three times as much as B-xylene212. Advise the EU Commission on the compatibility of the Decorative Paints (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2020 with EU law, and particularly Article 34 TFEU, referring to relevant CJEU case law throughout your answer.

questions.description: Outline by reference to both relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case law of the Union judicature the …
questions.text: Outline by reference to both relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case law of the Union judicature the respective roles of each of:- the European Parliament; the Commission; and the Council, in establishing new or amending EU legislative, delegated and implementing acts. Assess also whether the changes made to legislative procedures by the amendments introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon have improved, with effect from 1 December 2009, the democratic legitimacy of decision-making within the European Union.

questions.description: ANSWER PART (A) OR PART (B). DO NOT ANSWER BOTH PARTS PART (A) By reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature…
questions.text:
ANSWER PART (A) OR PART (B). DO NOT ANSWER BOTH PARTS PART (A) By reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature concerning Article 102 TFEU, analyse the extent to which a dominant undertaking may take steps to protect its own commercial position in the market on which it is dominant, or on a related market, if its position is threatened by a rival and/or new entrant. Could such steps or protective behaviour be acceptable if the effect is specifically to strengthen or extend the undertaking's dominant market position? OR 
 
PART (B) The 2014 EU Commission White Paper entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' (COM (2014) 449) makes it clear that, in its opinion: "[...] merger control at the EU level was strengthened by the 2004 Merger Regulation reforms, particularly the introduction of the SIEG test". The Commission also opines that: "[m]ore immediately, however, there are two main ways to improve the Merger Regulation through more limited amendments. First, the Commission considers bringing acquisitions of non-controlling minority shareholdings into the scope of EU merger control. Second, there is room to further streamline case referrals in the light of the Commission's experience with the 2004 reform". How does this review of merger policy affect the way in which EU law in this area has developed since the introduction of the original Merger Control Regulation in 1989 (Regulation 4064/89) and subsequent amendments, including Regulation 139/2004?

questions.description: Article 6 of (the fictional) Directive 2015/1210/EU on the use of mobile phone technology in cars and other motor vehicl…
questions.text: Article 6 of (the fictional) Directive 2015/1210/EU on the use of mobile phone technology in cars and other motor vehicles provides that: "[a]ll cars or motor vehicles, whether used or new, sold by any undertaking or other operator after the implementation of this Directive must include, as a minimum safety requirement [...] a 'hands-free', 'bluetooth' or similar capability for using the device in that private car or vehicle which provides the person(s) in control of that private car or vehicle with the possibility of orally communicating through their mobile device without having to hold it in their hand." Article 29 of the same Directive further provides that: "[m]ember States shall bring into force the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with this Directive including as regards compliance by undertakings and other operators concerned by 16 June 2017." Sonia was knocked down by a car, which was being driven by Graham, in January 2018, while she was out walking her dog. The driver of the car, Graham, mounted the footpath while holding and dialing a number on his mobile phone at the time of his collision with Sonia. Sonia suffered bad injuries to her neck and spine in the accident. She has had to give up her job as a physiotherapist as a result and she is finding it difficult to find alternative suitable employment. She is also undergoing a long and expensive course of physical rehabilitation. She was awarded €9,000 compensation, paid by Graham's motor insurance company, but she insists that it only partially covers the losses that she has and continues to suffer. Graham has been convicted under the Road Traffic Acts of causing harm by dangerous driving and, consequently, was fined €2,000. He was also disqualified from driving for 2 years. He subsequently, and consequently, lost his job as a double- glazing window salesman. Graham maintains that he would have used a 'hands-free' function on his phone had his car been fitted with the equipment to do so. The car had not been fitted with any such device or capability. He bought the car, which was second-hand and four years old, in October 2017 from Reville's Autos Limited, which is based in Enniscorthy, County Wexford. Directive 2015/1210/EU was not transposed into Irish Law until the introduction of the (fictional) Dangerous Driving (Use of Mobile Phones in Cars) (Amendment) Regulations in May 2018. Sonia seeks your advice on whether she can bring a claim for compensation against either (i) Reville's Autos Limited; or (ii) the State for the non-application and/or non-implementation of the 2015 Directive. Refer particularly to relevant EU law principles and case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer.

questions.description: As far back as 1994, Charlotte, an Austrian national, suffered excruciating pain in her left elbow, caused by rheumatoid…
questions.text: As far back as 1994, Charlotte, an Austrian national, suffered excruciating pain in her left elbow, caused by rheumatoid arthritis. The pain got progressively worse. By 2000, Charlotte had to give up her beloved art classes as her elbow was too sore. By 2007 she had to retire from her work as a part-time bartender for the same reason. She did undertake a programme of physiotherapy, which was provided free-of- charge by the Austrian national health service, from 2004-2012, but it was ineffective in alleviating her elbow pain.
