In March 2017, Charlotte contacted a doctor in Deggendorf, Germany, who specialises in arthritis of the elbow, to arrange an appointment to examine her the following month, on April 12th 2017. This examination confirmed advanced arthritis of the elbow causing a high-level of suffering in Charlotte. Alarmed by the advanced nature of the arthritis, the doctor advised Charlotte that she should stay in Deggendorf for the next 3 nights so that she could arrange for surgery to be carried out on Charlotte's elbow. By April 14th, 2017, the arthritis has worsened to the extent that the doctor was of the opinion that if she did not operate immediately, then Charlotte would have to get her elbow joint fused and would effectively lose the use of her left arm forever. Charlotte was operated on successfully on the morning of April 15th 2017. When Charlotte returned to her home in Linz, Austria, a week later, on April 22nd 2017, she applied to her local health board (BHL) for reimbursement of the costs of her treatment and accommodation in Deggendorf - a total cost of €11,270. This application was rejected on the ground that the operation constituted scheduled treatment for which Charlotte had not obtained prior authorisation from BHL. The BHL reimbursement scheme required prior authorisation for treatment received in another EU Member State in all circumstances. In January 2018, Charlotte brought an action against the decision rejecting her application for reimbursement before the District Court in Linz. That Court has asked the CJEU, among other questions, whether the principle of the freedom to provide services contained in Article 56 TFEU precludes a rule of a Member State which excludes, in all cases, reimbursement for healthcare services provided in another Member State without prior authorisation, regardless of the health of the patient and the apparent urgency of the health care in question. Referring to the case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, prepare the Advocate General's Opinion on Charlotte's case - focusing on the question of the compatibility of the BHL scheme with Article 56 TFEU.

questions.description: Jakub, who is 29 years old, moved to Ireland from Poland in October 2017. He is a Polish national. Jakub did not secure …
questions.text: Jakub, who is 29 years old, moved to Ireland from Poland in October 2017. He is a Polish national. Jakub did not secure any employment in Ireland for nearly a year after his arrival there. Also, he did not arrive in Ireland with much money, so he sought help from his local social welfare office in Arklow, but he was refused the full rate of jobseeker's allowance because he had not worked in Ireland for at least 13 weeks previously. Instead he was paid half the rate (€101.50) that Irish nationals who had not worked for 13 weeks previously were paid (€203). He had to take out a bank loan in December 2017 to assist with his living costs, a loan which he is still repaying in November 2020. After many applications, Jakub was eventually offered, and accepted, a full-time job as a tutor in Redcross Technical College, teaching electricians' apprentices. He has worked there ever since his appointment in December 2018. Jakub previously competed internationally as a weightlifter, representing Poland at two European Championship finals (where he finished fourth and sixth respectively) and winning a bronze medal at the Olympic Games in Rio in 2016. Mostly due to work commitments, he has not competed at weightlifting since he moved to Ireland in 2017, but he is keen to take it up again, with a view to representing Poland at the Olympics in Tokyo in 2021. He has heard about a grant scheme, where the Irish Government's Department of Transport, Tourism .and Sport pays high-performance athletes an allowance, enabling them to switch from full-time work to part-time' work to give them more time to devote to their training. Jakub has applied for this grant this year, but he has been refused on the basis that it is only payable to sportspeople who are likely to represent Ireland. He is incensed! Jakub has approached you, as his solicitor, for advice on: (i) the payment to him of only a half-rate, rather than the full-rate, of jobseeker's allowance by the local office of the Department of Employment Affairs and Social Protection from October 2017 to December 2018; and (ii) the refusal to consider his application for a high- performance sports grant by the Department of Transport, Tourism and Sport in 2020. Refer to relevant case-law of the Union Judicature and relevant EU legislation throughout your answer.

questions.description: In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLl:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that: [...] it should be borne in mind t…
questions.text: In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLl:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that: [...] it should be borne in mind that the fundamental rights guaranteed by the Charter are applicable in all situations governed by EU law and that they must, therefore, be complied with inter alia where national legislation falls within the scope of EU law. [para. 63] In this Judgment, the CJEU was addressing issues related to Article 17 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union on the right to property. With reference to the case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, assess how the CJEU has interpreted the Charter of Fundamental Rights to be used to challenge a wide range of EU and national legislative measures.

questions.description: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the Union judicature, setting out in each case-note: …
questions.text: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the Union judicature, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU and/or the General Court, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU and/or the General Court in its judgment: Case C-453/00, Kühne and Heitz v Produktschap voor Pluimvee en Eieren [2004] ECR 1-837; Case 112/84, Humblot v Directeur des Services Fiscaux [1985] ECR 1367; Cases T-778/16, Ireland v Commission, and T-892/16, Apple Sales International and Apple Operations Europe v Commission ECLl:EU:T:2020:338; Case 46/76, Bauhuis v Netherlands [1977] ECR 5; Case C-407/98, Abrahamsson v Fogelqvist [2000] ECR 1-5539; Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Síochana v Workplace Relations Commission ECLl:EU:C:2018:979; Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs- og Selskabsstyrelsen [1999] ECR 1- 1459; Case C-370/2012, Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012] ECLI 756.

questions.description: The Department of Health has sought to introduce a number of (fictional) measures in recent years in an attempt to reduc…
questions.text: The Department of Health has sought to introduce a number of (fictional) measures in recent years in an attempt to reduce sugar consumption in Ireland. In 2015 the Sale of High-Sugar Confectionary Regulations banned the use of vending machines in schools, when used to sell chocolate bars and sweets. Following a complaint made by vending machine suppliers based in Germany and Spain, and a chocolate manufacturer based in Denmark, the EU Commission informed the Department that this ban should be lifted or infringement proceedings could be started. The 2015 Regulations were consequently amended and a Code of Conduct on the use of vending machines in schools was introduced instead. In 2017 the Sale of Goods (Advertising) Regulations were amended to include a prohibition on the advertising of high-sugar products in cinemas before 6pm. Again, there were complaints from several EU Member States and this restriction had to be lifted. (Fictional) Figures released by the World Health Organisation in January 2017 show that Ireland has the fastest-rising rates of overweightness in the European Union. It also showed that the Irish population are the second biggest consumers of added sugar in Europe. Public outcry ensued and, as a result, the Department of Health issued a (fictional) press statement in February 2020 indicating that they are working on a Bill, which will include a provision setting a minimum price for soft drinks which contain more than 10g of added sugar per 100ml. The European Non-Alcoholic Beverages Association (ENBA), which represents the interests of soft drink manufacturers and retailers, have approached you about their concerns over this provision of the, yet to be published, Public Health (Sugar Consumption) Bill 2021. Of particular concern to them is the fact that the CJEU, in paragraph 49 of its Judgment in Case C-333/14, Scotch Whisky Association, stated that: "[i]t is for the referring court, which alone has available to it all the matters of fact and law pertaining to the circumstances ... to determine whether a [less trade-restricting measure could be] capable of protecting human life and health as effectively as [minimum pricing]". The European Non-Alcoholic Beverages Association fear that this will make it easier for the State to justify a national trade-restricting measure, which appears to be contrary to Article 34 TFEU, on the basis that it is necessary for the protection of human health, as potentially permitted by Article 36 TFEU. Advise the ENBA about this, highlighting the history of the case-law of the CJEU on Article 34 TFEU, the possible exceptions to its application, and the relevance of the Judgment in Scotch Whisky to the application of the Department of Health's proposal on minimum pricing.

questions.description: How has the status of human rights within the EU legal order changed since the foundation of the EEC in the 1950s? Refer…
questions.text: How has the status of human rights within the EU legal order changed since the foundation of the EEC in the 1950s? Refer to relevant case-law of the Union judicature, Treaty developments and EU legislative initiatives that have played a role in bringing about this level of change, throughout your answer.

questions.description: ANSWER PART (A) OR PART (B). DO NOT ANSWER BOTH PARTS PART (A) Article 5 of (the fictional) Directive 2016/456/EU on the…
questions.text:
ANSWER PART (A) OR PART (B). DO NOT ANSWER BOTH PARTS PART (A) Article 5 of (the fictional) Directive 2016/456/EU on the approximation of maximum permitted noise-levels at outdoor music concerts provides that: "[a]ll licence-holders operating outdoor music concerts must ensure that those working at these events are subjected to a maximum noise level of 116 decibels for a maximum continuous period of four hours." Various methods are set out in the Directive for measuring the noise level at these events. There are also a series of definitions set out, which clearly classify security staff as being amongst those who are "working at these events" and are therefore covered by the protection set out in the Directive. Article 12 of the same Directive further provides that: " [m]ember States shall bring into force the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with this Directive including as regards compliance by undertakings and other operators concerned by 31 March 2018." Prior to the introduction of Directive2016/456/EU, maximum permitted noise levels for concerts in Ireland were set out in the (fictional) Environmental Noise Act 1994. This 1994 Act allowed for exposure to a maximum noise level of 125 decibels for a continuous period of up to six hours. Both the 2016 EU Directive, and the 1994 Act, oblige the organisers of concerts to maintain records of the noise levels recorded during these events for five years. Brian was employed on a part-time basis as a security guard by Cool Concerts Ltd. between January 2017 and October 2019. He worked at these events for an average of four days per month during this period. He was regularly exposed to noise levels which exceeded 116 decibels. At one heavy metal festival at which Brian was working, held in Limerick on 3 August 2019, the noise level was recorded as having reached 124 decibels. Brian was exposed to this high noise volume for up to five hours on that day. He has been suffering from tinnitus and ear nerve damage since this date and he is no longer able to work for Cool Concerts Ltd. as a security guard due to the almost constant ringing noise in his ears and partial loss of hearing. Directive 2016/456/EU was not transposed into Irish Law until the introduction of the (fictional) Environmental Noise (Maximum Permitted Levels at Outdoor Events) Regulations in May 2020. The 2020 Irish Regulations set out a range of fines that may be imposed, up to a maximum level of €2,000 for the most serious breaches. Brian now seeks your advice on whether he can bring a claim for compensation against either: (i) Cool Concerts Ltd.; or (ii) the State, for the non-application and/or non implementation of the 2016 Directive. Your advice should focus on whether or not, by reference to relevant EU law principles as developed in pertinent case-law of the Union judicature, Brian was protected by the provisions of Directive 2016/456/EU on 3 August 2019 and, if so, whether any remedies may be available to him under EU law against Cool Concerts Ltd and/or the State. 
 
OR  
 
PART (B) The CJEU in Case C-603/2010, Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of procedural autonomy available for Member States in deciding what remedies should be available at national level for breaches of European Union law when it stated, in paragraph 23 of the judgment, that: "[...] in accordance with settled case-law of the Court, in the absence of relevant European Union rules, the detailed procedural rules designed to ensure the protection of the rights which individuals acquire under European Union law are a matter for the domestic legal order of each Member State, in accordance with the principle of the procedural autonomy of the Member States, provided that they are not less favourable than those governing similar domestic situations and that they do not render impossible in practice or excessively difficult the exercise of rights conferred by the European Union legal order." Have the above-referenced principles of 'equivalence' and 'practical possibility' been developed in relevant case-law by the Union judicature to provide clear guidance for national courts on how they should deal with breaches of EU law?

questions.description: Naas Natural Foods Ltd. has been supplying organic food shops all over Ireland with self-service 'zero-waste' food dispe…
questions.text: Naas Natural Foods Ltd. has been supplying organic food shops all over Ireland with self-service 'zero-waste' food dispensers and products since 2016. The most popular foodstuffs that it supplies to these shops include toasted hazelnuts, dried prunes and dried figs. The idea behind its business is that customers can go to organic food shops with their own glass jars and fill these from the food dispensers, in order to cut down on single-use packaging waste. It has been very successful in this regard and currently has a turnover of more than €3 million per annum, employing 26 full-time staff. Due to the Covid-19 virus pandemic, the Irish Government on the advice of the National Pandemic Prevention Team (NPPT) (fictional), has temporarily prohibited the use of the type of food dispensers used by Naas Natural Foods ltd. as a measure designed to help curtail the spread of the virus. The NPPT claims that these dispensers could add to the spread of this virus as many people might touch the same dispenser with unsanitised hands. The Irish Government has stated that the temporary ban on the food dispensers will last at least until May 2021. Faros Figs, a Greek company, has been supplying all the figs that it produces each year since 2017 to Naas Natural Foods Ltd. Naas Natural Foods Ltd. accepts that it could switch from the supply of dispensed food to pre-packaged food, but it insists that this would go against the very reason that they set up in the first place - to offer an alternative, environmentally-friendly way of shopping for items usually sold in single-use packaging. Faros Figs and Naas Natural Foods Ltd. have been talking to each other about their predicament and believe that the decision of the Irish Government effectively to suspend their way of doing business may be contrary to some aspect of EU law, possibly Article 34 TFEU regarding measures equivalent to quantitative restrictions on imports. Naas Natural Foods Ltd. insists that there is no evidence in existence to show that its dispensers have been responsible for the spread of the virus and points that it has been regularly and meticulously sanitising the dispensers. Referring throughout your answer to relevant CJEU case-law on the application of Articles 34-36 TFEU, advise Faros Figs and Naas Natural Foods Ltd. as to whether the Irish Government's measure may be incompatible with EU law on the free movement of goods.

questions.description: Rita is a 28-year old Belgian national who moved to Ireland in March 2017. She had graduated from Ghent University with …
questions.text: Rita is a 28-year old Belgian national who moved to Ireland in March 2017. She had graduated from Ghent University with an Honours Degree in English and History in 2014. She worked as a kitchen porter in Killarney for 4 months from May 2017 onwards, leaving that job to work as a classroom assistant from September 2017 for nine months in a secondary school in Kildare. Rita really enjoyed her job as a classroom assistant and decided that she would study and train to become an English and History teacher. Rita enrolled on a teacher-training correspondence course, run by a private college in Dublin, from September 2018. It is a two-year academic qualification, after which students who have passed all of their exams are placed in teaching-practice positions in schools for a full year. After the successful completion of this practical element of the education and training, students graduate as recognised qualified secondary school teachers in Ireland. However, Rita has now encountered two problems. First, she has twice presented for her Irish language exam, which is a compulsory part of her training course, but she has failed this exam on both occasions. As a result, the private teacher-education college is not allowing her to proceed to the practical part of her training. She has excelled in all other parts of her course to date - it is only the Irish language exam that she has failed to pass. Second, while she waits for the outcome of an appeal of the decision to refuse to grant her access to the practical teacher training, Rita has applied for a job as a security guard at the Department of Foreign Affairs in Dublin. Rita, who speaks fluent French, Flemish (Dutch), German and English, and is an amateur Mixed Martial Arts fanatic, thought that she would be an ideal candidate for this temporary position. After what she thought was a very successful interview, Rita has now been informed that she will not be offered the job for two reasons - 1. Because she is not an Irish national - the Department of Foreign Affairs did not realise that she was Belgian until she was interviewed; and - 2. She has a criminal record in Belgium, as she was found guilty of possessing a small quantity of cannabis with intent to supply while she was a student at Ghent University. She was sentenced to 40 hours of community service for this in 2012. The Department of Foreign Affairs have passed this information on to the Department of Justice, which has now started proceedings to deport Rita from the State as it deems that her conviction for a drugs offence renders her continued presence in Ireland contrary to public policy. Advise Rita on whether she may have any grounds to challenge: - (i) the decision of the teacher-training college to refuse to grant her access to the practical part of her education and training; (ii) the reasons for the refusal of the Department of Foreign Affairs to employ her as a security guard; and (iii) the proceedings that have been initiated by the Department of Justice to deport Rita from Ireland. Refer to relevant EU Treaty provisions and legislation, as interpreted in relevant case-law of the CJEU, throughout in support of your advice.

questions.description: When Advocate General Jacobs stated, in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on Case C- 50/2000, UPA v Council that: "[ ...] an …
questions.text: When Advocate General Jacobs stated, in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on Case C- 50/2000, UPA v Council that: "[ ...] an individual should be regarded as individually concerned within the meaning of the fourth paragraph of Article 230 EC by a Community measure where, by reason of his particular circumstances, the measure has, or is liable to have, a substantial adverse effect on his interests" he was clearly articulating that there was a lack of effective judicial protection for non privileged applicants who sought a judicial review of acts of the European Union's institutions. Explain briefly the concerns that the Advocate General was addressing here. In addition make an assessment of whether these concerns have been adequately addressed by the changes that were made to the fourth paragraph of Article 230 EC by the Treaty of Lisbon and related case-law particularly that interpreting the said new fourth paragraph of what became and was renumbered as Article 263 TFEU (upon the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon on 1 December 2009).

questions.text: Outline by reference to both relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of the Union judicature the respective roles of each of: the European Parliament; the Commission; and the Council, in establishing new or amending EU legislative, delegated and implementing acts. Assess also whether the changes made to legislative procedures by the amendments introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon have improved, with effect from 1 December 2009, the democratic legitimacy of decision-making within the European Union.

questions.description: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core…
questions.text: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: Cases 56 & 58/64, Etablissements Consten SARL and Grundig- Verkaufs-GmbH v Commission [1966] ECR 299; Cases 6 & 7/73, Istituto Chemioterapico Italiano SpA and Commercial Solvents v Commission [1974] ECR 223; Case C-372/04, Watts v Bedford Primary Care Trust [2006] ECR 1- 4325; Case C-370/2012, Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012) ECLI 756; Case 340/89, Vlassopoulou v Ministerium für Justiz, Bundes und Europaangelegenreiten Baden-Württemberg [1991] ECR 2357; Case C-236/2009, Association Beige des Consommateurs Test- Achats and others v Council [2011) ECR 1- 773; Case 87/75, Bresciani v Amministrazione ltaliana de/le Finanze [1976] ECR 129;. Case 193/85, Cooperative Co-Frutta Sri v Amministrazione de/le Finanze de/lo Stato [1987] ECR 2085.

questions.description: The 2014 EU Commission White Paper entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' (COM (2014) 449) makes it clear t…
questions.text: The 2014 EU Commission White Paper entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' (COM (2014) 449) makes it clear that, in its opinion: "[...] merger control at the EU level was strengthened by the 2004 Merger Regulation reforms, particularly the introduction of the SIEC test". The Commission also opines that: "[m]ore immediately, however, there are two main ways to improve the Merger Regulation through more limited amendments. First, the Commission considers bringing acquisitions of non-controlling minority shareholdings into the scope of EU merger control. Second, there is room to further streamline case referrals in the light of the Commission's experience with the 2004 reform". How will this review of merger policy affect the way in which EU law in this area has developed since the introduction of the original Mergers Regulation in 1989 (Regulation 4064/89) and subsequent amendments, including Regulation 139/2004?

questions.description: Directive 2016/200/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council on the prudential supervision of credit institutions…
questions.text:
Directive 2016/200/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council on the prudential supervision of credit institutions (fictional) establishes a number of new requirements for EU Member States, designed to ensure that only approved moneylenders are allowed to provide financial credit to customers. Article 9(1) of Directive 2016/200/EU specifically provides that: "[...] the competent national authorities shall refuse authorisation to commence the 
activity of a credit institution where the initial capital is less that EUR 6 million". As an exception to this, Article 9(4) of the Directive further provides that: "[m]ember States may grant authorisation to credit institutions where the initial capital is less than that specified in paragraph 1, subject to the following conditions: the initial capital is no Jess than EUR 1 million; and the Member State notifies the Commission of their reasons for exercising this option". Article 13 of the Directive provides that the directors of credit institutions should not be paid any bonus in a year where the institution has failed to make a profit in excess of EUR 1 million. Article 60 of Directive 2016/200/EU states that it was to be implemented by 1 August 2017. The Bank of Ballintubber was granted authorisation to operate credit, and other, facilities by the Central Bank of Ireland on 22 September 2017, despite having an initial capital investment of only EUR 3 million. The EU Commission was not notified about this authorisation. The Supervision of Credit Institutions (Amendment) Regulations 2017, transposing Directive 2016/200/EU, were not introduced until 3 December 2017. In January 2020, following a series of poor lending decisions and the award of large bonuses to the directors of the Bank of Ballintubber, which never made any profits, has now gone into liquidation. Secure Safes ltd. are owed EUR 238,900 by the Bank of Ballintubber for the supply and installation of timelock safes during 2018. An initial assessment by the liquidator has shown that there is no chance that unsecured creditors like Secure Safes ltd. will be paid what they are owed. Secure Safes ltd. have had to make two staff redundant as a result of this non-payment and the future of this limited company is also now in jeopardy. Advise the liquidator of Secure Safes ltd., by reference to relevant CJEU case-law, about whether the company could rely on the provisions of Directive 2016/200/EU in separate actions that the liquidator is considering authorising it to bring before the Irish courts against each of: (i) the State; (ii) the Central Bank of Ireland; and (iii) the directors of the Bank of Ballintubber.

questions.description: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has chan…
questions.text: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has changed within the EU legal order since its foundation in the 1950s and how the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights has, since it became legally binding, now become the main basis on which the EU Courts ensure that human rights are observed in the Union legal order.

questions.description: In 2018, the Irish Department of Health undertook a number of (fictional) studies into the safety of DIY and home improv…
questions.text:
In 2018, the Irish Department of Health undertook a number of (fictional) studies into the safety of DIY and home improvement substances. Two of these studies identified that the inhalation of the substance 'B-xylene212' may contribute to causing cancer. The study was not, however, definitive. It also concluded that further research into the risks associated with the use of B-xylene212 would have to carried out. B-xylene212 is used by many paint manufacturers as a thinning substance for their products. In March 2019, the Irish Minister for Health signed the (fictional) Decorative Paints (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2019, Regulation 3(2)(k) of which prohibits the use of B-xylene212 in paints used for domestic decoration. Regulation 6(4) further provides that any of the prohibitions or restrictions set out in these regulations can be retracted, or strengthened, if new, and reliable scientific studies suggest that any of the substances covered by the 2019 Irish Regulations, including B-xylene212, are, or are not, in fact toxic or dangerous in any way. In February 2020, the International Association for Research and Investigation into Cancer (IARIC) and the European Environment Agency both published findings stating that there is currently insufficient information to determine whether or not B-xylene212 causes cancer. They both declared it a low to moderate risk substance. Other '.l,oyv to moderate' risk products used to manufacture paints, such as C-Glycol55, were not included in the Decorative Paints (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2019, or in any other similar Irish regulations. Pforzheim Paints are a German company who have manufactured paints and paint products containing B-xylene212 since 2014. They have been exporting nearly 40% of their entire output of products to Ireland since 2015. The use of B-xylene212 in the production of paints for domestic use is permitted in Germany. However, products which contain B- xylene212 and are sold in Germany must state clearly and conspicuously on the front of the tin of paint that this substance has been used in manufacture and it may be toxic. All Pforzheim Paints containing B-xylene212 also comply with this requirement. 
 
Pforzheim Paints have had to withdraw all of their products from the Irish market after the introduction of the Decorative Paints. (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2019 into Irish law. Pforzheim Paints have lodged a complaint with the EU Commission about the Irish restrictions on the sale of products containing B-xylene212. Pforzheim Paints are considering using C-Glycol55 as a thinning substance for their products instead, but the cost of C-Glycol55 is three times as much as B-xylene212. Advise the Commission, referring to relevant CJEU case law throughout your answer.

questions.description: With reference to relevant CJEU case-law and Treaty provisions, make an assessment of whether the Treaty on the Function…
questions.text: With reference to relevant CJEU case-law and Treaty provisions, make an assessment of whether the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union currently provides an adequate system of legal protection for individuals who seek judicial review of '...legislative acts [and] of acts of the [EU Institutions] intended to produce legal effects vis-a-vis third parties'.

questions.description: Maria is a Dutch national, currently employed as a shift worker in a banking computer centre in Breda, a city in the sou…
questions.text:
Maria is a Dutch national, currently employed as a shift worker in a banking computer centre in Breda, a city in the south of The Netherlands. She resides with her husband Pierre and their two children, Lucas (aged 15) and Ruben (aged 18), in the northern suburbs of Antwerp, just over the border in northern Belgium. The family moved from Breda to Antwerp just under a year ago. Lucas is in his penultimate year of secondary school, still attending the same school in Breda as he was when Maria and her family resided there. He commutes by organised school transport from his home in Antwerp every day. Neither Maria nor her husband Pierre can bring Lucas to school every day due to the shift hours Maria has to work, which are often at night, and given that Pierre voluntarily took early retirement just under a year ago from his job as a firefighter in Brussels, Belgium. A locally administered scheme in Breda bears the cost of school transport for students in the final two years of their secondary education. However, Maria has been informed that the local council will not cover these costs for Lucas as the obligation to organise and pay for this transport relates to students residing in Breda only. 
 
Ruben finished his secondary schooling at the same school in Breda last year and now attends art college in Ghent, Belgium. He has been charged the exact same enrolment fees as all other EU nationals on his course (€1,750 per annum) but his application to the public student support scheme for a loan of €5,500 to cover accommodation costs in Ghent has been refused on the basis that these financial supports are only available to people who: (i) have been made involuntarily redundant from work in the past twelve months; or (ii) are the children of a parent currently working in Belgium; or (iii) are the children of a parent who has been made involuntarily unemployed in Belgium in the past two years. Outraged by these two discrete Dutch and Belgian decisions, Maria comes to you for advice on whether the refusal to: (i) cover the school transport costs for Lucas; and (ii) provide Ruben with a student loan, constitutes unjustifiable indirect discrimination against migrant workers, as prohibited under EU law. Advise Maria, referring to relevant CJEU case-law throughout your answer.

questions.description: Explain, by reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted practice"…
questions.text: Explain, by reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted practice" in Article 101(1) TFEU. In addition, explain separately, by reference to relevant case-law, how a "dominant position" can be held by "one or more undertakings" under Article 102 TFEU.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU; and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: Case C-341/05, Laval un Partneri Ltd v Svenska Byggnadsarbetaref6rbundet ea (2007] ECR 1-11767; Case C-370/2012, Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General (2012] EGLI 756; Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs-og Selskabsstyrelsen (1999] ECR 1- 14 59; Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Siochana v Workplace Relations Commission ECLl:EU:C:2018:979; Case C-44/93, Namur - Les Assurances du Credit SA v Office National du Ducroire and Belgian State [1994] ECR 1-3829; Case C-413/06, Bertelsmann AG ·and Sony Corporation of America v Impala (2008] ECR 1-4951; Cases 21-24/72, International Fruit Company NV v Produktschap voor Groentenen Fruit[1972] ECR 1219; Case T-184/95, Dorsch Consult lngenieurgesellschaft mbH.Y, Council [1998] ECR 11-667.

questions.description: The Irish Government has recently introduced two (fictional) financial charges on wine, regardless of where it is produc…
questions.text: The Irish Government has recently introduced two (fictional) financial charges on wine, regardless of where it is produced. The first is an inspection charge, which is imposed on any wine for sale in Ireland to cover the costs of analysing samples to ensure that the grapes used in production were free of 'black rot' fungus disease at the time of production. These inspections have been ordered following the- publication of a recent study by the Organic Growers of Ireland, which has suggested that the presence of this fungus could possibly have some detrimental consequences for the health of humans that consume wine produced from infected grapes. Similar inspections have not been ordered in any other EU Member States. However, (fictional) Directive 99/123 does allow Member States to analyse samples of wine, where a health protection concern has been identified. The second charge is an additional €1.50 tax on all bottles of wine that are sold by retailers for more than €20 per bottle. This is purely a revenue-raising measure. The average retail price for a bottle of wine in Ireland is €12. There is no significant level of commercial wine production in Ireland. The European Commission has started an investigation into both the (i) the sampling charge; and (ii) the additional retail tax. Advise the Government, by reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case-law of the Union judicature, on the compatibility of both of these charges with Article 30 TFEU and Article 110 TFEU.

questions.description: Outline and discuss, referring where apposite to relevant Union judicature case law, the most significant changes that w…
questions.text: Outline and discuss, referring where apposite to relevant Union judicature case law, the most significant changes that were made by the Lisbon Treaty amendments to the internal organisation of the EU institutions and to their respective powers .

questions.description: The Court of Justice in Case C-603/2010, Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of procedural autonomy availabl…
questions.text: The Court of Justice in Case C-603/2010, Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of procedural autonomy available for Member States in deciding what remedies should be available at national level for breaches of European Union law when it stated in paragraph 23 of the Judgment that: "[. ..] in accordance with settled case-law of the Court, in the absence of relevant European Union rules, the detailed procedural rules designed to ensure the protection of the rights which individuals acquire under European Union law are a matter for the domestic legal order of each Member State, in accordance with the principle of the procedural autonomy of the Member States, provided that they are not less favourable than those governing similar domestic situations and that they do not render impossible in practice or excessively difficult the exercise of rights conferred by the European Union legal order." How have these principles of 'equivalence' and 'practical possibility' referred to above been developed by the Union judicature to provide clear guidance for national courts on how they should deal with breaches of EU law?

questions.description: Article 11(f) of Regulation (EU) 2019/6 of the European Parliament and of the Council on veterinary medicinal products […
questions.text: Article 11(f) of Regulation (EU) 2019/6 of the European Parliament and of the Council on veterinary medicinal products [2019) OJ L 4/43, provides that the labelling of the outer packaging of veterinary medicinal products should: 'in the case of homeopathic veterinary medicinal products include the statement "homeopathic veterinary medicinal product". ' 0Based in County Meath, ZooZoo Ltd. are the largest producer of homeopathic veterinary medicinal products in the European Union, accounting for nearly three- quarters of all homeopathic veterinary products sold across the entire EU in 2018. However, a series of articles have been published in national newspapers in several EU Member States over the past few months (fictional), suggesting that the use of homeopathic remedies for the treatment of ill animals is cruel due to a lack of evidence supporting claims that they help in the treatment of many ailments and that they are incapable of dulling pain that animals may be suffering. ZooZoo Ltd. are concerned that an obligation to clearly indicate on the label of their products that they are homeopathic may cause severe detriment to their business. ZooZoo Ltd. point out that there is much evidence to support their own claims about the ability of homeopathic veterinary medicines to treat illness in animals and they argue that their products are being unfairly targeted for this additional labelling obligation set out in Article 11(f) of Regulation 2019/6. Referring to relevant case law and legislation, advise ZooZoo Ltd. about whether they may be able to seek a review of the legality of this provision of Regulation 2019/6 before the Union judicature.

questions.description: Directive 2016/123/EU of the European parliament and of the Council on the prudential supervision of credit institutions…
questions.text:
Directive 2016/123/EU of the European parliament and of the Council on the prudential supervision of credit institutions (fictional) establishes a number of new requirements for Member States, designed to ensure that only approved moneylenders  are allowed  to provide  financial  credit  to customers.  Article  9(1)  of Directive 2016/123 specifically provides that: "[. ..] the competent authorities shall refuse authorisation to commence the activity of a credit institution where the initial capital is less that EUR 6 million". As an exception to this, Article 9(4) of the Directive further provides that: "[m]ember States may grant authorisation to credit institutions where the initial capital is less than that specified in paragraph 1, subject to the following conditions: the initial capital is no less than EUR 1 million; and the Member State notifies the Commission of their reasons for exercising this option". Article 13 of the Directive provides that the directors of credit institutions should not be paid any bonus in a year where the institution has failed to make a profit in excess of EUR 1 million. Article 60 of Directive 2016/123/EU states that it was to come into effect by 1 August 2016. The Bank of Baltinglass was granted authorisation to operate credit, and other, facilities by the Central Bank of Ireland on 22 September 2016, despite having an initial capital investment of only EUR 3 million. The EU Commission was never notified about this authorisation. The Supervision of Credit Institutions (Amendment) Regulations 2016, transposing Directive 2016/123/EU, were not introduced until 3 December 2016. 
 
In January 2019, following a series of poor lending decisions and the award of large bonuses to the directors of the Bank of Baltinglass, which never made any profits, it has now gone into liquidation. Secure Safes Ltd. are owed €155,750 by the Bank of Baltinglass for the supply and installation of timelock safes during 2018. An initial assessment by the liquidator has shown that there is no chance that unsecured creditors like Secure Safes Ltd. will be paid what they are owed. Secure Safes Ltd. have had to make two staff redundant as a result of this non-payment and the future of this limited company is also now in jeopardy.

questions.description: The Irish Government's Minister for Transport, Tourism and Sport published the Road Safety Car Restraint Systems Bill (f…
questions.text: The Irish Government's Minister for Transport, Tourism and Sport published the Road Safety Car Restraint Systems Bill (fictional) in June 2019. Section 7 of the Bill provides that all children under 150 ems in height or 36 kgs in weight must be seated in a 'double-lock; double-harness' child restraint when travelling in a car. Section 9 of the Bill provides that all child car restraints (child car seats) used in cars in Ireland must be fitted by a licensed installer. Section 10 of the Bill provides that licenses to install child car restraints can only be granted to installers who, amongst other things, have at least 8 full-time employees. MotoProtect Ltd., operating in Rush, County Dublin, have been manufacturing, selling and installing child car restraints into cars since 2004. However, they currently only employ 6 people. MotoProtect Ltd. do not currently manufacture any 'double- lock; double-harness' child restraints. They claim that to alter the manufacturing premises so that they would be capable of producing the 'double-lock; double- harness' restraints would cost them in excess of €780,000 which, given their small size, they simply can not afford to do. AutoEnfant are a car-seat manufacturing company based in Rennes, France. They currently export 45% of their child restraining car seats to Ireland. They do not currently produce any 'double-lock; double-harness' products. The Irish Minister for Transport, Tourism and Sport insists that the 'double-lock; double-harness' car seats are much safer for use in cars, provided they are installed by professional and competent installer. Both MotoProtect Ltd. and AutoEnfant have come to you for advise on whether there are potentially any issues concerning the compatibility of the recently published Road Safety Car Restraint Systems Bill 2019 with EU law - in particular that on the free movement of goods and the freedom to provide services. Advise MotoProtect Ltd. and AutoEnfant, referring to relevant CJEU case law and EU Treaty provisions and legislation.

questions.description: Referring to relevant case law of the CJEU and relevant EU Treaty provisions and legislation, critically analyse whether…
questions.text: Referring to relevant case law of the CJEU and relevant EU Treaty provisions and legislation, critically analyse whether the establishment of the legal concept of EU citizenship has provided a stronger basis for the rights of individuals to move to, work in, and reside in, EU Member States other than their own.

questions.text: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: Case C-280/00, Altmark Trans GmbH and Regierungsprasidium Magdeburg v Nahverkehrsgesellschaft Altmark GmbH [2003) ECR 1-7747; Case C-370/2012, Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012] ECLI 756; C-617/10, Aklagaren v Akerberg Fransson EU:C:2013:105; Case C-236/09, Association Beige des Consommateurs Test-Achats v Conseil des Ministries [2011) ECR 1-773; 378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Slochana v Workplace Relations Commission ECLI: EU:C:2018:979; Case C- ltaliana dCase 87/75, Bresiani Amministrazione Italiana delle Finanze [1976] ECR 129; Case 140/79, Chemial Farmaceutici v OAF SpA [1981] ECR 1; Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs- og Selskabsstyrelsen [1999] ECR 1 1459. Case 87/75, Bresciani v Amministrazione

questions.description: Answer Part (A) or (B). Do not attempt both parts. PART A Explain, by reference to relevant case-law, the concepts of "a…
questions.text:
Answer Part (A) or (B). Do not attempt both parts. PART A Explain, by reference to relevant case-law, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted practice" in Article 101(1) TFEU. Also explain, by reference to relevant case-law, how a "dominant position" can be held by "one or more undertakings" under Article 102 TFEU. OR 
 
PART B By reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature concerning Article 102 TFEU, analyse the extent to which a dominant undertaking may take steps to protect its own commercial position in the market on which it is dominant, or on a related market, if its position is threatened by a rival and/or new entrant. Could such steps or protective behaviour be acceptable if the effect is specifically to strengthen or extend the undertaking's dominant market position

questions.description: Outline the roles of the various European Union institutions in EU law-making and assess how these roles were changed by…
questions.text: Outline the roles of the various European Union institutions in EU law-making and assess how these roles were changed by the Lisbon Treaty amendments. Pay particular attention to the changes that have been made to the roles of: The European Parliament; The Commission; The Court of Justice of the European Union; and The Council. Discuss whether or not, and in what ways, these Lisbon Treaty changes have made the European Union a more democratic organisation, referencing, where appropriate, relevant post 1 December 2009 case-law.

questions.description: Article 3 of Directive 2010/123/EU (fictional) states that: "Member States shall ensure that the substances set out in A…
questions.text:
Article 3 of Directive 2010/123/EU (fictional) states that: "Member States shall ensure that the substances set out in Annex I are not included in any paint-stripper after the date laid down in Article 15 of this Directive." Annex I includes a reference to the substance 'dichloromethane'. Article 15 sets the date for implementation as 30 November 2011. Dichloromethane was banned from use in paint-stripper by Directive 2010/123/EU due to the serious risks that it presents to human health, including death in some circumstances. A range of scientific studies, including some undertaken by the EU Commission's own scientific committees and advisory bodies, have shown that it can cause cancer when inhaled on a regular or ongoing basis. These reports have demonstrated that the  inclusion of dichloromethane at any level above 50 parts per million is potentially dangerous for human health and it was therefore recommended that an outright prohibition on the use of this substance in paint-stripper and similar products should be introduced. The Minister for Health in Ireland introduced the Paint and Paint Products (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2012 (fictional), which came into effect on 31 March 2012. Regulation 4 of these 2012 Irish Regulations provides that: "Dichloromethan e shall not be permitted for inclusion in paint-stripper where the quantity present exceeds 100 parts per million."· Frank, who is employed by Brighter Homes Ltd. as a painter and decorator, has just been diagnosed with lung cancer, which he believes may have been caused by over- exposure to dichloromethane. His medical consultant also thinks that the inhalation of dichloromethane over the past decade or so is likely to have played a significant role in Frank's development of this disease. When Frank raises the issue with0Brighter Homes Ltd. they insist that all of the paint-stripping products that they have used since 2012 were in compliance with 
the Irish Regulations. They are able to clearly substantiate this claim. Frank's solicitor insists that Directive 2010/123/EU has direct effect and that either Frank's employers or the State must compensate him for this unlawful exposure to the substance dichloromethane . Advise Frank on whether he may be able to bring a claim against either (i) Brighter Homes Ltd. or (ii) the Department of the Minister for Health for a failure to comply with the provisions of Directive 2010/123/EU on dichloromethane use in paint- strippers. In so doing, discuss the nature of the claims that may be brought and refer to relevant CJEU case-law in particular.

questions.description: When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on Case C- 50/2000, UPA v Council, that: "[. ..] an …
questions.text: When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on Case C- 50/2000, UPA v Council, that: "[. ..] an individual should be regarded as individually concerned within the meaning of the fourth paragraph of Article 230 EC by a Community measure where, by reason of his particular circumstances, the measure has, or is liable to have, a· substantial adverse effect on his interests" He was clearly articulating that there was a lack of effective judicial protection for non privileged applicants who sought a judicial review of acts of the European Union's institutions. Explain briefly the concerns that the Advocate General was addressing here. Also, make an assessment of whether these concerns have been fully addressed by the changes that were made to Article 230 EC by the  Lisbon Treaty and now contained in Article 263 TFEU and/or any developments that may have come from the related case law of the Union judicature on this matter, and in particular the case-law interpreting the new post-Lisbon fourth paragraph to Article 263 TFEU.

questions.description: Bob the Baker Ltd. have been making and selling bread in Ireland since 2014. Their range of products includes the 'Tradi…
questions.text:
Bob the Baker Ltd. have been making and selling bread in Ireland since 2014. Their range of products includes the 'Traditional Sliced Pan', the 'Farmhouse Irish Soda Bread' and the 'Artisan Rye Bread'. Bob the Baker Ltd. also export around 25,000 loaves of their 'Farmhouse Irish Soda Bread' to Germany every week. All of their other sales are on the domestic Irish market. 
 
Bob the Baker Ltd. have just been informed by the Irish Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine that terms such as 'Traditional', 'Farmhouse' and 'Artisan' can no longer be used by any bread manufacturer who produces more than 15,000 loaves of bread per week and that they must therefore cease to use these terms for their products. These descriptive terms are reserved under Regulation 7 of the (fictional) Irish Bread Regulations 2015 for use by small-scale producers only. EU Regulation 1169/2011 on the provision of food information to consumers does not limit the use of these specific descriptive terms in any way. It does, however provide that descriptions used on food labelling should not be 'misleading' in any way. This term 'misleading\  as used in Regulation 1169/2011, is clarified as relating to: "the characteristics of the food and, in particular, as to its nature, identity, properties, composition, quantity, durability, country of origin or place of provenance, method of manufacture or production". 
 
Bob the Baker Ltd. want to challenge the validity of Regulation 7 of the Irish Bread Regulations 2015. The basis of its argument is that the prohibition imposed on the use of these descriptive terms for larger producers like it goes beyond what is set out in EU Regulation 1169/2011. It also contends that it is incompatible with Articles 34 and 35 TFEU on measures equivalent to quantitative restrictions on trade and that this is unjustifiable under Article 36 TFEU. You have been asked by Bob the Baker Ltd. for your opinion on what specific arguments could be made both in support of and against their claim that the Irish Bread Regulations 2015 are incompatible with EU law, and with Articles 34-36 TFEU in particular. 
 
Advise Bob the Baker Ltd., supporting your legal advice with reference to and discussion of relevant case law of the CJEU and of relevant EU legislation.

questions.description: Tara is a 37 year old actress from Dublin. In early December 2018 she auditioned for the lead role in a three-part telev…
questions.text:
Tara is a 37 year old actress from Dublin. In early December 2018 she auditioned for the lead role in a three-part television drama miniseries called 'Planespotting' that is set in Edinburgh, Scotland. The audition went very well as Tara is an excellent actress with a strong curriculum vitae who is very highly regarded in the sector and who can clearly imitate a very convincing Edinburgh accent. She was told by the casting director , Alastair, that she was " perfect for the role" and that they "would be in contact soon to make her a firm offer'. 
 
Tara did not hear anything from the film company, Ben Nevis Productions Ltd., by mid January 2019, so she decided to phone the casting director Alastair to see whether they had made a final decision on the role and when she could expect to hear definitively from the production company. At this stage Alastair told Tara that he was very sorry but that the Board of the Scottish Film and Performing Arts Council (SFPAC) (fictional), who are funding the production of the miniseries have insisted that all of the performers and crew must come from within a 30-mile radius of Edinburgh. The SFPAC are entirely State-funded. They are directed by a Board of seven members, four of whom are appointed by the Scottish Minister for Sport and Culture (fictional). 
 
Tara is looking for some advice on whether this decision to employ local actors only is compatible with European Union rules on the free movement of workers, as set out in Article 45 TFEU, any relevant EU secondary legislation and the relevant case law of the CJEU. Advise Tara on whether she can bring a claim that the refusal of Ben Nevis Productions Ltd. to employ her in their production of 'Planespotting' is incompatible with Article 45 TF.EU and related case law of the CJEU and EU secondary legislation.

questions.description: Write a critical assessment of the way in which the status of human rights in the European Union legal order has changed…
questions.text:
Write a critical assessment of the way in which the status of human rights in the European Union legal order has changed since the foundation of the EEC in the 1950s. 
 
Refer to relevant case law of the CJEU and to EU primary law (particularly the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU) and any other EU legislative initiatives that have played a role in bringing about this level of change.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgement: Case C-79/93 Bayerische Motorenwerke AG v ALO Auto/easing D GmbH (1995] ECR 1- 3439; Cases C-468-478/06Sot Lelos kai Sia EE and Others v GlaxoSmithKline AEVE Farmaketikon Proi'onton [2008] ECR 1- 71 39; Case 112/84 Humblot v Directeur des Services Fiscaux [1985] ECR 1367; Case 87/75 Bresciani v Amministrazione de/le Finanze [1976] ECR 129; Case C-438/05 International Transport Workers Federation (ITF) and Finnish Seamen's Union (FSU) v Viking Line  ABP  and 00  Viking  Line Eesti [2007]  ECR 1- 779; Case C-372/04,Watts v Bedford Primary Care Trust [2006] ECR 1-4325; Case C-236/09 Association Beige des Consommateurs Test-Achats v Conseil des  Ministries (2011] ECR 1- 773; Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Siochana v Workplace Relations Commission ECLl:EU:C:2018:90

questions.description: The 2014 EU Commission White Paper, entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' (COM (2014) 449) makes it clear …
questions.text:
The 2014 EU Commission White Paper, entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' (COM (2014) 449) makes it clear that, in its opinion: 
 
"[. ..] merger control at the· EU level was strengthened by the 2004 Merger Regulation reforms, particularly the introduction of the SIEG test". 
 
But also that: 
 
"[m]ore immediately, however, there are two main ways to improve the Merger Regulation through more limited amendments. First, the Commission considers bringing acquisitions of non-controlling minority shareholdings into the scope of EU merger control. Second, there is room to further streamline case referrals in the light of the Commission's experience with the 2004 reform". Consider, by reference where appropriate, to relevant case-law of the Union judicature, how this review of merger policy might affect the way in which EU law in this area has developed since the introduction of the original Mergers Regulation in 1989 (Regulation 4064/89) and subsequent amendments, including Regulation 139/2004?

questions.description: You have been approached by a UK Parliament House of Lords Select Committee to draft an opinion on what disadvantages th…
questions.text: You have been approached by a UK Parliament House of Lords Select Committee to draft an opinion on what disadvantages there may be for UK citizens if the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights is no longer a source of human rights law in the UK following Brexit. Draft this opinion, which should include a discussion on the key rights set out in the Charter and the decided case law of the CJEU on the application of the Charter.

questions.description: Article 4(2) of EU Directive 333/2017 (fictional) reduces maximum permitted chlorine levels in swimming pools to 3 parts…
questions.text: Article 4(2) of EU Directive 333/2017 (fictional) reduces maximum permitted chlorine levels in swimming pools to 3 parts per million. This maximum permitted level was previously set by EC Directive 456/2008 (fictional) at 5 parts per million. EU Directive 33/2017 was introduced to address concerns raised in a number of scientific studies about the links between the overuse of substances like chlorine and bleach and the increasing prevalence of asthma in children and young adults. The Directive was to be transposed into the national law of all EU Member States by31 March 2018. Ireland has not yet updated the Recreational Waters (Chemical and biological Substances) Regulations 2010 (fictional) to reflect the new lower permitted levels of chlorine in swimming pools. These 2010 Irish Regulations set the maximum permitted chlorine level at 5 parts per million. David, who is 24 years old, joined his local public sports centre in Howth in May 2018as he tried to get fit after several years of relative physical inactivity. He started his new fitness regime by going swimming five days per week at the sports centre, which is owned and managed by Howth Council. However, after six weeks of this he started to develop a painful skin condition and breathing difficulties which have left him unable to do any exercise. He is subsequently informed by his dermatologist that the skin condition has probably been caused by the chlorine in the swimming pool. His GP has also informed him that the swimming may also have caused his breathing difficulties. Tests have shown that the average daily chlorine level in the Howth Swimming Pool during the summer of 2018 was 4.7 parts per million. Referring to the relevant CJEU case law, advise David as to (i) whether he can rely on the provisions of EU Directive 333/2017 when bringing a claim for compensation before the national courts against Howth Council; and (ii) what he will have to establish in order to succeed in any such claim.

questions.description: Make a critical assessment about whether you think that the way in which the principle of proportionality has been appli…
questions.text: Make a critical assessment about whether you think that the way in which the principle of proportionality has been applied by the CJEU in its deliberations in relevant decided case-law concerning Articles 34-36 TFEU has made it too difficult for EU Member States to have national measures in place that are designed to protect consumers and to protect human health.

questions.description: Lola, from Argentina, moved to Ireland in August 2017 to undertake research for a PhD in Law at the University of Portar…
questions.text:
Lola, from Argentina, moved to Ireland in August 2017 to undertake research for a PhD in Law at the University of Portarlington (fictional). Her boyfriend, Fred, who is French, has worked as a chef in a restaurant in Portarlington since 2014. The couple met while Fred was touring Argentina by bicycle in May 2017 and Lola moved into Fred’s apartment in Portarlington as soon as she arrived in Ireland. Lola and Fred are not engaged to by married, but they have discussed the probability of getting married at some stage in the future. The registration fees for a PhD at the University of Portarlington are €12,200 per year for non-EU students. Lola paid for the first year of her registration through a combination of personal savings (€4,000) and a partial grant (€8,200). However, the grant was only for one year. Lola has applied to several funding bodies for financial support for years 2, 3, and 4 of her PhD studies, but all applications were unsuccessful. Her student visa only allows her to work on a part-time basis for up to 18 hours per week. She earns on average of €225 
per week working as a kitchen porter in the same restaurant as Fred. Her rent alone is €105 per week, leaving her with little money for the remainder of her living costs and with no way to pay the university fees. Lola does still have personal savings of €4,400. Lola is almost certainly going to have to drop out of her PhD programme. She will also therefore have to return to Argentina as her residency visa is only valid for as long as she is registered for study at the University. She is not at all happy about this as she and Fred are very happy together, and she has really been enjoying studying at the University of Portarlington. She also recently discovered that she is pregnant. Fred is the father. Lola has come to you for advice on whether there may be any provisions of EU law, as interpreted in relevant CJEU case-law that could assist her. Ideally, she would really like to remain on the PhD programme, and she would be happy to work additional hours at the restaurant while she still can to help pay for this. Alternatively, at the very least she would like to remain living with Fred and their future child in Ireland, even if she cannot continue with her studies for now. Advise Lola.

questions.description: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with rega…
questions.text: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with regard to certain Union acts; and (ii) judicial attempts that have been made to reform the test for individual concern in particular, analyse critically whether it has become any easier in 2018 for non-privileged applicants to seek judicial review of EU acts than it was when Advocate General Jacobs delivered his opinion on Case C-50/00, UPA v Council [2002] ECR I-6677. Your answer should include an analysis of the case-law of the Union judicature interpreting article 263(4) TFEU.

questions.description: Sandra is a 38 year old mother of three who has lived all of her life in Letterkenny, County Donegal. When she was 31 ye…
questions.text: Sandra is a 38 year old mother of three who has lived all of her life in Letterkenny, County Donegal. When she was 31 years old she started to have abdominal pains, which got progressively worse. After suffering these pains for nearly 15 months, Sandra went to see her GP who referred her to the local hospital for tests. Five months later, these initial hospital tests were inconclusive, so she was referred to see a consultant in a hospital in Dublin. After a further four months of waiting for an appointment in Dublin, the consultant there discovered that Sandra has a rare kidney disease that will continue to get progressively worse. While the disease is rarely  fatal, it does cause the patient to suffer from a lot of discomfort. Sandra describes the pain as being ‘intolerable most of the time and very bad the rest of the time’. Ultimately, Sandra will need to have a kidney transplant but as her condition is not thought to be fatal, she has been informed that it is likely to be many years before she reaches the top of the kidney transplant list. There is an interim operation that can be carried out on Sandra to relieve her pain and discomfort. Sandra, who does not have private health insurance or a medical card, was put on a public hospital waiting list to have this operation in March 2014. She was informed in December 2017 that there was still a 2-3 year waiting list for this procedure as there was only one surgeon in Ireland who can carry it out. Sandra is fed up of waiting for this operation and her pain continues to get worse. It is badly affecting her family life and she has had to give up her job as a hotel manager. She therefore looked into other options and later arranged to have the operation done in Germany at the cost of €8,500. She borrowed nearly €5,000 from her sister to finance this operation and paid the remainder from the entirety of her own personal savings. The operation in Germany was a success. Upon returning to Ireland, a neighbour informed Sandra that she had heard someone being interviews on the radio about the possibility of getting reimbursed by the Irish Health Service Executive (HSE) for operations carried out abroad. This neighbour was a bit sketchy on the details, but she thinks that the person being interviewed on the radio said that such reimbursements might be possible in some circumstances under EU law. Sandra telephoned her local HSE office in Donegal but the person who she spoke with there stated that she was unaware of any such scheme for reimbursement and also that she would be surprised if what her neighbour told her was trues, especially if the operation could have been carried out in Ireland at a later date. Sandra has approached you, as a local solicitor, for advice. She is keen to know whether there are any provisions of EU Law that she might be able to use to make a case for reimbursement of the costs of her operation in Germany to the HSE. Referring to decided case lase of the CJEU, advise Sandra on whether the TFEU provisions on the freedom to provide and receive services could help her case.

questions.description: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core…
questions.text: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgement: Case C-41/90, Hofner and Elser [1991] ECR I-1979; Joined Cases C-6/90 & 9/90, Francovich and others v Italy [1991] ECR I-5357; Case 6/64, Costa v ENEL [1964] ECR 585; Cases 21-24/72, International Fruit Company v Produktschap voor Groenten en Fruit [1972] ECR 1219; Case 87/75, Bresciani v Amministrazione delle Finanze [1976] ECR 129; Case C-55/94, Gebhard v COAP di Milano [1995] ECR I-4165

questions.description: Grange Garden Products Ltd. Launched a new type of hosepipe onto the Irish market in March 2018. The hosepipe contains a…
questions.text:
Grange Garden Products Ltd. Launched a new type of hosepipe onto the Irish market in March 2018. The hosepipe contains a device that was developed by grange Garden Products Ltd., the 4l-max, which limits the amount of water that can pass through the pipe to 4 litres per minute by pumping air into the water supply. Normal water consumption through standard hosepipes is 8-10 litres per minutes. During the drought of summer 2018, the Irish Government introduced a hosepipe ban in many areas to address low water supplies. However, this did not extend to those hosepipes which contained the 4l-max. Over 95% of all hosepipes sold in Ireland since July 2018 now use the 4l-max. Many other EU Governments introduced similar hosepipe bans during the summer of 2018, with similar exemptions for those using the 4l-max device. As a result, Grange Garden Products Ltd. Now has an 88% share of the EU-wide market in garden hosepipes, which were selling at wholesale for €24 in June 2018, but this has since risen to €31 per hosepipe. Each hosepipe that uses the 4l-max costs around €6.25 to make. The Irish Water Conservation Trust provided Grange Garden Products Ltd. With a grant of €1.75 million in February 2016 to research and develop the 4l-max. It has also recommended use of the 4l-max in its radio advertising campaign. The Irish Water Conservation Trust is a Government-funded body, the Board of which  is appointed by the Minister for Communications, Climate Action and the Environment. Warwick water is a British company. It has seen sales of its hosepipes plummet during the summer of 2018. 
Although all hosepipe bans have now been lifted in those EU Member States where they were applied, Warwick water cannot get any of the large retailers in the UK or in Ireland to stock its products. It has been met with similar difficulties when trying to access markets in Germany and France. It subsequently discovers that Grange Garden Products Ltd. Has informed some retailers that it will no longer supply them with their 4l-max hosepipes if they continue to stock similar products made by anyone else, including Warwick Water. Fearing the reintroduction of hosepipe bans, many retailers across the EU have acted on this threat by only selling 4l-max hosepipes. Advise Warwick Water about any possible breaches of EU Competition Law and/or State Aid Law.

questions.description: What are the general principles of EU law and, using examples from the case-law, discuss how are they used by the Court …
questions.text: What are the general principles of EU law and, using examples from the case-law, discuss how are they used by the Court of Justice to protect individuals and to ensure that: (i) the European Union institutions: and (ii) the Member States when implementing EU law, act in accordance with the rule of law?

questions.description:
Portarlington Pumps Ltd. have been manufacturing a range of car-washing devices and 
accessories since 1.993. Approximat…
questions.text:
Portarlington Pumps Ltd. have been manufacturing a range of car-washing devices and 
accessories since 1.993. Approximately 35% of their total sales of the Power Washer 2000, 
that they manufacture in Ireland and which is by far their best-selling product, are sold to 
customers in Ireland. Almost all of the remaining 65% of sales of the Power Washer 2000 are sold to shops in other EU Member States. Most of these sales are in the UK, with smaller 
volumes sold in Germany, France; The Netherlands and Belgium. Concerned at the high 
volumes of waste in the public water supply, and the high costs associated with supplying this water in the first place, the Scottish Executive is in the process of introducing a (fictional) statutory instrument, the Water Services (Amendment) Regulations 2018, placing restrictions on the marketing of, amongst other things, power washers that consume in excess of 18 litres of water per minute. The Power Washer 2000 uses 20.5 litres of water per minute. These restrictions will include: (i) a ban on advertising these power washers; (ii) a ban on offering free car- washing accessories with the purchase of the power washers; and (iii) setting a maximum nozzle size of 14cm in diameter for all domestic power washers. The Power Washer 2000 has a basic nozzle size of 16cm in diameter. Portarlington Pumps Ltd. are very concerned about the introduction of this statutory instrument, due to come into force in October 2018. Research conducted on behalf of the Scottish Executive has shown that sales of the affected power washers, such as the Power Washer 2000, are likely to fall by around 20% from late 2018 onwards as a direct result of these new marketing restrictions that will be introduced by the Water Services (Amendment) Regulations 2018. Portarlington Pumps Ltd. have approached your firm, asking whether there is any legal basis on which they can raise an objection to, or challenge the 2018 Scottish Regulations. You have been asked to investigate whether there is any potential conflict between  the Water Services (Amendment) Regulations 2018 and Article 34 TFEU. Prepare an opinion  for the company outlining, by reference to relevant case-law, whether the restrictions set by the Scottish Water Services (Amendment) Regulations 2018 are potentially incompatible with Article 34 TFEU on measures equivalent to quantitative restrictions on imports. In the opinion, assume that application of Article 34 to Scotland will not be affected following the United Kingdom's anticipated departure from the European Union in March 2019.

questions.description: The (fictional) EU Films and Television Productions Protection Directive 2013/112 introduced, amongst other things, an o…
questions.text:
The (fictional) EU Films and Television Productions Protection Directive 2013/112 introduced, amongst other things, an obligation, set out in Article 3 of the Directive,requiring EU Member States to adopt national laws prohibiting the illegal downloading of films from the internet by 31st May 2015. This provision of Directive 2013/112 was transposed into Irish law by the (also fictional) EU Downloading of Films and Television Programmes Regulations 2015, SI No 345/2015, which established that downloading films from the internet without making a payment for this (Regulation 5), or facilitating others in illegally downloading such films (Regulation 6), was an offence punishable, on summary conviction, by a fine of up to €5,000 and/or a term of imprisonment of up to eighteen months (Regulation 9). The Irish Regulations came into effect on 31 st October 2015. Liam Lynott launched an application in March 2015, which was shut down at the end of August 2016, that allowed anyone who used this 'app' to download and watch films on their mobile phones, without any charge. In January 
2018, Liam was convicted in the District Court of facilitating others in the illegal downloading of films on all dates between 1st June 2015 and 27th August 2016, contrary to Regulation 6 of the 2015 Irish Regulations, and he was handed a suspended prison sentence of two months as well as being fined €3,000. Liam is now also being sued for damages by Prion Film Distributors Ltd, who are an Irish company based in Longford and who were supplying newly-released films, including many of those which were available on Liam's app, to cinemas throughout west Leinster and Connaught since February 2013. Prion have estimated that Liam's app cost their business lost profits of approximately €120,000 per annum. Liam has launched an appeal to the Circuit Court against the severity of the sentence imposed upon him by the District Court. He is also looking to defend himself in the action for damages that has been brought against him by Prion Film Distributors Ltd. in the High Court Firstly, advise Liam about whether the case-law of the Court of Justice on the direct effect of EU directives might help him in: (i) his appeal against the severity of the sentence imposed upon him; and (ii) defending himself against the claim being brought by Prion Film Distributors Ltd. Secondly, by reference to case-law of the Court of Justice, assess whether the State might also be liable for the payment of damages to Prion Film Distributors Ltd. for any losses that they have suffered as a result of Liam Lynott's actions and, if so, in respect of what period such a liability may arise.

questions.description: Has the seminal decision of the Court of Justice in Case 25/62 Plaumann v Commission continued to make it 'literally imp…
questions.text:
Has the seminal decision of the Court of Justice in Case 25/62 Plaumann v Commission continued to make it 'literally impossible' for a non-privileged applicant to 'institute proceedings against an act' of the European Union under Article 263(4) TFEU? In your opinion, refer to relevant case-law, legislation and to attempts that may have been made (particularly by the amendment introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon), to make it easier for non-privileged applicants to use Article 263 TFEU to review the legality of acts of the EU 
institutions. Consider also how any such attempts have been interpreted by the Union judicature in its relevant case-law.
