questions.description: Bradley is an Australian national who moved to live in France in January 2014, in order to take up an offer of employmen…
questions.text: Bradley is an Australian national who moved to live in France in January 2014, in order to take up an offer of employment as an investment fund manager with Banque du Nord in Paris. A keen mountaineer, Bradley resigned from this post with the bank in April 2017 to start working in a temporary position as an employed hiking guide in the Massif Central. In February 2016 Bradley got married to one of his colleagues at the Banque du Nord, a Dutch national named Sophie. The marriage did not go well and the couple separated in early March 2017, soon after Sophie informed Bradley that she never wanted to have any children. Bradley moved out of their apartment in Paris but Sophie still lives there. Neither Bradley nor Sophie has yet filed for divorce. Bradley was initially granted a French residence permit in January 2014, which lasted for four years. It was due for renewal by January 27th 2018. However, his application for renewal of the residence permit was refused. Bradley is devastated by this refusal to renew his French residency permit as he really enjoys working as a hiking guide in France and believes. that this job has helped him to deal with the break-up of his marriage. He is even considering setting up his own hillwalking and mountaineering company, running adventure holidays in several mountainous locations across France. Bradley has approached you as a legal expert to ask you whether there are any European Union laws that he could use to challenge this refusal to renew his residency and working permit. His initial appeal to the local authorities in France was rejected. Referring particularly to relevant EU legislation and case-law of the Court of Justice,  advise Bradley.

questions.description: Following a series of scandals surrounding the contamination of powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula produced …
questions.text: Following a series of scandals surrounding the contamination of powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula produced in France, China and Brazil, the Irish Government, through the Department of Health, wants to establish a (fictional) scheme for the sampling and analysing of all such products, regardless of whether they are manufactured in Ireland, in other EU Member States, or in any third country. The (fictional) EU Directive 2017/678 on powdered baby milk and follow-on infant formula allows EU Member States to carry out sampling and analysis of these products, but it does not oblige any Member State to do so. According to the Irish Government's proposal, the costs of operating this scheme will be met by the imposition of a 10% value added tax on all imported powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula. The products of Irish producers will not be taxed in this way, but will, instead, have to be certified regarding the sampling and analysis undertaken and detailing the results of these investigations. Irish producers will have to pay for these tests and the related certification themselves. It is estimated that this will add an additional production cost to the Irish producers Of powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula of between 8-12%. You have been approached to advise the Department Of Health about the compatibility of the proposed scheme with EU law. In your opinion, referring to relevant case law of the Court of Justice and having regard to the (fictional) EU Directive 20171678, consider the compatibility of the proposed scheme with the TFEU provisions on charges of equivalent effect to customs duties and internal taxation.

questions.description: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the Court of  Justice of the European Union, setting …
questions.text: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the Court of  Justice of the European Union, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the Court of Justice, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the Court of Justice in its judgement: Case C-352/98, Laboratoires Pharmaceutiques Bergaderm SA and Goupil v Commission [2000] ECR 1-5291; Case 106/77, Amministrazione delle Finanze dello Stato v Simmenthal SpA [1978] ECR 629; Cases 21-24/72, International Fruit Company v Produktschap voor Groenten en Fruit [1972] ECR 1219; Case C-438/05, International Transport Workers Federation (ITF) and Finnish Seamen's Union (FSU) v Viking Line ABP and OÜ Viking Line Eesti [2007] ECR 1-779; Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs-og Selskabsstyrelsen [1999] ECR 11459; Cases C-501, 513, 515 and 519/06, GlaxoSmithKline Services Unlimited and others v Commission [2009] ECR 1-9291 ; Cases 6 and 7/73, Instituto Chemioterapico Italiano SpA and Commercial Solvents v Commission [1974] ECR 223; and Case C-413/06, Bertelsmann AG and Sony Corporation of America v Independent Music Publishers and Labels Association (IMPALA) [2008] ECR 1 4951.

questions.description: Answer Part (a) OR Part (b). Do not attempt both parts. Referring particularly to the relevant case-law of the Court of …
questions.text: Answer Part (a) OR Part (b). Do not attempt both parts. Referring particularly to the relevant case-law of the Court of Justice and EU legislation, prepare a critical assessment of whether the law is now sufficiently well-developed in relation to the issue of sex equality in employment that the focus of the relevant EU institutions may safely now be shifted to dealing with other forms of discrimination, such as that which is based on religious belief, age, race, disability and/or sexual orientation. OR By reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature concerning Article 102 TFEU, analyse the extent to which a dominant undertaking may take steps to protect its own commercial position in the market on which it is dominant, or on a related market, if its position is threatened by a rival and/or new entrant. Could such steps or protective behaviour be acceptable if the effect were specifically to strengthen or extend the undertaking's dominant market position.

questions.description: Outline, by reference to the relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case law of the Union Jurisdiction, t…
questions.text: Outline, by reference to the relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case law of the Union Jurisdiction, the roles of (i) the European Parliament; (ii) the Commission; and (iii) the Council in establishing new or amending legislative, delegated and implementing acts. Assess also whether the changes made to legislative procedures but the Lisbon Treaty amendments have improved the democratic legitimacy of decision making within the European Union.

questions.description: What are the general principles of EU law and, using examples from the case law, discuss how are they used but the Court…
questions.text: What are the general principles of EU law and, using examples from the case law, discuss how are they used but the Court of Justice to protect individuals and to ensure that the European Union institutions, and the Member States when implementing EU law, act in accordance with the rule of law?

questions.description: Write a case-note on any three of the following eight judgements of the Court of Justice of the European Union, setting …
questions.text: Write a case-note on any three of the following eight judgements of the Court of Justice of the European Union, setting out in each case note the core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the Courts of Justice, and how those issues were addressed by the said Court in its judgement: Cases C-501, 513, 515 and 519/06, GlaxoSmithKline Services Unlimited and others v Commission [2009] ECR I-9291 Case C-372/04, Watts v Bedford Primary Care Trust [2006] ECR I-4325 Case C-370/2012, Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012] ECLI 756 Case 27/76 United Brands Company and United Brands Continental BV v Commission [1978] ECR 207; Case 340/89 Vlassopoulou v Ministerium fur Justiz Bundes und Europaangelegenheiten Baden- Wurttemberg [1991] ECR 2357 Case C-236/2009 Association Belege des Consommateurs Tes-Achats and others v Council [2011] ECR I-773 Case C-352/98 Laboratories Pharmaceutiques Bergaderm SA and Groupil v Commission [2000] ECR I-5291 Case C-104/09 Roca Alvarez [2010] ECR I-8661.

questions.description: Article 5 of (the ficitional) Directive 2013/456/EU on the approximation of maximum permitted noise levels at outdoor mu…
questions.text:
Article 5 of (the ficitional) Directive 2013/456/EU on the approximation of maximum permitted noise levels at outdoor music concerts provides that: “all licence holders operating outdoor music concerts must ensure that those working at these events are subject to a maximum noise level of 116 decibels for a maximum continuous period of four hours.” Various methods are set out in the Directive for measuring the noise level at these events. There are also a series of definitions set out, which clearly classify security staff as being amongst that who are “working at these events” and there are therefore covered but the protection set out in the Directive. Article 12 of the same Directive further provides that: “member states shall bring into force the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with the Directive including as regards compliance by undertakings and other operators concerned by 31 March 2015.” Prior to the introduction of Directive 2013/456/EU, maximum permitted noise levels for concerts in Ireland were set out in the (fictional) Environmental Noise Act 1994. This 1994 Act allowed for exposure to a maximum noise level of 125 decibels for a continuous period of up to six hours. Both the 2013 EU Directive, and the 1994 Act, oblige the organisers of concerts to maintain records of the noise levels recorded during these events for five years. Brian was employed on a part time basis as a security guard by Bool Concerts LTD, between January 2014 amd October 2016. He worked at these events for an average of four days per month during this period. He was regularly exposed to noise levels which exceeded 116 decibels. At one heavy metal festival at which Brian was working, held in Limerick on 3 August 2016, the noise level was recorded as having reached 124 decibels. Brian was exposed to this high noise volume for up to five hours on that day. He has been suffering 
from tinnitus since this date and he is no longer able to work for Cool Concerts Ltd. As a security guard due to the almost constant ringing noise in his ears. Directive 2013/456/EU was not transposed into Irish Law until the introduction of the (fictional) Environmental Noise (Maximum Permitted Levels at Outdoor Events) Regulations in May 2017. The 2017 Irish regulations set out a range of fines that may be imposed, up to a maximum level of €2,000.00 for the most serious breaches. Brian now seeks your advice on whether he can bring a claim for compensation against either (i) Cool Concerts Ltd; or (ii) the State for the non-application and/or non-implementation of the 2013 Directive. Your advice should focus on whether or not, by reference to relevant EU law principles as developed in pertinent case law of the Union judicature, Brian was protected by the provisions of Directive 2013/456/EU on 3 August 2016 and, if so, whether any remedies any be available to him under EU law.

questions.description: Considering both the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with regard t…
questions.text: Considering both the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with regard to certain Union acts and judicial attempts that have been made to reform the test for individual concern in particular, analyse critically whether it has become any easier in 2017 for non-privileged applicants to seek judicial review of EU acts than it was when Advocate General Jacobs delivered his opinion on Case C-50/00, UPA v Council [2002] ECR I-6677. Your answer should include an analysis of the case –law of the Union judicature interpreting Article 263(4) TFEU.

questions.description: The Irish Government has recently introduced two (fictional) financial charges on wine regardless of where it is produce…
questions.text: The Irish Government has recently introduced two (fictional) financial charges on wine regardless of where it is produced. The first is an inspection charge, which is imposed on any wine for sale in Ireland to cover the costs of analysing samples to ensure that the grapes used in production where free of black rot disease at the time of production. These inspections have been ordered following the publication of a recent study by the Organic Growers if Ireland, which has suggested that the presence of this fungus could possible have dome detrimental consequences for the health of humans that consume the wine produced from infected grapes. Similar inspections have not been ordered in any other EU member states, However, (fictional) Directive 97/1234 does allow Member States to analyse samples of wine where health protection concern has been identified. The second charge is an additional charge of €1.50 tax on all bottles of wine that are sold by retailers for more that €25.75 per bottle. This is purely a revenue-raising measure. The average retail price per bottle of wine in Ireland is €13.25. There is no significant level of commercial wine production in Ireland. The European Commission has started an investigation into both the (i) the sampling charge and (ii) the additional retail tax. Advise the Government, by reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case law of the Union judicature, on the compatibility of both of these charges with Article 30 TREU and Article 110 TFEU.

questions.description: Trim Technologies Ltd have been producing mobile phones at its factory in County Meath since 1997. All of the phones tha…
questions.text: Trim Technologies Ltd have been producing mobile phones at its factory in County Meath since 1997. All of the phones that it produces use lithium-ion batteries manufactured with cobalt. It exports its phones to many EU member states, but its biggest markets are Spain and Portugal. In 2016, following ongoing concerns about the ethical and environmental consequences of cobalt mining in parts of Africa, several EU Member States, including Belgium, Luxembourg and Spain, introduced (fictional) domestic measures requiring that any mobile phones sold in their territory must be certified as being produced to minimum standards – including providing proof that the cobalt used in the production of their batteries was not extracted using child labour or involving dangerous mining conditions. To date no European Union legislation has been introduced that deals with the ethical or environmental consequences of cobalt mining. Trim Technologies Ltd sources its lithium-ion batteries from a company based in Germany. This company is unable to provide any certification that the batteries have been manufactured with components that did not involve child labour, nor can it certify about any of the other working conditions employed in their production. Certification is available, but only after sending the batteries off to a laboratory for analysis where it can be ascertained that the cobalt used came from specific areas where child labour is definitely no allowed. These tests are expensive. Advise Trim Technologies Ltd about the legal implication of these national measures, especially their compatibility with EU Treaty provisions on measures equivalent to quantitative restrictions on trade as interpreted by the Union judicature.

questions.description: Hugo moved to Ireland from France in March 2014. He found employment in a hotel in Kilkenny in Mat 2014. However, the wo…
questions.text: Hugo moved to Ireland from France in March 2014. He found employment in a hotel in Kilkenny in Mat 2014. However, the work was seasonal and his employment there was terminated in late September of the dame year. He did not have set of minimum hours of work during this five month period, working anything from 12 to 35 hours per week, depending on how many guests were staying at the hotel. Hugo was then unemployed for several months before the hotel in Kilkenny re-hired him in March 2015. Hugo left his job at the hotel in June 2015 after the manager accused him of stealing money from the cash register – an accusation Hugo strenuously denies. After another period of unemployment, Hugo applied for a place on a diploma course in catering management in Carlow Catering College. The fees for the two-year course were €2,200.00 per year. Hugo paid these fees during the first year but was unable to afford the fees for the second year of tuition. As a result, the catering college will not release his exam results for the second and final year of the course. Hugo is furious. He has since discovered that students who have lived in Carlow county for the past three years are entitled to apply for a bursary of up to €1,500.00 towards their college fees. Anyone who has worked in the hospitality sector in Ireland for the past year immediately prior to their enrolment at the college is also entitled to apply for this bursary where he or she has been made involuntarily unemployed. Hugo applied for the bursary, which is awarded by Carlow County Council, but this applications was refused on the grounds that he did not meet the conditions for the award. Advise Hugo on whether this refusal to consider his application for the bursary is compatible with EU law on free movement of persons and what his remedies might be.

questions.description: Outline the main changes that have been brought about to the roles of the various European Union institutions in the EU …
questions.text: Outline the main changes that have been brought about to the roles of the various European Union institutions in the EU law-making by the Lisbon Treaty amendments. Pay particular attention to the changes that have been made to the roles of; The European Parliament; The Commission; and The Council Discuss whether or not, and in what ways, these changes have made the European Union a more democratic organisation.

questions.description: The Court of Justice in Case 603/2010, Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of procedural autonomy available …
questions.text: The Court of Justice in Case 603/2010, Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of procedural autonomy available for Member States in deciding what remedies should be available at national level for breaches of European Law when it stated in paragraph 23 of the Judgement that: ‘….in accordance with settled case-law of the Court, in the absence of relevant European Union rules, the detailed procedural rules designed to ensure the protection of the rights which individuals acquire under European Union Law are a matter for the domestic legal order of each Member State in accordance with the principle of the procedural autonomy of the Member States, provided that they are not less favourable than those governing similar domestic situations and that they do not render impossible in practice or excessively difficult the exercise of rights conferred by the European Union legal order’. How have these principles of ‘equivalence’ and practical possibility referred to above been developed by the Union judicature to provide clear guidance for national courts on how they should deal with breaches of EU law?

questions.description: Write a case-note on any three of the following eight judgements of the European Court of Justice, setting out in each c…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any three of the following eight judgements of the European Court of Justice, setting out in each case-note the core facts, the key issues of EU law that arouse for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Union judicature: Case C-341/2005, Laval un Partneri Ltd v Svenska Byggnadsarbetareforbundet ea [2007] ECR I-11767; Cases C-402 and 415/2005, Yassin Abdullah Kadi and Al Barakaat International Foundation v Council and Commission [2008] ECR I-6351; Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs-og Selskabsstyrelsen [1999] ECR I-1459; Case C-370/2012. Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012] ECLI 756; Case C-280/2000, Atlmark Trans GmbH and Regierungsprasidium Magdeburg v Nahverjehrsgesellschaft Altmark FmbH [2003] ECR I-7747 Case C-459/99, Courage Ltd. v Crehan [2001] ECR I-6297; Case 112/84, Humblot v Directeur des Service Fiscaux [1985] ECR 1367; Case C-236/2009, Association Belge des Consommateurs Test-Achats and others v Council [2011] ECR I-773;

questions.description: When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on Case C-50/2000, UPA v Council, that: “[…] an indi…
questions.text: When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on Case C-50/2000, UPA v Council, that: “[…] an individual should be regarded as individually concerned within the meaning of the fourth paragraph of Article 230 EC by a Community measure where, by reason of his particular circumstances, the measure has, or is liable to have, substantial adverse effect on his interests” He was clearly articulating that there was a lack of effective judicial protection for non-privileged applicants who sought a judicial review of acts of the European Union’s institutions. Explain briefly the concerns that the Advocate General was addressing in his UPA Opinion; and Make an assessment of whether these concerns have been fully addressed by the changes that were made to Article 230 EC and what are now contained in Article 263 TFEU and related case law interpreting those changes.

questions.description: The Department of Health has sought to introduce a number of (fictional) measures in recent years in an attempt to reduc…
questions.text:
The Department of Health has sought to introduce a number of (fictional) measures in recent years in an attempt to reduce sugar consumption in Ireland. In 2012 the Sale of High Sugar Confectionary Regulations banned the use of vending machines in schools, when used to sell chocolate bars and sweets. Following a complaint made by vending machine suppliers based in Germany and Spain, and a chocolate manufacturer based in the UK, the EU Commission informed the Department that this ban should be lifted or infringement 
proceedings could be started. The 2012 Regulations were consequently amended and a Code of Conduct on the use of vending machines in schools was introduced instead. In 2014 the Sale of Goods (Advertising) Regulations were amended to include a prohibition on the advertising of high-sugar products in cinemas before 6pm. Again, there were complaints from several EU Member States and this restriction has to be lifted. (Fictional) Figures released by the World Health Organisation in January 2017 show that Ireland has the fastest-rising rates of overweightness in the European Union. It also showed that the Irish population are the second biggest consumers of added sugar in Europe. Public outcry ensued and, as a result, the Department of Health issued a (fictional) press statement in February 2017 indication that the Department was working on a Bill, which will include a provision setting the minimum price for soft drinks which contain more than 10g of added sugar per 100ml. The European Non-Alcoholic Beverages Association (ENBA), which represents the interests of soft drink manufacturers and retailers, have approached you about their concerns over this provision of the, yet to be published, Public Health (Sugar Consumption) Bill 2017. Of particular concern to them is the fact that the Court of Justice, in paragraph 49 of its Judgement of 23 December 2015 in Case C-333/14, Scotch Whiskey Association v The Lord Advocate and the Advocate General for Scotland stated that: “[i]t is for the referring court, which alone has available to it all the matters of fact and law pertaining to the circumstances…to determine whether a [less trade-restricting measure could be] capable of protecting human life and health as effectively as [minimum pricing]”. The ENBA fear that this will make it easier for the State to justify a national trade-restricting measure, which appears to be contrary to Article 34 TFEU, on the basis that it is necessary for the protection of human health, as potentially permitted by Article 36 TFEU. Advise the ENBA about this, highlighting the history of the case law of the Court of Justice on Article 34 TFEU, the possible exceptions to its application, and the relevance of the Judgement in Scotch Whiskey to the application of the Department of Health’s proposal on minimum pricing.

questions.description: Directive 2014/200/EU of the European parliament and of the Council on the prudential supervision of credit institutions…
questions.text: Directive 2014/200/EU of the European parliament and of the Council on the prudential supervision of credit institutions (fictional) establishes a number of new requirements for Member States, designed to ensure that only approved moneylenders are allowed to provide financial credit to customers. Article 9(1) of Directive 2014/200 specifically provides that: "[...] the competent authorities shall refuse authorisation to commence the activity of a credit institution where the initial capital is less that EUR 6 million". As an exception to this, Article 9(4) of the Directive further provides that: "[m]ember States may grant authorisation to credit institutions where the initial capital is less than that specified in paragraph 1, subject to the following conditions: the initial capital is no less than EUR 1 million; and the Member State notifies the Commission of their reasons for exercising this option". Article 13 of the Directive provides that the directors of credit institutions should not be paid any bonus in a year where the institution has failed to make a profit in excess of EUR 1 million. Article 60 of Directive 2014/200/EU states that it was to come into effect by 1 August 2014. The Bank of Ballintubber was granted authorisation to operate credit, and other, facilities by the Central Bank of Ireland on 22 September 2014, despite having an initial capital investment of only EUR 3 million. The EU Commission was never notified about this authorisation. The Supervision of Credit Institutions (Amendment) Regulations 2014, transposing Directive 2014/200/EU, were not introduced until 3 December 2014. In January 2017, following a series of poor lending decisions and the award of large bonuses to the directors of the Bank of Ballintubber, which never made any profits, it has now gone into liquidation. Secure Safes Ltd. are owed EUR 238,900 by the Bank of Ballintubber for the supply and installation of timelock safes during 2016. An initial assessment by the liquidator has shown that there is no chance that unsecured creditors like Secure Safes Ltd. will be paid what they are owed. Secure Safes Ltd. has  had to make two staff redundant as a result of this non-payment and the future of this limited company is also now in jeopardy. Advise Secure Safes Ltd., by reference to relevant Court of Justice case law, about whether it can rely on the provisions of Directive 2014/200/EU in separate actions that it is bringing before the Irish courts against (i) the State; (ii) the Central Bank of Ireland; and (iii) the directors of the Bank of Ballintubber.

questions.description: Lena moved from Russia to Belgium in 2004, when she was 17 years old. The following year she was convicted of several co…
questions.text:
Lena moved from Russia to Belgium in 2004, when she was 17 years old. The following year she was convicted of several counts of burglary before the Belgian courts and she was sentenced to three months' imprisonment. Following her release from prison in early 2006, Lena met Ivan, a Belgian national, and they had a child together in April 2009, a boy called Georgi, who was born in Brussels. Georgi is a Belgian national. Lena and Ivan, who have never been married, moved to Dublin in 2013. Soon after this, Lena started work as a nutritionist with a chain of heath food and nutrition supplements shops, called 'Healthy Ways'. Although she has worked in several of their stores around Dublin, Lena has never been promoted. During this period three of her male colleagues, only one of which had more experience and better qualifications than Lena, and one of her female colleagues, who had similar experience and qualifications to Lena, were promoted to assistant managerial positions. Almost seventy per cent of the Healthy Ways workforce are female. Lena previously worked as a nutrition advisor in a similar shop in Brussels, where she also passed a series of certificate exams and completed two correspondence courses on nutrition and supplementation. Lena and Ivan separated in 2015 and Ivan has returned to Belgium, where he has since met and got engaged to another woman. Despite being of the opinion that she is regularly overlooked for promotion and advancement in her employment, Lena really likes living in Dublin and Georgi started primary school there in September 2014. Lena has not yet met the requirements for naturalisation and Irish citizenship and her most recent application for permission to remain in Ireland was rejected in January 2017, because of her criminal 
record, relating to the offences of which she was convicted in Belgium in 2005. Lena has come to you for advice on (i) whether she may be able to appeal the refusal to grant her continued residence in Ireland because of her criminal record; and (ii) whether she should bring a claim against her employers over their failure to promote her, which she feels is based on gender-bias. Advise Lena on both of these issues by reference to relevant EU legislation and Court of Justice case law.

questions.description: The 2014 EU Commission White Paper, entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' .COM (2014) 449) makes it clear …
questions.text: The 2014 EU Commission White Paper, entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' .COM (2014) 449) makes it clear that, in its opinion: “[…] merger control at the EU level was strengthened by the 2004 Merger Regulation reforms, particularly the introduction of the SIEC test" But also that: "[m]ore immediately, however, there are two main ways to improve the Merger Regulation through more limited amendments. First, the Commission considers bringing acquisitions of non-controlling minority shareholdings into the scope of EU merger control. Second, there is room to further streamline case referrals in the light of the Commission's experience with the 2004 reform" How will this review of merger policy affect the way in which EU law in this area has developed since the introduction of the original Mergers Regulation in 1989 (Regulation 4064/89) and subsequent amendments, including Regulation 139/2004?

questions.description: Outline and assess the key changes and developments that have been made by Treaty amendments since the Single European A…
questions.text: Outline and assess the key changes and developments that have been made by Treaty amendments since the Single European Act of 1987 to the role of the European Parliament in the EU law making process. In particular, you should make an assessment about whether these changes may be sufficient to address concerns about the ‘democratic deficit’ which it has been claimed exists in the European Union.

questions.description: Answer Part (A) OR Part (B). Do NOT attempt both parts. Part (A) The Court of Justice in Case 603/2010, Pelati v Sloveni…
questions.text:
Answer Part (A) OR Part (B). Do NOT attempt both parts. Part (A) The Court of Justice in Case 603/2010, Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of procedural autonomy available for Member States in deciding what remedies should be available at national level for breaches of European Law when it stated in paragraph 23 of the Judgement that…. ‘….in accordance with settled case-law of the Court, in the absence of relevant European Union rules, the detailed procedural rules designed to ensure the protection of the rights which individuals acquire under European Union Law are a matter for the domestic legal order of each Member State in accordance with the principle of the procedural autonomy of the Member States, provided that they are not less favourable than those governing similar domestic situations and that they do not render impossible in practice or excessively 
difficult the exercise of rights conferred by the European Union legal order’. How have these principles of equivalence and practical possibility referred to above been developed by the Union judicature to provide clear guidance for national courts on how they should deal with breaches of EU law? Assess by reference to relevant case-law. 
 
OR Part (B) Article 5 of (the fictional) Directive 2012/456/EU on the approximation of maximum permitted noise-levels at outdoor music concerts provides that: ‘ all licence holders operating outdoor music concerts must ensure that those working at these events are subjected to a maximum noise level of 116 decibels for a maximum continuous period of four hours’. Various methods are set out in the directive for measuring the noise level at these events. A series of definitions is also set out which clearly classify security staff as being  amongst those who are ‘working at these events’ and who are therefore covered by the protection set out in Article 5 of the Directive. Article 12 of the same Directive further provides that: ‘member states shall bring into force laws. Regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with this Directive including as regards compliance by undertakings and other operators concerned by 31 March 2014’. Prior to the introduction of Directive 2012/456/EU , maximum permitted noise levels for concerts in Ireland were set out in the (fictional) Environmental Noise Act 1994. This 1994 Act allowed for exposure to a maximum noise levels of 125 decibels for a continuous period of up to 6 hours. Brian was employed on a part time basis as a security Guard by Cool Concerts Ltd between January 2013 and October 2015. He worked at outdoor music concert events for an average of four days per month during this period. He was regularly exposed to noise levels which exceeded 116 decibels. At one heavy metal festival at which Brian was working, held in Limerick on 3rd August 2015, the noise levels reached 124 decibels. Brian was exposed to this high noise volume for up to five hours on that day. He has been suffering from tinnitus 
since this date and he is no longer able to work for Cool Concerts Ltd as a security guard due to the almost constant ringing noise in his ears. Directive 2012/456/EU was not transposed into Irish Law until the introduction of the (fictional) Environmental Noise (Maximum permitted levels at outdoor events) Regulations in May 2016. Brian now seeks your advice on whether he can bring a claim for compensation against either (i) Cool Concerts Ltd or (ii) the State for the non-application and/or non-implementation of the 2012 Directive. Your advice should focus on whether or not Brian was protected by the provisions of Directive 2012/456/EU on 3rd August 2015 and if so, whether any remedies may be available to him under EU Law.

questions.description: Article 3 of (fictional) Regulation (EU) No 999/2016 on the use of traffic cones on national primary roads in the intern…
questions.text: Article 3 of (fictional) Regulation (EU) No 999/2016 on the use of traffic cones on national primary roads in the internal market sets out the specifications for these products where they are used by national road authorities in all EU member states. It states, amongst other things, that all traffic cones when used on national primary roads must be (i) 95 cm in height (ii) have a base that is 45cm wide (iii) be bright orange in colour and (iv) be covered by a reflecting white band between the 28cm and 42cm vertical points. Saggart Safety Company Ltd have been manufacturing traffic cones since 1977. They are the sole supplier of traffic cones to those responsible for road safety in Ireland, including An Garda Siochana and the Road Safety Authority. Saggart Safety produced 95% of all traffic cones sold in Ireland in 2015. The standards set for traffic cones by Regulation 999/2016 are based on those which are already in use in every EU member state, except for two, Ireland and Malta. Advise Saggart Safety Co Ltd as to whether there is any basis upon which they may possibly have a standing (locus standi) under Article 263 TFEU to challenge the legality of Regulation 999/2016 before the Court of Justice.

questions.description: Referring to the relevant case law and legislation, make an assessment of whether EU law and its implementation by the M…
questions.text: Referring to the relevant case law and legislation, make an assessment of whether EU law and its implementation by the Member States is now sufficiently well developed in relation to the issue of sex equality in employment that the focus of the relevant EU institutions can now be shifted to dealing with other forms of discrimination such as that which is based on religious belief, age, race, disability and /or sexual orientation.

questions.description: The Department of Health introduced the (fictional) Water Coolers at Work Bill in May 2016 as a direct response to a ser…
questions.text: The Department of Health introduced the (fictional) Water Coolers at Work Bill in May 2016 as a direct response to a series of scientific studies which have shown that there has been at least 14 cases of infection, per year for each of the six years, with Hepatitis A in Ireland following the consumption of contaminated water from coolers. Under the provisions set out in the bill, all water used in coolers that is for consumption in places of employment must now be certified as being free from Hepatitis A. Inspections of the containers of water used in coolers are to be carried out by officials from the Irish Department of Health. The inspection and certification process applies to both Irish water and that imported from any other country including all EU member states. The Department of Health is also promoted to impose a charge on producers to cover the costs of these inspections. There is no provision for EU law which provides that water used in coolers must be inspected for safety purposes but Directive 1997/313 (fictional) does allow member states to carry out inspections of any beverages, including water, where they are in possession of evidence showing that there is a potential risk of infection. The EU commission and several EU member states are concerned about the ramifications that the introduction of the Water Coolers at Work Bill 2016 could have for the free movement of bottled water. Advise the Department of Health as to whether the provisions of the 2016 Bill on the inspection, certification and charging of producers for these inspections would, if enacted, be compatible, or otherwise, with the Treaty provisions and related case-law on the free movement of goods – in particular Articles 30 and 34 TFEU.

questions.description: Write a case note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgements of the Union judicature, setting out in each case note …
questions.text:
Write a case note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgements of the Union judicature, setting out in each case note the core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Court of Justice in the three judgements chosen: CASE c-352/98 Laboratoires Pharmaceutiques Bergaderm SA and Goupil v Commission [2000] ECR-I 5291; Cases C-402 and 415/2005 Yassin Abdullah Kadi and Al Barakaat International Foundation v Council and Commission [2008] ECR-I- 6351; Case C370/2012 Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012] ECLI 756; Case 106/77 Amministrazione delle Finanze dello Stato v Simmenthal SpA [1978] ECR 629; 
Case C-453/99 Courage Ltd v Crehan [2001] ECR-I 6297; Case C438/2005 International Transport Workers Federation and Finnish Seamens Union v Viking Line ABP and Viking Line Eesti [2007] ECR I- 779; Case C-236/2009 Association Belge des Consommateurs Test Achats and others v Council [2011] ECR I- 773; Case C-384/93 Alpine Investments v Minister van Finacien [1995] ECR I- 1141.

questions.description: ‘The status of EU citizenship created by EU law has been criticised on various grounds, including the thinness of the ri…
questions.text: ‘The status of EU citizenship created by EU law has been criticised on various grounds, including the thinness of the rights created and their economic focus the conditions to which they are subject, the reinforcement of the distinction between third country nationals and EU nationals, the limited impact of the new electoral rights, and the reluctant pace of implementation’ Craig and De Burca, EU Law, 6th edition (OUP 2015) Present a discussion on whether, or to what extent, you agree with this statement on the legal nature of EU citizenship, with particular consideration for developments in the case-law of the Court of Justice, such as those delivered in the judgements in Ruiz Zambrano, Metock and Dano.

questions.description: Answer Part (A) OR Part (B). Do NOT attempt both parts. Part (A) Explain, by reference to relevant case-law, the concept…
questions.text:
Answer Part (A) OR Part (B). Do NOT attempt both parts. Part (A) Explain, by reference to relevant case-law, the concepts of ‘agreement’, ‘decision’ and ‘concerted practice’ in Article 101 (1) TFEU. Also, explain, by reference to relevant case law, how a ‘dominant position’ can be held by ‘one or more undertakings’ under Article 102 TFEU. 
 
OR Part (B) By reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature concerning Article 102 TFEU. Analyse the extent to which a dominant undertaking may take steps to protect its own commercial position in the market on which it is dominant or on a related market, if its position is threatened by a rival and/or new entrant. Could such steps or protective behaviour be acceptable if the effect is specifically to strength or extend the undertaking’s dominant market position?

questions.description: Answer Part (A) AND Part (B) (A) Outline and discuss the key functions in the creation and application of European Union…
questions.text: Answer Part (A) AND Part (B) (A) Outline and discuss the key functions in the creation and application of European Union law of (i) The European Commission, (ii) The European Parliament and (iii) the Council AND (B) Assess the key differences between the legal nature of those laws that are introduced into national law by a directive and those which are introduced by a regulation, as set out in Article 288 TFEU.

questions.description: How are the ‘general principles’ of EU law such as fundamental rights, legal certainty, non-discrimination and proportio…
questions.text: How are the ‘general principles’ of EU law such as fundamental rights, legal certainty, non-discrimination and proportionality recognised and used by the Court of Justice in its interpretation and application of EU law?

questions.description: In January 2016, a (fictional) regulation, Regulation (EU) No 26/2016 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 2…
questions.text:
In January 2016, a (fictional) regulation, Regulation (EU) No 26/2016 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 23 January 2016 on setting maximum permitted power usage levels for household appliances, was published in the Official Journal. Article 55 of Regulation 26/2016 provides that: ‘This Regulation shall enter into force on the 20th day following its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union [and] it shall apply from 12 March 2016, with the exception of Article 10(2) which shall apply from 13 August 2016’. Article 10(2) of the same regulation states that: ‘Household vacuum cleaners shall have a maximum permitted input power of 1300 watts’. Virginia Vacuums, based in Co Cavan have manufactured budget-priced household vacuum cleaners since 1997. All of their products have an input power level of 1400 watts or greater and are significantly less expensive that most of their competitor’s products. As a result they have become very competitive with consumers in those EU member states where they are sold/ Their main markets are in Ireland, the UK, France, Germany, Italy and Poland although they do sell their products in most other Member States as well. They currently have a 40% market share in all vacuum cleaners sold across the EU. 85% of all vacuum cleaners 
with a power rating above 1400 watts that are sold in the EU Aare manufactured by Virginia Vacuums. This latter market share has been steadily rising since 2008. Virginia Vacuums now seek your advice on whether they have any legal standing to challenge this new Regulation 26/2016 before the General Court of the European Union. Advise Virginia Vacuums on the basis of existing case law and TFEU provisions.

questions.description: Answer (A) OR (B). Do not answer both parts. (A) Discuss, with reference to relevant case law in particular, the extent …
questions.text: Answer (A) OR (B). Do not answer both parts. (A) Discuss, with reference to relevant case law in particular, the extent to which and the conditions by which EU directives have ‘direct effect’ in national law. OR (B) Critically assess the jurisprudence of the Court of Justice on the direct effect of International Agreements such as the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) and the World Trade Organisation (WTO) Agreements in European Union and National Law.

questions.description: Pollaphuca Publishing Ltd have been producing and marketing books in Ireland since 1949. Their focus as a company has al…
questions.text: Pollaphuca Publishing Ltd have been producing and marketing books in Ireland since 1949. Their focus as a company has always been the production of high quality and well-crafted books. All of its products are hard bound with the covers made from leather. Most of the leather that they use for this purpose is imported into Ireland from other EU Member States including Italy, Spain and France. Ireland introduced a new (fictional) law in 2015, the Animal Products (Control of the Spread of Disease) Regulations. The regulations include a provision that requires that all imported leather must be inspected by officials from the Irish Department of Agriculture for possible traces of animal disease before it can be brought into and used in the State. If no traces of any disease are found on inspected leather then the Department of Agriculture will provide certification verifying this. Directive 2011/613 (fictional) of the European Parliament and of the Council of 15 April 2011 on measures that they may be adopted to control the spread of disease in bovine animals includes a provision in Article 7 thereof which states that: ‘Member States may require the production of a valid certification upon importation from any other Member State or Third Country that any product made from bovine animals is free from any transferrable diseases’. Pollaphuca Publishing imported a consignment of 12 tonnes of leather from Spain in December 2015 which was subjected to the required inspection by the Department of Agriculture. Pollaphuca Publishing have now received a bill from the Department of Agriculture for €2,400 before they will provide the required certification, which they state is to cover the costs of carrying out the inspections and related administrative charges. On the basis of existing case law and TFEU provisions, advise Pollaphuca Publishing Ltd as to whether the carrying out of inspections under the Animal Products (Control of the Spread of Disease) Regulations 2015 and the imposition of related charges is compatible with EU Law.

questions.description: With reference to relevant case-law and Commission decisions, analyse critically the extent to which Article 106 TFEU pr…
questions.text: With reference to relevant case-law and Commission decisions, analyse critically the extent to which Article 106 TFEU provides for an exception to the application of EU competition law, particularly Articles 101 and 102 TFEU and the scope and limits to any such exception as regards public undertakings, undertakings wo which Member States have granted special or exclusive rights and undertakings entrusted by Member States with the operation of services of general economic interest.

questions.description: Ellen, who will be 47 years old next July has been employed by Cutlery Catering Ltd since 2004. She was initially employ…
questions.text:
Ellen, who will be 47 years old next July has been employed by Cutlery Catering Ltd since 2004. She was initially employed as a catering assistant on a part time basis for four years during which time she had no set hours of work but instead would be called in by the company to assist at large functions. This usually involved working one 12 hour shift per week at these functions as well as some additional hours, normally 6 or 7, helping out with the company’s book-keeping. Some weeks she had no work at all but she did normally work 
at least 18-20 hours per week for 32 to 38 weeks of the year. From July 2008 on, Ellen was employed on a full time basis by Cutlery Catering Ltd usually working for at least 49 hours per week. Her salary rose from the part time rate of €11.75 per hour to the full time rate of €14.50 per hour. In April 2010, Cutlery Catering Ltd signed all of their full time employees up to a private pension scheme. This was to be backdated to their starting date of full time employment with the company. Cutlery Catering Ltd made payments to the private pension company to make up for the shortfall in contributions which were not paid for full-time or any employees prior to April 2010. Ellen is concerned that when she retires, in around 20 years time, she will only have made 28 years of contributions to the pension scheme. As this is a defined contribution scheme, where pensions received are based upon years of contributions, she can only receive a maximum pension based on these 28 years (July 2008 until date of compulsory retirement from the company, at 67 years of age – July 2036) and not the usual maximum of 40 years. Ellen is of the opinion that she should be entitled to a pensionable period that also includes the years 2004-2008 when she was employed by Cutlery Catering on a part-time basis. This would at least raise her pension to one based on 32 years contributions. 70% of all full time employees with Cutlery Catering are male. She has also raised some concerns about the fact that she was paid at a lower rate (hourly) during this period than her full time colleagues (by €2.75 per hour). Her employers have dismissed these concerns stating that they are entitled to offer better conditions of employment to different categories of workers dependent upon their full time or part time status. Advise Ellen as to any possible breaches by Cutlery Catering Ltd of EU law equality.

questions.description: Answer part (A) and part (B) (A) Outline and discuss the key differences between the legal nature of (i) regulations (ii…
questions.text:
Answer part (A) and part (B) (A) Outline and discuss the key differences between the legal nature of (i) regulations (ii) 
directives and (iii) decisions in European Union Law as set out in Article 288 TFEU; (B) What are the general principles of European Union Law and how are they used in the interpretation and application of the European Union legal order?

questions.description: Has the introduction of the ordinary legislative procedure under Article 289 TFEU set the type of role for the European …
questions.text: Has the introduction of the ordinary legislative procedure under Article 289 TFEU set the type of role for the European Parliament in the legislative process that should satisfactorily address any outstanding concerns that may have existed about a lack of democratic decision making in the creation of European Union laws?

questions.description:
When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his opinion on Case C-50/2000 
UPA v Council; that: ‘[…] an indi…
questions.text:
When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his opinion on Case C-50/2000 
UPA v Council; that: ‘[…] an individual should be regarded as individually concerned within the meaning of the fourth paragraph of Article 230 EC by a community measure where, by reason of his particular circumstances, the measure has, or is liable to have, a substantial adverse effect on his interests’. He was clearly articulating that there was a lack of effective judicial protection for non-privileged applicants who sought a judicial review of acts of the European Union’s institutions. Explain briefly the concerns that the Advocate General was addressing here. Also, make an 
assessment of whether these concerns have been fully addressed by the changes that were made to Article 230 EC by what is now contained in Article 263 TFEU and related case law.

questions.description: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. Part (A) The Court of Justice in Case C-603/2010 Pelati v Sloven…
questions.text:
Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. Part (A) The Court of Justice in Case C-603/2010 Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of 
procedural autonomy available for Member States in deciding what remedies should be 
available at national level for breaches of European Union law when it stated in paragraph 23 of the Judgement that: ‘[…] in accordance with settled case-law of the Court, in the absence of relevant European 
Union rules, the detailed procedural rules designed to ensure the protection of the rights which individuals acquire under European Union law are a matter for the domestic legal order of each Member State in accordance with the principle of the procedural autonomy of the Member States, provided that they are not less favourable than those governing similar domestic situations and that they do not render impossible in practice or excessively difficult the exercise of rights conferred by the European Union legal order’. How have these principles of ‘equivalence’ and ‘practical possibility’ referred to above been 
developed by the Union judicature to provide clear guidance for national courts on how they should deal with breaches of EU law. OR Part (B) Article 6 of (the fictional) Directive 2012/1210/EU on the use of mobile phone technology in cars and motor vehicles provides that: ‘[all] cars or motor vehicles, whether used or new, sold by any undertaking or other operator after the implementation of this Directive must include, as a minimum safety requirement […] a ‘hands free’, ‘bluetooth’ or similar capability for using the device in that private car or vehicles which provides the person(s) in control of that private car or vehicle with the possibility of orally communicating through their mobile device without having to hold it in their hand’. Article 29 of the same Directive further provides that: ‘[member] States shall bring into force the laws, regulations and administrative provisions 
necessary to comply with this Directive including as regards compliance by undertakings and other operators concerned by 16 June 2014’. Sonia was knocked down by a car, which was being driven by Graham, in January 2015 while she was out walking her dog. The driver of the car, Graham, mounted the footpath while holding and dialling a number on his mobile phone at the time of his collision with Sonia. Sonia suffered bad injuries to her neck and spine in the accident. She has to give up her job as a physiotherapist as a result and she is finding it difficult to find alternative suitable employment. She is also undergoing a long and expensive course of physical rehabilitation. She was awarded €9,000 compensation paid by Graham’s motor insurance company, but she insists that it only partially covers the losses that she has and continues to suffer. Graham has been convicted under the Road Traffic Acts of causing harm by dangerous driving and, as a consequence, was fined €2,000. He was also disqualified from driving for 2 years. He subsequently, and consequently, lost his job as a double glazing window salesman. Graham maintains that he would have used a ‘hands free’ function on his phone had his car 
been fitted with the equipment to do so. The car had not been fitted with any such device or 
capability. He bought the car, which was second hand and four years old in October 2014 from Reville Auto’s Limited which is based in Enniscorthy, County Wexford. Directive 2012/1210/EU was not transposed into Irish Law until the introduction of the 
(fictional) Dangerous Driving (Use of Mobile Phones in Cars)(Amendment) Regulations in 
May 2015. Sonia seeks your advice on whether she can bring a claim for compensation against either (i) Reville’s Autos Limited or (ii) the State for the non-application and/or non-implementation of the 2012 Directive.

questions.description: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. Part (A) The (fictional) Health (Consumption of Sugar) Act 2014 …
questions.text:
Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. Part (A) The (fictional) Health (Consumption of Sugar) Act 2014 was introduced by the Irish 
Government as part of State Policy designed to combat rising rates of obesity in the population.  Article 9 of the Act introduces maximum permitted sugar levels in non-alcoholic beverages. It sets this at 10g of added sugar per 100ml of water used in preparation. Borris Beverages Limited produce a range of non-alcoholic drinks, including their most 
popular brand, Toxicola, which has an added sugar content of 18g per 100ml of water. Since 
the introduction of the 2014 Act they have reduced the sugar content used in Toxicola to the 
permitted 10g level but sales of the product have declined dramatically since then. Consumer research undertaken by Borris Beverages Limited indicates that people have stopped buying Toxicola as they do not like the new reduced sugar recipe. Borris Beverages Limited also imports a number of drinks from elsewhere in the European 
Union, including Limelov, which is made in France and contains 12g of added sugar per 100ml of water. They have continued to import and market Limelov in Ireland after the application of the 2014 Act, presuming that Article 9 of the legislation only applies to domestically produced products but they have now been warned that the continued sale of this product in Ireland may actually be contrary to the legislation. Borris Beverages have made a compliant to the European Commission about the application 
of the Health (Consumption of Sugar) Act 2014. The Commission has written to the 
Department of Health seeking reassurances from the Irish Government that the Act is in compliance with TFEU obligations on the free movement of goods. Advise the Department of Health. OR Part (B) Write a legal opinion on whether the introduction by the Irish Government of an additional €2 tax on the sale of every bottle of wine from off-licenses and restaurants may be contrary to Article 110 TFEU. You may presume that Ireland produces very little wine when compared to other alcoholic 
beverages that are not subject to the additional taxation.

questions.description: Colm is a 20 year old student who completed his leaving certificate school exams in Ireland in June 2014. He has always …
questions.text:
Colm is a 20 year old student who completed his leaving certificate school exams in Ireland in June 2014. He has always wanted to study homeopathy and alternative medicine at university but he has not done well enough in his leaving certificate to get a place on any of the alternative medicine courses offered in Ireland. He did not want to repeat his leaving certificate in 2015 as he felt that he was already quite old for school, having taken a transition year option and having repeated his fifth year due to several long periods of absence when he was suffering from a virus that left him with very little energy. He later applied to, and was accepted for, homeopathy and alternative medicine at the New University of Amsterdam in the Netherlands. The course starts in October 2015. Colm moved to Amsterdam in February 2015 as he wanted to learn some Dutch and earn some money before starting his course. He worked for an average of 15 hours per week in a 
supermarket as well as one evening per week of voluntary work at a local animal hospital. He earned around €160 per week which covered his rent of a small bedsit and some other expenses but he did not save any money during this period and his income was supplemented by a gift of €75 which was sent from his mother every month. Given his low income, Colm will not be able to pay his fees or to support himself during his 
studies so he has applied to the Netherlands Student Support Scheme which offers low cost 
loans to students. His application was refused however, as he is not a Dutch national. Colm’s 
virus recurred during July 2015 and he had to spend a week in hospital in Amsterdam. He has just been sent a bill for €5700 by the hospital as public medical care is only free in the  Netherlands for non-nationals when they are in full time employment. Colm has returned to Ireland in order to avoid paying his medical bills. He is however, very 
keen to get back to Amsterdam so that he can start his new course. Advise Colm as to whether he can (i) appeal the decision of the Netherlands Student Support Scheme on the basis that it amounts to discrimination against a non-national who is a citizen of another EU Member State and (ii) challenge the bill that he has been sent by the hospital.

questions.description: Make a critical assessment of the ways in which European Union law had been developed and applied to ensure that, where …
questions.text: Make a critical assessment of the ways in which European Union law had been developed and applied to ensure that, where possible, men and women are treated equally in employment. Your answer should refer to the provisions set out in: Article 157 TFEU; and related secondary legislation; and the most significant related case-law of the Court of Justice.

questions.description:
Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments, setting out in each case-
note the core facts, the key …
questions.text:
Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments, setting out in each case-
note the core facts, the key issues of EU Law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Union Judicature: Case T-201/2004, Microsoft v Commission [2007] ECR II 3601; Case C-370/2012 Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012] 
ECLI756; Joined cases 56 and 58/1964 Consten and Grundig v Commission [1966] ECR 299; Case C-200/2002 Zhu and Chen [2004] ECR I-9925; Case T-342/2007, Ryanair Holdings v Commission [2010] ECR II-3457; Case C-236/2009 Association Belge des Consommateurs Test- Achats and others v Council 
[2011] ECR I-773; Case C-384/1993 Alpine Investments v Minister van Faincien [1995] ECR I-1141; Case 81/1987, The Queen v HM Treasury ex parte Daily Mail and General Trust Plc [1988] ECR 5483.

questions.description: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do NOT attempt both parts. (A) Having regards to both its role and its composition, analyse…
questions.text: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do NOT attempt both parts. (A) Having regards to both its role and its composition, analyse critically whether the Commission effectively ‘promotes the general interest of the Union’ as required by Article 17(1) TEU. Your answer should particularly focus on the Commission’s power of initiative in the legislative process, its function in ensuring the application of measures adopted by the Union institutions and its role as ‘guardian of the Treaties’. It should also refer to relevant case law of the Union judicature. OR (B) By reference particularly to Treaty provisions and relevant case-law of the Court of Justice compare and contrast: the legal nature of a ‘regulation’, a ‘directive’ and a ‘decision’ in Union law; the core differences between a ‘legislative act’ and a ‘non legislative act’ in Union law as regards both their nature and the means by which they are adopted; the difference between ‘delegated’ acts and ‘implementing’ acts in Union law.

questions.description:
Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do NOT attempt both parts. 
 
(A) ‘The right to an effective remedy is a general principle …
questions.text:
Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do NOT attempt both parts. 
 
(A) ‘The right to an effective remedy is a general principle of EU law that exists within the context of the multi-level constitutional system of the EU’ (Hoffmann, HCH in his essay Article 47- Right to an effective remedy’ in the EU charter of Fundamental Rights – A commentary (Peers, Hervey, Kenner & Ward editors, 2014) at page 1211. By reference, in particular, to relevant case-law and the scope of the right conferred by Article 47 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU as well as its import for the principles of effectiveness and equivalence, discuss critically the view expressed by Professor Hoffmann as to the import of the right to an effective remedy. OR (B) ‘No one shall be liable to be tried or punished again in criminal proceedings for an offence for which he or she has already been finally acquitted or convicted within the Union in accordance with the law’ (Article 50 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU). Discuss critically, by reference particularly to Article 50 of the Charter and to relevant case law of the Union judicature, the scope and application of the principle of ne bis in idem in EU law.

questions.description: Article 119(2) TFEU provides that the activities of the Union ‘shall include a single currency, the euro, and the defini…
questions.text: Article 119(2) TFEU provides that the activities of the Union ‘shall include a single currency, the euro, and the definition and conduct of a single monetary policy and exchange-rate policy the primary objective of both of which shall be to maintain price stability’ and that ‘without prejudice to this objective’, the Union’s monetary policy shall ‘support the general economic policies in the Union’. Discuss by reference to relevant case law in particular the judgment of the Court of Justice in Case C-34-09 Pringle v Government of Ireland, the scope and limits of the exclusive Union competence with regard to monetary policy. To what extent is that competence limited by the express prohibitions on monetary financing contained in Article 123(1) TFEU and on the assumption of the commitments of Member States and/or the public authorities in Article 125(1) TFEU?

questions.description: Consider the following fictional situation which is based loosely on that considered by the Court for Justice in Case C-…
questions.text:
Consider the following fictional situation which is based loosely on that considered by the Court for Justice in Case C-244/13 Ogieriakhi v Minister for Justice and Equality (Judgment of 10 July 2014 : C: 2014: 2068). Edward, a Somalian national, came to Ireland fleeing war torn Somalia in 1998 and claimed asylum. A year later he married a Spanish national, Maria, withdrew his asylum application and was granted a one year residence permit on the basis of his marriage to Maria. This enabled Edward to fins work with a semi-state body. For the next three years, Edward lived with Maria, who, having initially come to Ireland in 1998, was an employee until June 2004. Each year, Edward’s residence permit was renewed for a further year. In June 2001, Maria moved out of the family home to take up residence with a woman. Subsequently, Edward moved out of to reside with Deirdre, an Irish national, with whom he subsequently had an Irish National child, born in December 2005. From June 2001, Maria played no part in the provision or the making available of housing for Edward and in June 2004 when her relationship with her female partner broke down, she returned to Spain. In May 2005, the Minister for Justice and Equality refused to renew Edward’s residence permit on the basis 
that he could no longer show that Maria was exercising her EU rights in Ireland and/or providing him with housing. In May 2006, Edward submitted an application for a permanent right of residence contending that the right to permanent  residence, conferred by Articles 16 and 18 of Directive 2004/38/EC of the EU Parliament and of the Council of 29 April 2004 on the right of citizens of the Union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States, could be invoked by family members like him, who have legally resided in a host ember State for a continuous period of five years. Edward claimed that he satisfied this requirement with regard to the period prior to this deadline for transposition of that Directive i.e. 30 April 2006. The Minister rejected this application in May 2007 on the basis that Edward could not rely on periods of residence prior to 30 April 2006 as well as on the basis that, since Maria had neither resided with nor provided housing for Edward since June 2001, he was no longer entitled to a residence permit under the previously applicable Article 10 of Regulation No 1612/68 of the Council of 15 October 1968 on freedom of movement for workers within the Community. Edward challenged this decision unsuccessfully in judicial proceedings in the High Court. Edward appealed but withdrew his appeal, when the State agreed not to pursue him for the costs of the High Court if he withdrew it. However, on learning subsequently of the judgment of the Court of Justice in Case C-162/09 Lassal, Edward sought leave from the Supreme Court to re-enter his appeal. The Supreme Court refused this request and a linked request for a preliminary reference. In May 2012, Edward also applied to the Minister for Justice and Equality to reconsider his May 2007 decision in the light of Lassal judgment. In May 2014, the Minister finally took a decision with regard to this application and granted Edward a right of permanent residence but based this decision not on EU law but on the exercise of her discretion under Irish law having regard particularly to his Irish National child. In December 2005, Edward was dismissed from his job with his semi-state employer on the sole basis that he no longer had a right of residence in Ireland. He was unable to work again and became dependent upon his partner Deirdre until May 2014. Edward has sought your advice as an EU law specialist regarding whether: He was entitled to the right to permanent residence conferred by Directive 2004/38/EC based on his residence prior to 30 April 2006 and if so, whether; The decision of his former employer based on the Ministers refusal to grant him a residence permit may be challenged and, if so, on what basis; He would have an entitlement to compensation and, if so, on what basis and from whom and for what could damages be sought?

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of ANY TWO of the following three (hypothetical) measures and practices with Union law, particu…
questions.text:
Assess the compatibility of ANY TWO of the following three (hypothetical) measures and practices with Union law, particularly (as relevant) with Articles 18 and 20-21, 34, 36, 45, 49, 56 and 63 TFEU and provide the required legal advices to the clients concerned: (i) A national law that requires all bread bakeries or those engaged in commercial break baking activities to have a ‘bread-baking’ licence and which subjects the grant of such a licence to compliance with a number of requirements including having a specified area for kneading bread, using organic flour in baking and paying an annual ‘artisanal’ levy to the ‘Artisanal Bakers Association’. Under this law, ‘FE-1 Value’ a supermarket, has been prosecuted for producing, without possessing a bread-baking licence, fresh bread from bought in frozen dough at the bakery counters of its various retail premises. Furthermore, the local ‘Artisanal Bakers Association’ has brought civil proceedings against FE-1 Value seeking back contributions with interest thereon of the ‘artisanal’ levy. Advise FE-1 Value as to whether it could have a defence based on EU law to the prosecution and to suit for the back levies, referring to relevant Treaty provisions and case law; (ii) A national law in Member State A that requires an airline, which has an operating license from Member State B for scheduled and chartered flights i.e. commercial flights in non-scheduled traffic to obtain a discrete authorisation for each onward charter flight from third (i.e. non-member) countries into the territory of Member State A in  circumstances where airlines authorised in Member State A for scheduled and charter flights are not subject to any further requirements with regards to such charter flights into Member State A from third countries. FE1 Jet Management has been fined by the Transport Regulator of Member State A for operating charter flights from third countries without authorisations for the flights in question. Advise FE1 Jet Management whether, based on EU law, it could challenge before the courts of Member State A the imposition of the fines and seek an injunction against future application of the requirement to obtain flight authorisations. (iii) John and Mary are residents of Member State C with a portfolio of investments in investment funds that are established both in that Member State and in other Member States, Member State C’s ‘Law on Investment Tax’ imposes ‘transparency’ requirements on all resident and non-resident investment funds requiring them to communicate to their shareholders in the official language of Member State C, various items of information  prescribed by the law and to publish the information concerned in that State’s electronic bulletin for official announcements, accompanied by a certificate issued by a tax professional authorised by the Law to provide tax advisory services confirming that the information was established in accordance with the Law. Failure by funds to comply with these ‘transparency’ requirements results in investors resident in Member State C being 
taxed each year on the basis of receiving a dividend deemed to be worth 25% of the total value of their investment holding, instead of on the basis of the actual dividends received by them. John and Mart consider that, although the flat rate tax mechanism under the Law applies equally to ‘non-transparent’ resident and non-resident investment funds, it leads to indirect discrimination against non-resident funds. This is because, although resident funds generally meet the ‘transparency’ requirements of the law, this is not the case regarding non-resident funds. Advise John and Mary of the possible basis in EU law upon which the 
requirements of the Law could be challenged.

questions.description: (A) As an expert in EU private international law, you have been requested to advise the following clients: (i) Gertrude …
questions.text:
(A) As an expert in EU private international law, you have been requested to advise the following clients: (i) Gertrude is a professional photographer domiciled in Member State A, specialising in photographic works depicting the buildings of certain Austrian architects, including Georg. With permission from Gertrude, Georg used some of Gertrude’s photographs in order to illustrate his buildings at a conference organised in Member State B by an international development construction firm, FE1 Limited which has its seat in Member State C. Following the conference, and without permission from Gertrude and without providing a statement of Authorship, FE1 Limited made the photographs used by Georg at the conference available on its website for viewing and downloading. Taking the view that her copyright had been infringed by FE1 Limited, Gertrude has brought an action for damages against FE1 Ltd before the Commercial Court of Member State A. FE1 Ltd has asked you for advice as to whether it would have any basis in Council Regulation No 44/2001 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial matters for contesting the jurisdiction of the courts of Member State A and, if not, as to what if any, might be the limits of the damage for which compensation could be sought in those courts by Gertrude. (ii) Blackhall Air in Member State D which is an authorised airline having its seat in Member State D, considers itself to have been a victim of a cartel between two public-owned airport undertakings (Dire and Dastardly) in Member State E, both of whom conspired to fix airport charges at the several airports in Member State E used by Blackhall Air. The existence of the cartel has been established by a formal EC decision which both Dire and Dastardly challenged unsuccessfully before the Union judicature. Blackhall Air has sought your advice as to whether (a) it could rely upon Regulation 44/2001 to bring an action for damages for compensation for breach of Article 101 TFEU before the courts of Member State D, notwithstanding that Dire and Dastardly acted under public law conferred powers in Member State E in fixing the relevant airport charges and (b) if it successfully convinced the courts of Member State D to take jurisdiction and grant it judgment, the enforcement of that judgment in Member State E against the assets of Dire and Dastardly could be resisted on 
public policy grounds. OR (B) Recital 12 in the preamble to Council Regulation (EC) 2201/2003 PF 27 November 2003 on the recognition and enforcement of judgments in matrimonial matters and matters of parental responsibility states: The grounds of jurisdiction in matters of parental responsibility established in the present Regulation are shaped in the light of the best interests of the child, in particular on the criterion of proximity...’ By reference to relevant case law interpreting Regulation 2201/2003, assess how effect has been given in practice to the ‘best interest of the child’ particularly in alleged child abduction or wrongful retention cases.

questions.description: Answer ONE only of the following three parts. Do not attempt more than one part. (A) All electrical power stations opera…
questions.text: Answer ONE only of the following three parts. Do not attempt more than one part. (A) All electrical power stations operating on coal in Member State F belonged to the national electricity company, FE. A competing electricity generator has complained to the commission regarding the favourable position conferred by law in Member State F on FE. The Commission identified two relevant markets – the first being the supply of lignite and the second being the wholesale electricity market, which concerns the generation and supply of electricity and power stations and the importation of electricity by means of interconnection systems. The Commission has found in a formal decision that FE holds a dominant position on both of those markets. Furthermore, FE was granted exploitation rights for 91% of public deposit of coal in Member State F for which rights were granted.
