questions.text: Write a note on any three of the following eight judgments, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how these issues where addressed by the Court of Justice: Case 26/62 Van Gend en Loos [1963] ECR 1; Case C-376/98 Germany v. European Parliament and Council (Tobacco Advertising Directive) [2000] ECR I-8419; Case C-224/01 Kobler [2003] ECR I-10239 Case C-341/05 Laval un Partneri [2007] ECR I-11767; Joined Cases C-402/06 P and C-415/05 P Kadi and Al Barakaat International Foundation v. Council [2008] ECR I-6351; C-236/09 Association belge des Consommateurs Test-Achats and Others v. Council [2011] ECR I-nyr (Judgment of 1 March 2011); Case C-34/09 Zambrano [2011] ECR I-nyr (Judgment of 8 March 2011); Joined Cases C-106/09 P and C-107/09 P Commission v. Government of Gibraltor and United Kingdom [2011] ECR I-nyr (Judgment of 15 November 2011).

questions.description: Tomina is a surgeon of Italian nationality now working and resident in Rome who did a one-year post-graduate internship …
questions.text: Tomina is a surgeon of Italian nationality now working and resident in Rome who did a one-year post-graduate internship in surgery in an Irish hospital in 2003 working on average 60 hours per week during which she received no remuneration.  She has just discovered that under a hypothetical 1992 directive on the acquisition of the title of medical specialist she should have been paid a “reasonable remuneration” and should not have been required to work on average more than a 48-hour week.  The Directive should have been transposed into Irish law by no later than 31 December 1999.  It has, however, not yet been implemented into Irish law. Tomina has sought your advice as whether she could bring an action in Ireland, relying upon the relevant provisions of the directive, against either the hospital concerned and/or Ireland for unpaid wages and/or damages for being forced to work an average 60-hour week, notwithstanding the provisions of the Statute of Limitations prescribing a general six- year limitation period in Irish Law. Does the fact that Italian law would provide a ten-year limitation period make any difference?

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of any three of the following four (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with Union law, …
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of any three of the following four (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with Union law, particularly free movement law: A statutory prohibition on the towing of trailers by mopeds and motorcycles, unless designed specifically for mopeds and motorcycles. No trailers of any sort are manufactured in Ireland. The Government argues the measure does not hinder the free movement of trailers as they may be used for other purposes and that the prohibition is necessary to ensure road safety. It however means that trailers designed specifically for mopeds and motorcycles require modification before being capable of being towed by other vehicles; A decision of the Minister for Justice and Equality to refuse an application for a permanent right of residence in Ireland by Tomasz, a Polish national who came to Ireland in 2006 and worked in Dublin for three years before being made redundant in 2009, but who has since been on jobseeker’s allowance; A copy Amendment Act that prohibits the use of satellite decoders purchased or acquired outside of the state by , or on behalf of, undertakings active in the hospitality industry, including public houses, hotels and restaurants, in order to screen showings of live sporting events taking place outside the State; A Finance Act provision that allows natural persons resident in Ireland and who invest in companies established in Ireland to claim for tax purposes an exemption from  income tax on dividends distributed by such companies in the form of shares in their subsidiaries up to a maximum share value of €5,000 in any given tax year.

questions.description: Answer part (A) or (B). Do not attempt both parts. As a specialists in Union anti-discrimination law, you have been aske…
questions.text: Answer part (A) or (B). Do not attempt both parts. As a specialists in Union anti-discrimination law, you have been asked to advise regarding the following two client’s situations: Joan has worked as a manager for a health authority for the past five years. Previously, following her graduation from university, she worked in the private sector for several years before becoming a homemaker for a period of six years. She has discovered that a number of male colleagues, who hold similar managerial positions to Joan for between two and four years, are all paid more than her. In response to Joan’s complaint, the health authority argues that the differences in pay are justified by the male comparator’s longer service records with the authority, albeit mostly at lower grades than Joan. Advise Joan as to the lawfulness of the authority’s use of the criterion of length of service as a determinant of pay under Union sex discrimination law; Mary is a waitress who has undergone in vitro fertilisation treatment. As a result of an incident which occurred during this treatment she became unwell and was certified as unfit for work by her general practitioner for a week. Upon her return to work, she was dismissed by her employer. However, by the time of that return to work in vitro fertilisation of her ova with her partner’s sperm cells had already taken place, although the fertilised ova were not transferred into Mary’s uterus until several days later Advise Mary as to whether she would have a right under Union equal treatment directives to challenge the fairness of her dismissal. Or Analyse by reference to relevant case-law how the expression “the place where the harmful event occurred or may occur” in Article 5 (3) of the ‘Brussels Regulation’ (Council Regulation (EC) No 44/2001 of 22 December 2000 on jurisdiction and the recognition and enforcement of judgments in civil and commercial matters) has been interpreted by the Court of Justice.

questions.description: Big Ship plc is a maritime undertaking that operates various passenger and freight services between Ireland and the Unit…
questions.text: Big Ship plc is a maritime undertaking that operates various passenger and freight services between Ireland and the United Kingdom and between the United Kingdom and a number of other Union Member States. It also owns several of the ports it uses, including the post of Holyhead in Wales. Having invested heavily in a new computerised reservation system for its passenger services, Big Ship has decided progressively to change its sailing timetables on the route from the port of Dublin to the port of Holyhead to facilitate the sale of ‘landlink’ tickets to Irish-resident customers desirous of travelling by car to the European mainland via Great Britain, on the one hand , and to customers resident in mainland Europe wishing to travel by car to Ireland via Great Britain on the other. Solicitors acting on behalf of Long Beag Teo., a privately owned Irish ferry-operator that competes with the Big Ship on the Dublin to Holyhead route and purchases mooring and docking services from Big Ship at the port of Holyhead, have written to the latter’s Irish solicitors complaining that the new timetable operated by Big Ship for its own services to and from the port of Holyhead makes it difficult , particularly at times of higher tide, for Long Beag to load and unload, there certain of its mixed passenger and freight vessels. Long Beag is threatening to complain to the Commission that Big Ship is breaching Union competition law and /or to bring legal proceedings in Ireland against Big Ship alleging such breaches. Long Beag has also recently launched new low-cost, freight-only service from Dublin port to Rotterdam in the Netherlands.  Big Ship wishes to increase the frequency of its existing freight services from Dublin to Holyhead and from Harwich to Rotterdam to avoid losing market share to the new Long Beag service. However, it is fearful that this could breach Union completion rules as, in addition to owning Holyhead port, it holds approximately 60% of the market on the Dublin to Holyhead rout (but less than 50% of the market for freight traffic) and 40% overall if the market is defined as routes from Ireland to Great Britain.    Moreover, it holds approximately a 30% market share on the Harwich to Rotterdam freight route, where it competes with several other carriers.  No other carriers are currently offering a direct freight service from Dublin to Rotterdam. As an expert in Union competition law, Big Ship’s Irish solicitors have asked you to draft an opinion for their client setting out for Big Ship, firstly, the types of action and/or complaint based on Union competition law that Long Beag could take against it , either in the Irish courts and / or with the Commission and, secondly, the likelihood of Long Beag’s succeeding in any legal action taken and/or of the Commission’s acting upon any complaint made and the likely nature of such possible action by the Commission. Your opinion should analyse the relevant markets and potential breaches of competition law that could be involved if Big Ship: (a) proceeds to introduce its proposed new timetable on the Dublin to Holyhead route; (b) increases the frequency of its existing freight services from Dublin to Holyhead and from Harwich to Rotterdam. Your opinion should also suggest any relevant less risky (from a Union competition law perspective) alternative business strategies that Big Ship might wish to consider adopting.

questions.description: Answer Part (a) or Part (b). Do not attempt both parts. “The limits of Union competence are governed by the principle of…
questions.text: Answer Part (a) or Part (b). Do not attempt both parts. “The limits of Union competence are governed by the principle of conferral.  The use of the Union competences is governed by the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality” (Article 5 TEU (formerly Article 5 EC)). Explain the principle of conferral. Assess, by reference in particular to relevant case-law of the Union judicature, whether the principles of subsidiary and proportionality have, since their formal introduction by the Treaty of Maastricht in 1993, acted as an effective constraint on the use by the Union legislature of its law-making competence under the TFEU.  Evaluate what impact the changes introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon, in particular by the ‘Protocol on the Application of the Principles of Subsidiarity and Proportionality’, have had, if any, in this regard. Or Article 15 TFEU (formerly Article 255(1) EC), which was originally inserted by the Treaty of Amsterdam with effect from 1st May 1999, provides that: “[A]ny citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, shall have a right of access to European Parliament, Council and Commission documents, subject to the principles and the conditions to be defined in accordance with this paragraph”. Analyse the extent to which this Treaty provision, EU legislation and the case-law of the Union judicature guarantees for Union citizens an effective right of public access to documents held by the institutions of the Union?

questions.description: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. Describe briefly the most significant changes to the competences of …
questions.text: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. Describe briefly the most significant changes to the competences of the European Community and the powers of its institutions that were introduced by the Treaty on European Union (Maastricht Treaty) of 1991.  Describe briefly the principal further significant changes that were introduced by the Treaty of Amsterdam of 1997.  What, in your opinion, were the most significant changes to the legal order and competences of the European Union introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon of 2007? Or Having regard in particular to the case-law of the High and Supreme Courts interpreting the notion of “necessitated by obligations of membership” of what is now the European Union (and the European Atomic Energy Community) under Article 28.4.6 of the Constitution of Ireland, analyse critically the constitutional limits to transposing and implementing Union legislation, in particular directives, into Irish law by statutory instrument pursuant to the European Communities Act 1972 to 2009.

questions.description: Discuss by reference to relevant case-law the interpretation by the Court of Justice of the notions of a “court or tribu…
questions.text: Discuss by reference to relevant case-law the interpretation by the Court of Justice of the notions of a “court or tribunal” and of “a court or tribunal against whose decisions there is no judicial remedy in national law” for the purposes of Article 267 TFEU.  Your answer should also ‘explain what bodies in Ireland, apart from the courts established under the Constitution and by law, could fall within the scope of this first notion, and what courts, apart from the Supreme Court, could  fall within the second notion. In what circumstances may courts “against whose decisions there are no judicial remedy in national law” decline, in conformity with the third subparagraph of Article 267 TFEU, to make a preliminary reference when a matter of EU law arises before them?

questions.description: Write a note on any three of the following cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for …
questions.text: Write a note on any three of the following cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Court of Justice: Case 26/62 Van Gend en Loos [1963] ECR 1; Case 22/70 Commission v. Council (“ERTA”)[1971] ECR 263; Case C-91/92 Faccini Dori [1994] ECR 1337; Cases C-46/93 and C-48/93 Brasserie du Pecheur v. Germany & Factortame III [1996] ECR 1-1029; Case C-112/00 Schmidbereger v. Austria [2003] ECR I-5669 ; Case C-167/02 P Rothley & Ors v. European Parliament [2004] ECR I-3149; Case C-341/05 Laval un Partneri [2007] ECR I-11767; Joined Cases C-402/06 P and C-415/05 P Kadi and Al Barakaat International Foundation v. Council [2008] ECR I-6351; C-236/09 Association belge des Consommateurs Test-Achats & Others V. Conseil des ministres [2011] ECR I-nyr (Judgment of 1 March 2011).

questions.description: On 1 February 2008, West County Council (“WCC”), a local authority in the (fictitious) EU Member State of Boomtonia, gra…
questions.text: On 1 February 2008, West County Council (“WCC”), a local authority in the (fictitious) EU Member State of Boomtonia, granted Windy Ltd ‘special planning permission’ (‘SPP’) for construction of a windfarm in West County.  West County Council did not carry out an ‘environmental detriment assessment’ (‘EDA’) in respect of the application, as none was required under the planning law of the Boomtonia.  On 1 July 2008, the ECJ decided in Commission v Boomtonia that the Boomtonian planning practice of granting SPP’s without requiring EDA’s to be carried out was incompatible with the (fictitious) EU EDA Directive. The Green Alliance, a group of local residents opposed to the development, wish to have the national court quash WCC’s grant of SPP.  In addition, Mr Farmer would like compensation for the reduction in the value of his property that has been occasioned by the grant of the SPP for the construction of the windfarm on lands adjacent to Mr. Farmer’s farm. Advise the Green Alliance as to whether and, if so, in what circumstances, it may: rely on the EDA Directive against WCC in order to have the grant of SPP quashed; rely on the EDA Directive against Windy Ltd to stop the development. Advise Mr Farmer as to whether he is: entitled to damages from Boomtonia for its breach of EU law; entitled to damages from Windy Ltd under EU law

questions.description: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). John, an Irish citizen, has been accepted onto a course of study at a Belgian universi…
questions.text: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). John, an Irish citizen, has been accepted onto a course of study at a Belgian university.  The Belgian authorities refuse his application for the student maintenance grant provided for Belgian students, on the basis that he has not been resident in Belgium for 5 years before commencing his course of study.  Belgium argues that its refusal is permitted under Article 24 of Directive 2005/38/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.  John seeks your advice as to whether the refusal is lawful under EU law. And Mairead is an Irish national, who lived and worked in the United Kingdom for several years before returning to Ireland last year, where after she met and married Hanam, a failed asylum seeker from Afghanistan.  The Minister for Justice has recently served a notice of intention to deport on Hanam.  Mairead has asked for advice as to whether Hanam has an EU-law based right to reside in Ireland as her spouse.  Ireland argues that Directive 2004/38/EC does not apply as Mairead is an Irish citizen resident in Ireland. And Heinz is an Austrian national who has recently moved to Ireland in search of employment. He applies for a job as a senior IT manager in the Department of Foreign Affairs, but is refused an interview on the basis that the post is reserved for Irish nationals. Ireland argues that EU law permits it to reserve some public service posts to nationals. Advise Heinz on whether this is so, and, if so, whether the exemption may be applied to the post in question. Set out your advice to John, Mairead and Heinz.

questions.description: Compare and contrast (i) the scope: (ii) definition of discrimination; and (iii) the availability of grounds of possible…
questions.text: Compare and contrast (i) the scope: (ii) definition of discrimination; and (iii) the availability of grounds of possible justification, as regards the prohibitions in EU law of discrimination on the grounds of: (i) sex; and (ii) age; and (iii) racial or ethnic origin.  In your answer, refer to relevant Treaty provisions, directives and case-law of the Union judicature.

questions.description: Answer Part (a), (b) or (c). Do not attempt more than one part. Assess critically the operation of the new decentralised…
questions.text: Answer Part (a), (b) or (c). Do not attempt more than one part. Assess critically the operation of the new decentralised system for the enforcement of EC competition law under Council Regulation (EC) No. 1/2003 of 16 December 2002 on the implementation of the rules of competition laid down in Articles 101 and 102 TFEU, which has been in force since 1 May 2004. Or Discuss the approach of (i) the European Commission and (ii) the Union judicature, when interpreting and applying Article 101 TFEU, to the analysis of provisions restrictive or allegedly restrictive of competition in agreements between undertaking operating at different levels in a market, i.e. to what are known as vertical restraints.  Has a ‘rule of reason’ been applied and are considerations that are unrelated to competition taken into account? Or “[...] Price competition is the essence of free and open competition which is the objective of Community policy to establish on the internal market.  It favours more efficient firms and it is for the benefit of consumers both in the short and long run.  Dominant firms not only have the right but should be encouraged to compete on price” (Opinion of Advocate General Fennelly in Case C-395/96 P Compagnie Maritime Belge v. Commission [2000] ECR I-1365). Discuss this view with particular reference to the case-law of the Union judicature relating to the constraints placed by Article 102 TFEU on the pricing activities, such as regards predatory pricing, price squeezing and excessive prices, of dominant firms.

questions.description: Answer part (A) or part (B).  Do not attempt both parts. Analyse and explain the contribution made over the years by the…
questions.text: Answer part (A) or part (B).  Do not attempt both parts. Analyse and explain the contribution made over the years by the Union judicature to the development of a doctrine of fundamental rights protection in the Union legal order. Or Critically assess the seminal judgment of the Court of Justice in Joined Cases C-402/06 P and C-415/05 P kadi and AI Barakaat International Foundation v Council {2008} ECR I-6351. Has the Court effectively disregarded the primacy of international law over domestic law in striving to assert the autonomy of Union law and to safeguard rights as protected under Union law?

questions.description: Answer part (A) or part (B).  Do not attempt both parts. Having particular regard to Articles 289 to 291 TFEU and releva…
questions.text: Answer part (A) or part (B).  Do not attempt both parts. Having particular regard to Articles 289 to 291 TFEU and relevant case-law, critically assess the role of the Council, the Commission and the European Parliament in the “ordinary legislative procedure”, as well as the role of the Commission in the adoption of “delegated” and “implementing” acts. Based on your assessment, would you say that there is a discernible separation of legislative and executive powers in the EU system? Or The Commission adopted in April 2011 a (fictitious) “green paper” whereon it is now seeking observations on a proposed directive to harmonise certain aspects of the laws and regulations of the Member States relating to civil marriages. The objective of the draft directive would be: “to facilitate the functioning of the internal market by harmonising those aspects of diverging national marriage laws most likely to affect the unhindered free movement of persons within the internal market”. The directive would concentrate on the formal requirements for contracting a valid civil marriage by proposing a minimum age of consent of 18 years for heterosexual couples and 21 for homosexual couples. However, it would also require the following substantive rights, which are currently only granted in some Member States, to be guaranteed by all Member States: (ii) community of property rights between spouses in respect of movable and immovable (real and personal) property acquired by either spouse after the marriage. The proposed legal basis for the draft is Article 114 TFEU. In the alternative, the commission proposes Article 352 TFEU as the legal basis. As an expert in EU law you have been asked by the Attorney General to consider whether the Union legislature would have competence to adopt such a directive on the bases proposed by the Commission or at all. By reference, in particular, to relevant Court of Justice case-law concerning the scope of articles 114 and 352 TFEU, prepare a legal opinion setting out your advice.

questions.description: Solidarity is a trade union representing mostly lower paid public sector workers. It seeks advice on behalf of a number …
questions.text: Solidarity is a trade union representing mostly lower paid public sector workers. It seeks advice on behalf of a number of its members who are non-established civil servants (hereinafter “the members”) and who, between 30 September 2003 and 30 September 2011, were given a number of successive fixed term contracts of varying durations by several Government departments, under which their terms and conditions as regards pay and pensions in particular were less favourable than those of established civil servants doing comparable work. Under Article 4 of a 1999 EC Directive, discriminatory treatment regarding employment conditions of fixed-term contracts by comparison with permanent employees is prohibited “unless the difference in treatment can be justified on objective grounds”. Article 5 of the same Directive, and with the aim of preventing abuse, required Member States to introduce “one or more of the following measures”. (i)  an  obligation for employers to offer fixed-term employees a permanent position after three years unless there are “objective reasons justifying the renewal” of the relevant fixed-term employment contract; (ii) a maximum total duration of successive fixed-term employment contracts; (iii) a limit on the number of permissible renewals of such contracts. The Directive should have been implemented by 30 September 2001. Ireland, however, only implemented the Directive by a 2009 Act of the Oireachtas, the commencement of which was postponed until 30 September 2011. Advise Solidarity, by reference to relevant EU law principles and case-law, whether the members: may rely directly on Article 4 of the 1999 Directive in challenging their less favourable employment conditions; may rely on Article 5 to challenge the successive renewal of their contracts between 30 September 2001 and 30 September 2011; and are obliged to introduce, so as to pursue the claims referred to in points (i) and (ii) above, individual legal proceedings before the ordinary Irish courts, in circumstances where Solidarity could bring a representative action on their behalf before the labour court pursuant to the 2009 Act (which permits such representative cases and which provides that, if unsuccessful in such representative actions, trade unions will not be exposed to an order for costs in favour of the defendant employer) if the claims being pursued were based on the rights conferred by the 2009 Act of Irish law more generally rather than on the Directive.

questions.description: Write a note on any three of the following seven cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that aros…
questions.text: Write a note on any three of the following seven cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by EU courts: Case 26/62 Van Gend en Loos (1963) ECR 1; Case 152/84 Marshall v. Southampton Area Health Authority (No.1)(1986) ECR 723; Case C-50/00 P Union de Pequenos Agricultores (UPA) V. Council (2002) ECR I-6677; Case C-200/02 Zhu & Chen (2004) ECR I-9925; Case T-201/04 Microsoft v. Commission (2007) ECR II-3601; Case C-118/08 Transportes Urbanos y Servicios Generales v. Aministration del Estado (2010) ECR I-635; Case C-550/07 P Akzo Nobel Chemicals v. Commission (2010) ECR I-NYR  (Judgment of 14 September 2010)

questions.description: Consider and advise in both of the following situations: Mary is a waitress working for a restaurant on a 6-month tempor…
questions.text: Consider and advise in both of the following situations: Mary is a waitress working for a restaurant on a 6-month temporary contract of employment. Just as her contract is due to expire; she discovers that she is pregnant. On informing her employer, it decides not to renew her contract on its expiry. As Mary would be taking maternity leave in the following 6 months, the employer considers that she be unable to fulfil the terms of a new contract. Advise Mary as to whether the refusal to renew her contract under these circumstances is contrary to EU sex discrimination law. And Joan has worked as a manager for a health authority for the past five years. Previously, following her graduation from university, she worked in the private sector for several years before becoming a homemaker for a period of six years. She has discovered that a number of male colleagues, who hold similar managerial positions to hers, but for periods of between two and four years, are paid more than her. In response to Joan’s complaint, the health authority argues that the differences in pay are justified by the male comparators’ longer service records with the authority, notwithstanding that the service was mostly at lower non-managerial grades. Furthermore, Joan is informed that her previous private sector work experience cannot be taken into consideration because of her six-year career break. However, career breaks of up to three years do not preclude previous experience being taken into account. Two of her male colleagues, whose previous work experience has been taken into account, took three year career breaks to “travel around the world”. Advise Joan as to the lawfulness of the authority’s use of the criterion of length of service as a determinant of pay under EU sex discrimination law, in particular Article 157 TFEU, and its refusal, based on its three-year rule, to consider her previous work experience.

questions.description: Consider and advise in each of the following situations: A Swedish trade union is boycotting the provision of building s…
questions.text: Consider and advise in each of the following situations: A Swedish trade union is boycotting the provision of building services by FE-1, an Estonian building contractor, because FE-1 is paying its workers less than the minimum wage agreed in the collective agreement that binds Swedish employers and trade unions in the building industry. Swedish labour law permits trade unions to engage in boycotting, which is seen as a legitimate form of collective action, in order to protect the collectively agreed Swedish minimum wage, whenever an employer, whether Swedish or foreign, has not accepted the relevant collective agreement. FE-1 is paying its workers posted to Sweden marginally over the minimum hourly rate for building workers in Sweden in just under half of the relevant collectively agreed Swedish rate. Advise FE-1 as to whether it could challenge the collective boycott in Sweden based on EU law. Helmut resides in Hamburg, Germany, but holidays regularly in Austria. Helmut is severely disabled and cannot travel on his own by public transport and so, as a single person, normally uses a specially adapted car for travelling. On being subject to a random control in his car on a tolled section of an Austrian motorway, Helmut is found not to be displaying a toll disc for the motorway, which, under Austrian law must be purchased by all motorway users prior to using such tolled sections. Toll discs are issued free of charge to disabled persons who are resident or ordinarily resident in Austria. Helmut has been fined for not displaying the disc and to appeal. Advise him as to his chances of success under EU law. Mary is a dual British and Irish national, who has lived all her life in the United Kingdom. She marries Bob, a Jamaican national, whilst on holiday in Jamaica. Bob is refused a right of residence in the UK under UK immigration law. Advise Mary and Bob on whether EU law affords them a right of residence in the UK.

questions.description: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. Assess critically the interpretation, in the case-law of the Uni…
questions.text: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. Assess critically the interpretation, in the case-law of the Union judicature, of the following important concepts under Article 101 TFEU: “agreements”, “undertakings”, “concerted practices”. What in particular, are the critical distinguishing factors between anti-competitive “agreement(s) between undertakings” and “concerted practices” involving undertakings? How has the Union judicature interpreted the requirement that such agreements or practices must “affect trade between Member States” EU competition law will apply? Or By reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature concerning Article 102 TFEU, analyse the extent to which a dominant undertaking may take steps to protect its own commercial position in the market on which it is dominant or on a related market if its position is threatened by a rival and/or new entrant. Could such steps or protective behaviour be acceptable if its purpose is specifically to strengthen or extend the dominant market position of the dominant undertaking in question? And Indicate, giving reasons for your answer, whether your advices would be different if John’s will was invalid and he therefore died intestate. Note that John’s parents have predeceased him and his three brothers are his next of kin. 8 marks

questions.description: Mary died recently at the age of 92. She lived the final years of her life in a state of some confusion and fear for her…
questions.text: Mary died recently at the age of 92. She lived the final years of her life in a state of some confusion and fear for her personal safety. Her house is located in a quiet rural spot and Mary was often petrified by news reports of attacks on elderly persons in circumstances similar to her own. Thus, she was very relieved when Betty, a young neighbour, began to visit her on a regular basis. Betty began to take charge of the day to day running of Mary’s life and, having gained Mary’s confidence, suggested to her that she should transfer the house to Betty. Betty argued that if she did this Mary would acquire peace of mind and freedom from constant worry about intruders. Before making any decision, Mary spoke to her solicitor who advised strongly against the transfer to Betty. Despite this advice, Mary, being timid in nature and not wishing to offend Betty or lose her companionship, transferred the property into Betty’s name. Mary’s nephew, Conor, to whom Mary left all of her property by will, now wishes to challenge the transfer to Betty and seeks your opinion on the matter.

questions.description: Answer parts (a), (b) and (c). (a)  Write a brief summary of the comparison and legislative powers of each of the follow…
questions.text: Answer parts (a), (b) and (c). (a)  Write a brief summary of the comparison and legislative powers of each of the following Union institutions: (i)  the European Parliament; (ii)  the European Council; (iii) the Council; and (iv)  the European Commission. (b)  In light of your answer in (a), explain whether there is separation of legislative and executive powers in the EU? (c)  Outline the role of national parliaments in the EU legislative process, as provided for in Article 12 TEU and Protocol Nos. 1 and 2 to the Treaty of Lisbon.  What is the rationale for according national parliaments this role?

questions.description: Answer parts (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)  “The position of directives is very complicated” (Craig & De B…
questions.text: Answer parts (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)  “The position of directives is very complicated” (Craig & De Burca, EU law, 4th ed. (2008) at p. 303) Explain the sources of this complexity in the case-law on the direct effect of directives.  Should directives be accorded horizontal direct effect? Or (b)  On 1 February 2010, a planning authority in the (fictitious) Member State of Boomtonia, granted Windy Ltd ‘special planning permission’ (‘SPP’) for construction of a windfarm.  No ‘environmental detriment assessment’ (‘EDA’) was carried out in respect of Windy’s application, as none was required under the planning law of the Boomtonia.  On 1 July 2010, the Court of Justice decided in the (fictitious) case of Commission v Boomtonia that the Boomtonian planning practice of granting a SPP without an EDA was contrary to the (fictitious) EU Assessment of Environmental Damage Directive, the deadline for the transposition of which into national law was 1 February 2009.  Considering its planning permission to be fully valid as a matter of national law, Windy Ltd is still proposing to build the wind farm. The Green Alliance, a group of local residents opposed to the development, wishes to have the planning authority’s decision annulled by the national courts.  Moreover, Mr Farmer, a local farmer, would like compensation for the probable reduction in the value of his farm that would be occasioned by the construction of the windfarm, which there is evidence to suggest could render it prone to landslides in times of heavy rainfall. Advise the Green Alliance whether it could rely on: (i)  the EDA Directive against the planning authority in judicial review proceedings in 	Boomtonia seeking to have grant of SPP quashed; (ii)  the EDA Directive in injunctive proceedings against Windy Ltd seeking to stop the 	development. Advise Mr Farmer whether, if the wind farm is built, he could obtain, in legal proceedings brought in Boomtonia alleging breach of EU law: (iii)  damages against Boomtonia; (iv)  damages against Windy Ltd.

questions.description: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)  “One of the novel features of the Community legal order is the …
questions.text: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)  “One of the novel features of the Community legal order is the power given to the Commission to supervise compliance by the Member States with their obligations under the Treaties.” (Audretsch, Supervision in European Community Law 2nd ed, 1986) Appraise the procedures under Articles 258 to 260 TFEU (formerly Articles 226 to 228 EC) by reference to relevant Court of Justice case-law and from the perspective of their contribution to securing the Member States’ compliance with their obligations under EU law.  From this perspective, evaluate the changes introduced by successive Treaty reforms, including the Treaty of Lisbon. Or (b)  Analyse by reference to relevant case-law the interpretation by the Union judicature of the standing requirements of “direct and individual concern” under the fourth paragraph of Article 263 TFEU (formerly Article 230 EC).  Do this standing requirement and the Lisbon Treaty reform thereto ensure effective judicial protection?

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with EU law and in particu…
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with EU law and in particular with Articles 28 and 30 TFEU, Articles 34 and 36 TFEU, Article 49 TFEU and Article 10 TFEU (formerly Articles 23 and 25 EC, Articles 28 and 30 EC, Article 43 EC and Article 90 EC): (i)  A tax by Mayo County Council on the transportation of natural gas extracted off the Mayo coast and processed within the Council’s functional area out of that area, with the result that only gas extracted and used in Mayo in that area is not subject to the charge.  Mayo County Council says it is justified because local gas uses pay rates, other local charges and /or have to tolerate the location of a massive gas refinery in the country; (ii)  A provision in the Corrective Lenses Act which authorises the sale of contract lenses solely in shops which specialise in the supply of medical devices, such as opticians.  The Government says that the measure is necessary in the interest of protecting human health to ensure that potential purchasers of contact lenses get proper advice regarding the desirability of having their eyes tested first and their suitability for wearing contact lenses; (iii)  A provision in the Pharmacy Acts which prohibits the owning, operation or control of pharmacies by persons not having the status of pharmacist and limiting the number of pharmacies that any individual pharmacist may own, operate or control to a maximum of three, all of which must be located within a 50-kilometre radius of each other.  The Government says that these measures are necessary to protect consumers by rendering individual pharmacists fully responsible for products sold in chemists and reducing the likelihood that the maximisation of profit margins might overly influence their advice to consumers.

questions.description: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). (i)  Mary is a dual British and Irish national, who has lived all her life in the Unit…
questions.text: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). (i)  Mary is a dual British and Irish national, who has lived all her life in the United Kingdom.  She marries Bob, a Jamaican national, whilst on holiday in Jamaica.  Bob is refused a right of residence in the UK under UK immigration law.  Advise Mary and Bon on whether EU law affords them a right of residence in the UK. (ii)  Olivier is a trainee player with a French football club, who has been offered a one-year professional contract with that club.  He has also received a two-year offer from an English premiership club, which he wishes to accept.  Under a French Football Association collective agreement, which is binding in French la, trainee players must accept offers from professional contracts with the club that trained them.  if not, they must pay damages to that club equal to a minimum of one year’s salary under the contract that was offered but declined.  Advise Olivier as to whether he could rely upon EU law to resist an action for damages against him by his French club if he accepts the offer from the English club. (iii)  Jean is a French national who has been living in Ireland for several years without ever having applied for a residence permit.  He has no obvious financial means and appears to have lived from income made from occasional busking.  He does not have health insurance.  He is arrested by Gardaí on suspicion of terrorist offences, but released without charge.  The security services in Northern Ireland had passed on information linking him to terrorist groups there.  Advise the Irish Government on the compatibility with EU law of deporting Jean back to France.

questions.description: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)  By reference to the relevant case-law of the Court of Justice, …
questions.text: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)  By reference to the relevant case-law of the Court of Justice, analyse the scope of Treaty provisions on the free movement of capital and, in particular, the distinction between a restriction of that freedom and a restriction on the freedom to provide services and on the freedom of establishment.  Which of the restrictions, if any, is the broader? (b)  Under Article 67(4) TFEU “[t]he Union shall facilitate access to justice, in particular through the principle of mutual recognition of judicial and extrajudicial decisions in civil matters”. Assess by reference particularly to Council Regulation (EC) No. 44/2001 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters (“the Brussels I Regulation”) and Council Regulation (EC) 2201/2003 of 27 November 2003 on the recognition and enforcement of judgments in matrimonial matters and matters of parental responsibility (“the Brussels 2 bis Regulation”), as well as the case-law of the Court of Justice interpreting those Regulations, the extent to which the Union is achieving the objective of Article 67(4) TFEU.

questions.description: Ireland exempted from excise duty fossil fuels used for the production of alumina in the Shannon region between 1983 and…
questions.text: Ireland exempted from excise duty fossil fuels used for the production of alumina in the Shannon region between 1983 and 2005.  In 2005, the exemption was replaced with a reduced rate.  The Commission, which had expressed concerns to the Government regarding the exemption, initiated in 2006 the procedure provided for in Article 108(2) TFEU (formerly Article 88(2) EC) with regard both to the exemption and reduced rate.  In its resulting decision, the Commission has decided that both the exemption and reduced rate are new State aids that are incompatible with the common market.  However, its decision only orders Ireland to recover the difference between the reduced rat and the normal rate of excise duty from the date of introduction of the reduced rate in 2005. Advise the Irish government on whether there are grounds to challenge the Commission decision before the Union judicature.  Explain the procedure to be followed for such a challenge.  Is there a risk that the Commission decision on recovery could be challenged as being too lenient before the Union judicature and, if so, by whom?

questions.description: “Fundamental rights, as guaranteed by the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedom…
questions.text: “Fundamental rights, as guaranteed by the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms and as they result from constitutional traditions common to the Member States, shall constitute general principles of the Union’s law” (Article 6(3) TEU, as amended by the Treaty of Lisbon). (i)	Discuss the development of the scope of application and the sources of fundamental rights protection in EU law. (ii)	Is that development likely to be affected by Article 6(1) of the TEU, as amended, by 	the Treaty of Lisbon, which provides for recognition by the Union of the rights and 	freedoms set out in the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union and for 	those rights and freedoms to have “the same legal value as the Treaties”?

questions.description: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)	“The limits of Union competence are governed by the principle of…
questions.text: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)	“The limits of Union competence are governed by the principle of conferral. The use 	of Union competences is governed by the principles of subsidiarity and 	proportionality” (Article 5 TEU (formerly Article 5EC)). Assess, by reference in particular to relevant case-law and legal literature, whether the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality have, since their formal introduction by the Treaty of Maastricht in 1993, acted as an effective constraint on the adoption of legislation by the Union (formerly the European Community).  Evaluate the likely impact of the changes introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon, in particular by the ‘Protocol on the Application of the Principles of Subsidiarity and Proportionality’ in this regard. (b)	Article 15 TFEU (formerly Article 255(1) EC), which was originally inserted by the 	Treaty of Amsterdam with effect from 1st May 1999, provides that: “[A]ny citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its 	registered office in a Member State, shall have a right of access to European 	Parliament, Council and Commission documents, subject to the principles and the 	conditions to be defined in accordance with this paragraph”. Analyse the extent to which the case-law of the Union judicature guarantees for EU citizens an effective right of public access to documents held by EU institutions?

questions.description: Consider whether the Brussels Convention of 27th September 1968 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgements in Civ…
questions.text: Consider whether the Brussels Convention of 27th September 1968 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgements in Civil and Commercial Matters, as now largely replaced by Council Regulation (EC) No. 44/2001 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgements in Civil and Commercial Matters (“the Brussels I Regulation”), facilitates the progressive establishment of ‘an area of freedom, justice and security’ as envisaged by Article 67 TFEU (formerly Article 61 EC) by promoting clear jurisdictional rules for determining which courts have jurisdiction in civil and commercial matters involving inter-state elements. In your answer refer, in particular, to the scope and operation of the principal jurisdictional rule under Article 2(1) of Regulation No. 44/2001 and the special rules laid down by Article 5(1) and (3).

questions.description: Answer both parts (i) and (ii): (i)	FE-1 Transport is a haulage company that was precluded for many years under Irish VA…
questions.text: Answer both parts (i) and (ii): (i)	FE-1 Transport is a haulage company that was precluded for many years under Irish VAT legislation from claiming VAT deductions in its VAT returns in respect of inputs (costs) relating to the transport of certain goods.  The Commission later brought infringement proceedings against Ireland, wherein the Court of Justice determined that the relevant Irish VAT provisions were incompatible with the EU VAT Directive.  The Oireachtas repealed the Irish legislation six months after the Court’s judgment.  FE-1 Transport has sought reimbursement of both the total of the deductions it was not allowed make during the six months between the Court’s judgment and the repeal of the offending Irish provisions and the total of the deductions it was not allowed during the three years immediately preceding the Court’s judgment.  The Irish tax authorities are not prepared to reimburse any of the deductions.  They claim that a Member State is allowed reasonable time to comply with Court judgments and that a period of six months is reasonable.  As regards the period prior to the Court’s judgment, whilst admitting the general limitation period for claims against them under the VAT legislation is three years, the tax authorities claim that no reimbursement is payable to FE-1 Transport because the relevant provision of the VAT Directive was not directly effective and, if it was, FE-1 could have but did not either bring legal proceedings to challenge the provisions precluding the deductions or contest, in any of its two-monthly VAT returns during the three-year period in question, the non-availability of the deductions. Advise FE-1 Transport as to whether the VAT Directive could be relied upon directly by it and, if so, whether EU law would entitle it to reimbursement of the precluded deductions. (ii)	FE-2 Ferries was a private company engaged in the provision of ferry services to several islands off the west coast of Ireland.  It was in competition with Western Boats Limited for several years but went out of business due to its inability to compete with the low fares offered by Western Boats, and particularly Western Boat’s policy of only charging island residents token fares for travel to the mainland and back.  The liquidator of FE-2 discovered that Western Boats was in receipt of substantial subsidies from the Western Development Agency throughout the period during which FE-2 Ferries was in competition with it.  The liquidator brought an action for damages for breach of the EU competition and State aid rules in the High Court.  This was dismissed by the High Court, which held that there was no effect on trade between Member States and so the competition rules in Articles 101, 102 and 106 TFEU (formerly Articles 81, 82 and 86 EC) were inapplicable the High Court also considered that the subsidies were justified to facilitate travel to and from the islands by island residents, and that, accordingly, no breach of the State aid rules of Articles 107 and 108 TFEU (formerly Articles 87 and 88 EC) had occurred.  This decision was upheld on appeal by the Supreme Court, which refused a request from the liquidator for a reference to the Court of Justice.  Following the Supreme Court decision, the Commission found, following an investigation, that the subsidies constituted unlawful State aid in that they had never been notified to it and, furthermore, were incompatible with the common market.  The liquidator is now considering bringing an action for damages in the High Court against Ireland for the breach of EU law regarding both the grant of the subsidies to Western Boats and the Supreme Court decision. Advise FE-2 Ferries’ liquidator regarding the relevant EU law principles concerning damages for breach of EU law as they emerge from the case-law of the Court of Justice and, based thereon, as to the liquidator’s chances of success.

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with EU law and in particu…
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with EU law and in particular with Articles 28 and 30 TFEU, Articles 34 and 35 TFEU, Article 49 TFEU, Article 56, and Article 110 TFEU (formerly Articles 23 and 25 EC, Articles 28 and 30 EC, Article 43 EC, Article 49 EC and Article 90 EC): (i) A ‘carbon levy’ of 0.2 cent per unit of electricity supplied for consumption, save in respect of units of electricity generated from renewable energy sources within the State and supplied to the national grid in respect of which a ‘green energy promotion levy’ of 0.1 cent is applied.  The percentage of units subject to the reduced levy on each individual customer’s electricity bill is calculated solely by reference to the percentage of the total units supplied for consumption in the State in the previous year that originated from renewable energy generating sources within the State.  The proceeds from the ‘green energy promotion levy’ collected from consumers by the suppliers of electricity are remitted to the Department of Communications, Energy and Natural Resources, which uses them to fun grants for new wind farm projects within the State; (ii)  A new provision in the Pharmacy Acts which prohibits the owning, operation or control of pharmacies by persons not having the status of pharmacist and limiting the number of pharmacies that any individual pharmacist may own, operate or control to a maximum of three, all of which must be located within a 50-kilometre radius of each other.  The Government claims that these measures are necessary to protect consumers of medicaments by rendering individual pharmacists fully responsible for products sold in chemists and reducing the likelihood that the maximisation of profit margins might overly influence the advice given therein to consumers; (iii)  A law that only permits building firms registered on a trade register, which (under the law) is maintained by the Irish Chamber of Commerce on behalf of the Department of Enterprise, Trade and Employment, to be engaged for the provision of building works in the State.

questions.description: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). Note: These scenarios arise after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/EC on t…
questions.text: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). Note: These scenarios arise after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/EC on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.  Candidates should have particular regard to the potential relevance of citizenship case-law. (i)  Heinrich is an Austrian national who has recently moved to Ireland in search of employment.  He applies for a job as a senior IT manager in the Department of Foreign Affairs, but is refused an interview on the basis that the post is reserved for Irish nationals.  Ireland argues that EU law permits it to reserve some public sector posts to nationals.  Advise Heinrich on whether this is so, and whether the exemption may be applied to the post in question; (ii)  Patrick, and Irish national, went to Belgium in 2007, where he has now resided for almost three years, first at a campsite near a seaside resort and then from December 2008 in Brussels initially at a youth hostel and then in a Salvation Army hostel, where, in return for board and lodging and some pocket money, he does various jobs for about 30 hours a week as part of a personal socio-occupational reintegration programme.  He has no savings but applied for a state minimum subsistence allowance so as to be able to leave the hostel and live independently.  The Belgian social assistance authorities have refused his application as he is not a Belgian national and not a worker or self-employed.  Patrick seeks your advice as to whether the refusal is compatible with EU law; (iii)  Gheorghe is a Romanian national who travelled to France in search of work.  Several months later, he was deported from France on the basis that he was homeless and had no obvious means of supporting himself.  The deportation took place within a few days of Gheorghe’s arrest and, although he speaks only little French, he was given no opportunity to make submissions against his deportation or challenge it before a French court.  Subsequently, back in Romania, he received a letter explaining that the deportation was because of his ‘illegal residence’ in France.  He has also learned that, under pressure from the French authorities, the Romanian Minister for the Interior proposes to apply a Romanian court for an order prohibiting Gheorghe from returning to France for a period of three years.  Gheorghe seeks your advice as to whether his deportation from France to Romania was compatible with EU law and as to whether, based on EU law, he could oppose the order that may be sought against him in Romania.

questions.text: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. In so doing, refer both to relevant Treaty provisions, Directives and caselaw of the Union judicature. (b)	Assess critically the interpretation in the case-law of the Union judicature of the following important concepts under Article 101 TFEU (formerly Article 81 EC): (i)  “agreements between undertakings”; (ii)  “undertakings”; (iii)  “concerted practices”.

questions.description: Answer parts (a), (b), and (c) Assess, with reference to relevant EU Treaty provisions and case-law, whether there is a …
questions.text: Answer parts (a), (b), and (c) Assess, with reference to relevant EU Treaty provisions and case-law, whether there is a discernible separation of legislative and executive powers in the EU system. Your answer should also consider: How such separation as exists is affected by the role of the European Council under Article 15 TEU, as inserted by the Treaty of Lisbon. How such separation as exists is affected by the formalisation and expansion of the role of national parliaments in the EU legislative process in Article 12 TEU and Protocol Nos. 1 and 2 to the Treaty of Lisbon.

Article 15 TFEU (formerly Article 255(1) EC), which was originally inserted by the Treaty of Amsterdam with effect from 1st May 1999, provides that: “[A]ny citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, shall have a right of access to European Parliament, Council and Commission documents, subject to the principles and the conditions to be defined in accordance with this paragraph”. Analyse the extent to which the case-law of the Union judicature guarantees for EU citizens an effective right of public access to documents held by EU institutions?

questions.text: Answer parts (a) and (b) Analyse by reference to relevant case-law the interpretation by the Union judicature of the standing requirements of “direct and individual concern” under the fourth paragraph of what is now Article 263 TFEU (formerly Article 230 EC) for natural or legal persons. questions.description: Consider both of the following situations: COMAIR, the (hypothetical) Irish aviation regulator, calculates landing fees …
questions.text: Consider both of the following situations: COMAIR, the (hypothetical) Irish aviation regulator, calculates landing fees at Dublin airport for the period 2010 to 2013 in a manner that violates a (fictitious) EU aviation Directive. The Directive has not been transposed into Irish law, although the implementation date has passed. COMAIR grants a private company, FE-1 Air, landing slots at Dublin airport subject to acceptance of the notified landing fees. FE-1 Air fails to challenge COMAIR’s decision determining the landing fees within the one-month limitation period provided under the relevant Irish rules. Advise FE-1 Air as to whether EU law entitles it to refuse to pay the landing fees and whether it may challenge the legality of the fees under the Directive even though the one-month limitation period in Irish law has passed. FE-2 Car Parks, a private company engaged in the provision of off-street car parking services in Ireland, brought an unsuccessful High Court challenge based on a (fictitious) EU VAT Directive.  The High Court rejected FE-2’s argument that the VAT exemption enjoyed by local authorities in respect of the off-street parking they provide was incompatible with the Directive. FE-2 appealed to the Supreme Court. In the meantime, the Court of Justice of the European Union, in a case the Commission brought under Article 258 TFEU (formerly Article 226 EC) against Ireland, held that the exemption was indeed incompatible with that Directive. Advise FE-2 Car Parks regarding the import of the Court of Justice’s judgment for: (i) its appeal before the Supreme Court; and (ii) its possible entitlement to damages under EU law arising out of Ireland’s mis-implementation of the Directive and the High Court’s error of EU law.

questions.text: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with EU law and in particular with Articles 28 and 30 TFEU, Articles 34 and 36 TFEU, Article 49 TFEU, and Article 110 TFEU (formerly Articles 23 and 25 EC, Articles 28 and 30 EC, Article 43 EC and Article 90 EC): Waste regulations adopted by all of Dublin’s four local authorities imposing a charge per kilogramme on business and residential waste collected in the Dublin area and applied when it is moved for disposal and/or recycling outside of Dublin. The proceeds of the charge are used by the local authorities for general budgetary purposes. Licensed waste operators which collect in the Dublin area and which do not transport it outside for disposal and/or recycling pay no corresponding charge. The authorities’ aim is to encourage operators to dispose of their waste at a new incineration plant near the city, which is partially owned by the four local authorities; Under a ‘Confectionery Hygiene’ regulation the Minister for Health and Children has prohibited the marketing of sugar confectionery, or similar products made using sugar substitutes, without wrapping from vending machines. Such products are lawfully marketed from vending machines in the United Kingdom (including Northern Ireland) and in most other Member States; A new provision in the Pharmacy Acts which prohibits the owning, operation or control of pharmacies by persons not having the status of pharmacist and limiting the number of pharmacies that any individual pharmacist may own, operate or control to a maximum of three, all of which must be located within a 50-kilometre radius of each other. The Government claims that these measures are necessary to protect consumers of medicaments by rendering individual pharmacists fully responsible for products sold in chemists and reducing the likelihood that the maximisation of profit margins might overly influence the advice given therein to consumers.

questions.description: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). Note: These scenarios arise after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/EC on t…
questions.text: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). Note: These scenarios arise after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/EC on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States. Candidates should have particular regard to the potential relevance of citizenship case-law. Patrick, an Irish national, was arrested whilst on holiday at a night club in Spain and charged with possession of an offensive weapon. He understands that under Spanish criminal procedure trials are normally conducted in Spanish, but that an accused person has the right to opt to have his/her trial conducted in either the Catalan or Basque languages. Spanish nationals, or EU nationals resident in Spain for at least five years, may, in the further alternative, opt for a trial in English. Patrick, who is resident in London, would like his trial procedure conducted in English and seeks your advice as to whether, having regard to the Spanish criminal procedure linguistic regime, he would be entitled, under EU law, to opt for a trial in English; Michael, an Irish national, went to Belgium in 2007, where he has now resided for almost three years, first at a campsite near a seaside resort and then from December 2008 in Brussels initially at a youth hostel and then in a Salvation Army hostel, where, in return for board and lodging and some pocket money, he does various jobs for about 30 hours a week as part of a personal social-occupational reintegration programme. He has no savings but applied for a state minimum subsistence allowance so as to be able to leave the hostel and live independently. The Belgian social assistance authorities have refused his application as he is not a Belgian national and not a worker or self-employed. Michael seeks your advice as to whether the refusal is compatible with EU law; Alice is Irish, and wishes to establish a clinic providing a counselling service for pregnant women in Malta, where Alice has been working as a psychologist since 2007. In Malta, the intentional medical termination of a pregnancy is strictly prohibited, as is the dissemination of any advertisement for, or information or advice about the availability of, such medical terminations in other countries, however supplied. Alice seeks your advice as to whether she may rely on EU law to allow abortion clinics based in other Member States, where such services are legal, to advertise at her clinic, and whether she may provide, in return for a fee, information and advice regarding abortion options in other Member States.

questions.text: Answer part (A) or (B).  Do not attempt both parts. (A)	“It is axiomatic that the EC has only the powers ascribed to it, but defining those so as to impose meaningful limits on Community competence is difficult [...]  The ECJ is often case as the ‘villain’ in this regard, by construing Articles 308 and 95 very broadly.  It should be recognised that the greatest expansion of Community competence has been through successive Treaty revisions.  It has been the Member States themselves that have been most willing to accord new competences to the EC” (Craig & De Burca, EU Law, 4th ed., at p. 107) Analyse critically the above opinion by reference to the case-law of the Court of Justice on Articles 95 EC and 308 EC, in particular, and by reference to the successive amendments of the former EEC Treaty since the Single European Act. (B)	Analyse the principles of subsidiary introduced into the EC by the Treaty of Maastricht (now Article 5 EC) and as developed in the Protocol to the Treaty of Amsterdam, and assess, by reference in particular to relevant case-law and legal literature, whether it acts as an effective check on the adoption of legislation by the Community in areas where it does not have exclusive competence.
