questions.description: Consider both of the following situations: (i)	COMTEL, the Irish telecommunications regulator, calculates telecommunicat…
questions.text: Consider both of the following situations: (i)	COMTEL, the Irish telecommunications regulator, calculates telecommunications licence fees in a manner that violates an EC Directive.  The Directive has not been transposed into Irish law, although the implementation date has passed.  COMTEL grants a private company, FE-1 Telecom, a licence subject to payment of such a licence fee.  FE-1 fails to challenge COMTEL’s imposition of the licence fee within the one-month limitation period provided under the relevant Irish rules, but has not yet paid the fee. Advise FE-1 as to whether EC law entitles it to refuse to pay the fee and whether it may challenge the legality of the fee under the Directive even though the one-month limitation period in Irish law has passed.  Advise also whether it is entitled to damages under EC law arising out of Ireland’s breach of the Directive. (ii)	The National Roads Authority (‘NRA’) granted permission to build a motorway through an area of outstanding natural beauty.  John challenged this permission in the High Court, but the High Court rejected his argument that the permission violated an EC Directive.  The High Court also ordered that John pay the NRA’s legal costs, which amounted to over €300,000.  John believes that this High Court costs award violated article 10a of the Directive, which provides for access to justice in environmental cases “at reasonable cost”.  Ireland has not formally implemented Article 10a, but the Irish courts have a general discretion to award costs as they see fit. Advise John on whether he may rely on Article 10a of the Directive in his appeal to the Supreme Court and whether, on this basis, the Supreme Court must overturn the High Court costs award.  Advise John also as to whether the Supreme Court must refer the matter to the Court of Justice under Article 234 EC.  The latter has yet to interpret Article 10a.

questions.description: Write a note on any three of the following cases, setting out, inter alia, their core facts, the key issues of EU law th…
questions.text: Write a note on any three of the following cases, setting out, inter alia, their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Court of Justice/Court of First Instance: (i)	Case 22/70 Commission v Council (“ERTA”) [1971] ECR 263; (ii)	Case 106/77 Amministrazione deel Finanze dello Stato v Simmenthal [1978] ECR 629; (iii) 	Cases C-46/93 and C-48/93 Brasserie du Pecheur v Germany & Factortame III [1996] ECR I-1029; (iv)	Case C-112/00 Schmidberger v Austria [2003] ECR I-5659; (v)	Case C-176/03 Commission v Council (Validity of Framework Decision 2003/80/JHA) [2005] ECR I-7879; (vi)	Case C-127/08 Metock and Others [2008] ECR I-NYR (Judgment of 25/07/2008); (vii)	Case T-201/04 Microsoft v Commission [2007] ECR II-3601.

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with Articles 28 EC to 30 …
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with Articles 28 EC to 30 EC, 43 EC, 49 EC and Articles 56-58 EC as applicable: (i)	A statutory prohibition on the towing of trailers by mopeds and motorcycles.  The Government argues the measure does not hinder the free movement of trailers as they may be used for other purposes and that the prohibition is necessary to ensure road safety.  Trailers designed specifically for mopeds and motorcycles require modification before being capable of being towed by other vehicles; (ii)	A statutory restriction on the sale of herbal remedy for colds and flu, tincture of phantasm, without a doctor’s prescription.  The Government argues that the remedy may have side-effects on the operation of other prescription drugs, so on this basis, should only be available on prescription; (iii)	A provision that allows a natural person living in Ireland to claim an exemption from income tax on dividends distributed in the form of shares in a subsidiary by companies established in Ireland.

questions.description: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). Note: Each of the scenarios arises after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/…
questions.text: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). Note: Each of the scenarios arises after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/EC on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States. (i)	Heinz is an Austrian national who has recently moved to Ireland in search of employment.  He applies for a job as a senior IT manager in the Department of Foreign Affairs, but is refused an interview on the basis that the post is reserved or Irish nationals.  Ireland argues that EC law permits it to reserve some public service posts to nationals.  Advise Heinz on whether this is so, and whether the exemption may be applied to the post in question; (ii) 	Jacqueline is a German national who moved to the Netherlands to study to become a special needs teacher on a four-year course accredited by an Amsterdam University and involving both periods of academic study and practical in-school training.  Having initially been awarded a maintenance grant on account of being classified as an EU migrant worker, this was later withdrawn when, during her final year, she devoted herself full-time to study.  The relevant awarding authority justified its decision on the basis that she could not, in fact, be regarded as a worker for the purpose of the grant having come to the Netherlands principally for the purpose of student and, furthermore, had not been resident in the Netherlands for five years prior to first applying for the grant, a prerequisite to being regarded as sufficiently integrated into Dutch society to be treated the same as a student of Dutch nationality.  Advise Jacqueline on the legality under EC law of the withdrawal of her maintenance grant. (iii)	Dominique, a French national, worked as a computer programmer in Ireland for seven years before being arrested and charged with possession of cannabis for the purpose of supply.  She was later convicted of the lesser criminal offence of possession of the drug and sentenced to three years imprisonment and to expulsion and exclusion from Ireland upon her release for a period of five years.  She has a five-year old child who is an Irish national and has commenced primary school in Dublin.  Whilst in prison, she married Michel, an old school flame of Algerian nationality who came over to support her during her trial.  Advise Dominique, who is due soon for release, on the legality of the expulsion order under EC law and of whether Michel is entitled under EC law to reside with her in Ireland as her spouse.

questions.description: Answer part (A) or (B).  Do not attempt both parts. (A)	Assess, by reference to the relevant Treaty provisions, legislat…
questions.text: Answer part (A) or (B).  Do not attempt both parts. (A)	Assess, by reference to the relevant Treaty provisions, legislation and case-law, the significance of the contribution of EC law to promoting equality between men and women as regards equal pay and equal treatment in respect of access to and conditions of employment, including protection in respect of pregnancy and maternity. (B)	Assess critically the operation of the new decentralised system for the enforcement of EC competition law under Council Regulation (EC) No. 1/2003 of 16 December 2002 on the implementation of the rules of competition laid down in Articles 81 and 82 of the Treaty which has been in force since 1 May 2004.

questions.description: Discuss the following features of the European Commission: (i)	the mode of appointment of its President and other member…
questions.text: Discuss the following features of the European Commission: (i)	the mode of appointment of its President and other members; (ii)	the standard of conduct required of its members; (iii)	its role in the legislative process under the EC Treaty, including its own law-making 	powers, if any.

questions.description: Answer part (A) or (B).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)With reference to the relevant case-law of the Court of Justice, …
questions.text: Answer part (A) or (B).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)With reference to the relevant case-law of the Court of Justice, explain when recourse to Article 95 EC as a legal basis is justified.  Why is it important to establish legal limits on the scope of Article 95 EC? (b)By examining the case-law, explain the sources, scope and legal status of the general principles of EC.law.Consider in particular the case-law of non-discrimination, fundamental rights and legitimate expectations.

questions.description: National Motorways Limited (“NM”) is a private Irish company, whose principal shareholder is the Minister for Transport …
questions.text: National Motorways Limited (“NM”) is a private Irish company, whose principal shareholder is the Minister for Transport (“Minister”).  By licence from the Minister, responsibility for the construction, planning, operation, maintenance and financing of all new motorways and expressways if given to NM, which is entitled to impose tariffs for the use of the motorways it designs, builds and operates.  All Irish-registered hauliers are obliged to pay an annual ‘user fee’ per vehicle for the use of NM’s motorways.  Hauliers not registered in Ireland are not required to pay this ‘user fee’, but instead pay a ‘trip fee’ each time their truck use NM’s motorways. Article 1 of a (fictitious) EC Directive obliges Member States to ensure motorway charges do not discriminate, directly or indirectly, on the grounds of the nationality of the haulier and/or the origin or destination of the vehicle.  The Directive has not been implemented into Irish law, although its implementation date passed in January 1993.  LS, an Irish haulier, is considering bringing High Court proceedings based on the Directive against NM claiming repayment of the difference between the ‘user fee’ it paid and the total ‘trip fees’ it would have paid had it been charged as a foreign haulier.  LS claims that NM should be regarded as a state body.  This is denied by NM, which relies on a 2002 Supreme Court decision in earlier proceedings concerning the period 1993 to 1999 brought by LS, where the Supreme Court held that the Directive was not directly effective and expressly refused to refer a question to the Court of Justice on the basis that it was “quite clear” that the relevant provision lacked the necessary precision to be directly effective.  In 2003, the Court of Justice, in a reference from an Austrian court, held that the provision satisfied all the requisite conditions for direct effect.  The (fictitious) Limitations of Actions (Amendment) Act 2008 restricts the time period in respect of which claims for the reimbursement of sums levied by the State or public bodies in contravention of EC law to one year preceding the initiation of legal proceedings to recover any such debt has now been brought into operation.  In Irish law the limitation period for claims against the State remains generally six years. Advise LS on whether and in what circumstances in a fresh action: (i)	it may rely on the Directive against NM; (ii)	the High Court would be obliged to disapply the 2008 Act; (iii)	it could obtain damages from NM and/or the State under EC law in the event it is 	precluded from bringing its claim for reimbursement; (iv)	the breach by the Supreme Court in the earlier case LS brought could entitle LS now 	to damages from the State in respect of the period 1993 to 1999.

questions.description: Write a note on any three of the following cases, setting out, inter alia, their core facts, the key issues of EU law th…
questions.text: Write a note on any three of the following cases, setting out, inter alia, their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Court of Justice/Court of First Instance: (i)	Case 26/62 Van Gend en Loos [1963] ECR1; (ii)	Case 22/70 Commission v Council (“ERTA”) [1971] ECR 263; (iii)	Case C-167/02 P Rothley & Ors v European Parliament [2004] ECR I-3149; (iv)	Case C-200/02 Zhu and Chen [2004] ECR I-9925; (v)	Case C-411/05 Féliz Palacios de la Villa [2007] ECR I-8531; (vi)	Case C-341/05 Laval un Partneri [2007] ECR I-11767; (vii)	Case T-201/04 Microsoft v Commission [2007] ECR II-3601.

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with Articles 25, 28 EC to 30 EC a…
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with Articles 25, 28 EC to 30 EC and/or 90 EC as applicable: (i) Excise duty is payable on intoxicating liqueur made in Ireland within four weeks of its release from licensed bonded warehouses, or, if it is not stored in such bonded warehouses, within four weeks of its delivery for retail sale.  Excise duty on imported intoxicating liqueur is payable within four weeks of its importation; (ii) A statutory provision prohibits the affixing of ‘tinted film’ to the windows of mechanically propelled vehicles ‘with the exception of lawful stickers and dark, non-reflective film to the goods compartments of good vehicles’.  The government argues that the provision aims to prevent crime and ensure compliance with the legal obligation to wear seatbelts; (iii) A statutory prohibition on the towing trailers by mopeds and motorcycles. The government argues the measure does not hinder the free movement of trailers as they may be used for other purposes and that the prohibition is necessary to ensure road safety.

questions.description: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). Note: Each of the scenarios arises after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/…
questions.text: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). Note: Each of the scenarios arises after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/EC on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States. (i) Hans, a German national, has worked in the security industry in Ireland for several years.  His application to become a trainee Garda was refused because of his non-Irish nationality and because he was 36 at the date of his application, the cut-off age being 35. A limited number of traineeships are open to non-Irish nationals, where for operational reasons recruits with non-Irish backgrounds and foreign language skills are required.  In addition, no restriction has been placed on UK national applicants joining the force.  The age restriction is applied 	across-the-board to all applicants without regard to their experience or level of health and fitness.  Advise Hans on the legality of the refusal of his application under EC law. (ii) Mary, an Irish national, lived and worked in Belgium for several years before returning to Ireland in 2007, whereafter she met and married Aman, a failed asylum seeker from Afghanistan.  The Minister for Justice, Equality and Law Reform recently served a notice of intention to deport on Aman.  Advise Mary as to whether Aman has an EC legal right to reside in Ireland as her spouse. The Department of Justice argues that Directive 2004/38/EC does not apply as Mary is an Irish citizen resident in Ireland, who married after her return to Ireland. (iii)	Dominique, a French national, has worked as a schoolteacher in Ireland for seven 	years.  She was arrested and charged with possession of cannabis for the purpose of 	supply, but convicted only of the lesser criminal offence of possession of the drug.  	Dominique was sentenced to three years imprisonment and ordered to be expelled 	from Ireland upon her release from prison for 5 years.  She has a two-year old child 	who is an Irish national.  Advise Dominique on the legality of her expulsion under EC 	law.

questions.description: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)	A market study commissioned by the Government of Erse (and islan…
questions.text: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)	A market study commissioned by the Government of Erse (and island Member State whose 	northernmost region forms part of another neighbouring Member State) of overcapacity in the Erse’s beef processing industry recommended reducing the number of processors.  Following this report, the major processors from the whole island formed the Beef Industry Development Society Ltd (BIDS), which formulated an arrangement (‘the BIDS arrangement’) whose aim was to reduce the total capacity of the processing industry by 25%.  The arrangement provides that member processors desirous of leaving (“goers”) would enter into agreements with BIDS in which they undertake to leave the processing industry, to decommission their processing plants and to respect a two-year non-compete clause (‘exit agreements’). The exit agreements are also to include the obligations not to use land associated with the decommissioned plants for the purposes of beef processing for a period of five years (‘restriction on use’) and to sell the equipment used for primary beef processing to processors in Ireland only for use as back-up equipment or spare parts, or otherwise only outside Ireland (‘restriction on disposal’).  In return, goers are to be compensated by BIDS, with such compensation payments being financed by its members who are staying in processing (‘stayers’). On being notified of the first such proposed agreement between a processor and BIDS, Erse’s competition regulator informed BIDS that it did not consider the agreement to be compatible with Article 81 EC and sought a declaration from Erse’s 	High Court that the agreement infringed Article 81 EC.  BIDS has resisted the application on the basis that the agreement does not fall under the prohibition contained in Article 81(1) EC as having neither an anti-competitive object nor anti-competitive effects. Assess whether this agreement infringes the prohibition set out in Article 81(1) EC and, if so, whether it could satisfy the conditions required for exemption under Article 81(3) EC. (b) ATCE, the automobile and touring club of Erse, is a non-profit making organisation which,	inter alia, in that (hypothetical) Member State organises motor sports  competitions and concludes sponsorships, advertising and insurance agreements for such competitions.  Furthermore, ATCE has been entrusted by Erse authorities with the supervision of national racing events and a statutory consultee of the Ministry of Transport of Erse in respect of applications for motor sports event licences throughout Erse from other bodies.  FE-1 is a rival non-profit-making motor sports 	association whose activity likewise includes the organisation of motorcycling events in Erse.  FE-1 has regularly found that its applications for licences to organise events are frustrated by delays on the part of ATCE in giving its opinion thereon to the 	Ministry and/or by negative opinions that normally result in Ministry refusals. Advise FE-1 as to whether it could bring proceedings to challenge ATCE’s role on the basis of Articles 82 EC and 86 EC.  In particular, consider whether ATCE would be likely to be considered an undertaking for the purposes of EC competition law and, if so, whether its dual role (organising events and participating in the licensing process for other event organisers) is compatible with Articles 82 EC and 86 EC.

questions.description: Analyse the role, power and obligations of the Commission under Article 88 EC in assessing potential State aid measures …
questions.text: Analyse the role, power and obligations of the Commission under Article 88 EC in assessing potential State aid measures notified to it.

questions.description: “The story of the European Parliament is one of gradual transformation from a relatively powerless Assembly under the 19…
questions.text: “The story of the European Parliament is one of gradual transformation from a relatively powerless Assembly under the 1952 ECSC Treaty to the considerably strengthened institution it represents today” (Craig & De Burca, EU Law: Texts, Cases and Materials, (Oxford, OUP, 4td edition), 2007, p.58). By reference to, inter alia, Treaty provisions and relevant case-law of the Court of Justice, analyse this statement with particular reference to: (i)	the evolution of the European Parliament’s role in the EC legislative process; (ii)	the features that distinguish its legislative role from that on any national parliament.

questions.description: On 1 February 2008, West County Council, a local authority in the (fictitious) EU Member State of Boomtonia, granted Win…
questions.text: On 1 February 2008, West County Council, a local authority in the (fictitious) EU Member State of Boomtonia, granted Windy Ltd ‘special planning permission’ (‘SPP’) for construction of a windfarm in West County.  West County Council did not carry out an ‘environmental detriment assessment’ (‘EDA’) in respect of the application, as none was required under the planning law of Boomtonia.  On 1 July 2008, the ECJ decided in Commission v Boomtonia that the Boomtonian planning practice of granting SPP without EDA was contrary to the (fictitious) EC EDA Directive. The Green Alliance, a group of local residents opposed to the development, wish to have the national court quash West County Council’s grant of SPP.  In addition, Mr Farmer would like compensation for the reduction in the value of his property occasioned by the construction of the windfarm, which has rendered his farm prone to landslides in times of heavy rain. Advise the Green Alliance on whether and in what circumstances: it may rely on the EC EDA Directive against West County Council in order to have grant of SPP quashed; it may rely on the EC EDA Directive against Windy Ltd to stop the development. Advise Mr Farmer on whether: he is entitled to damages from Boomtonia for its breach of EC law.  Would it make 	any difference to your answer if West County Council’s decision had been taken on 1 	August 2008? he is entitled to damages from Windy Ltd under EU law.

questions.description: Discuss by reference to relevant case-law of the Court of Justice: the interpretation of the term “court or tribunal of …
questions.text: Discuss by reference to relevant case-law of the Court of Justice: the interpretation of the term “court or tribunal of a Member State” for the purposes 	of the second paragraph of Article 234 EC; the interpretation of the term “court or tribunal of a Member State against whose 	decisions there is no judicial remedy under national law” for the purposes of the third 	paragraph of Article 234 EC; the legal effects of a preliminary ruling from the Court of Justice under Article 234 EC 	for, firstly, the referring court and, secondly, for other national courts or tribunals.

questions.description: Write a note on any three of the following cases, setting out for each case, the relevant facts, key EC legal issues and…
questions.text: Write a note on any three of the following cases, setting out for each case, the relevant facts, key EC legal issues and how the ECJ addressed those issues. Case 106/11 Simmenthal [1978] ECR 629; Case 44/79 Hauer v Rheinland-Pfalz [1979] ECR 3727; Case C-376/98 Germany v European Parliament and Council [2000] ECR I-8419; Case C-50/00 P Unión de Pequeños Agricultores v Council [2003] ECR I-6677; Case C-280/00 Altmark Trans and Regierunspräsidium Magdeburg [2003] ECR I-	7747; Case C-224/01 Köbler [2003] ECR I-10239.

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of each of the following measures and practices with Articles 10 EC, 28 to 30 EC and/or 49 to 5…
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of each of the following measures and practices with Articles 10 EC, 28 to 30 EC and/or 49 to 50 EC, as applicable: Irish animal rights activists routinely blockade ports to hinder the export of live cattle 	to Spain, as they believe that the trade embodies cruelty to animals.  The Irish 	authorities invariably permit the protests on the basis that they are an exercise of the 	fundamental right to protest under Irish law; A Swedish trade union is boycotting the provision of building services by an Estonian 	building contractor, because the Estonian building contractor is paying its workers 	less than the minimum wage agreed in the collective agreement that binds Swedish 	employers and trade unions in the building industry.  Swedish labour law permits 	trade unions to engage in this sort of collective action, in order to protect the 	collectively agreed Swedish minimum wage, wherever an employer, whether Swedish 	or foreign, has not accepted the relevant collective agreement; The law in Finland prohibits all advertisement of alcoholic beverages available for sale 	in the traditional or electronic media, unless the alcoholic content of the beverage is 	less than 2.5%.  Finland argues the measure is justified on public health grounds. Set out your advice in respect of each law.

questions.description: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). John, an Irish citizen, has been accepted onto a course of study at a Belgian universi…
questions.text: Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). John, an Irish citizen, has been accepted onto a course of study at a Belgian university. The Belgian authorities refuse his application for the student maintenance grant provided for Belgian students, on the basis that he has not been resident in Belgium for 5 years before commencing his course of study.  Belgium argues that its refusal is permitted under Article 24 of Directive 2004/38/EC on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.  John seeks your advice as to whether the refusal is lawful under EC law. Mairead is an Irish national, who lived and worked in the United Kingdom for several years before returning to Ireland last year, whereafter she met and married Hanam, a failed asylum seeker from Afghanistan.  The Minister for Justice, Equality and Law Reform recently served a notice of intention to deport of Hanam.  Mairead has asked for advice as to whether Hanam has an EC legal right to reside in Ireland as her spouse. Ireland argues that Directive 2004/38/EC does not apply as Mairead is an Irish citizen resident in Ireland. Roman, an Italian national holding a doctorate in German language and literature, has been refused employment with a private financial institution in Bolzano, the German-speaking region of Northern Italy, on the sole basis that he does not hold the requisite official certificate of bilingualism in Italian and German issued by the Bolzano ministry of education.  Roman seeks your advice as to whether the refusal to engage him is compatible with EC law. Set out your advice to John, Mairead and Roman.  Note that each of the scenarios arises after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/EC on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.

questions.description: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. Advise Joan and Mary in the following situations: Joan has worked as…
questions.text: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. Advise Joan and Mary in the following situations: Joan has worked as a manager for a health authority for the past five years. Previously, following her graduation from university, she worked in the private sector for several years before becoming a homemaker for a period of six years.  She has discovered that a number of male colleagues, who hold similar managerial positions to Joan for periods of between two and four years, are all paid more than her.  In response to Joan’s complaint, the health authority argues that the differences in pay 	are justified by the male comparators’ longer service records with the authority, albeit 	mostly at lower grades than Joan. Advise Joan as to the lawfulness of the authority’s use of the criterion of length of 	service as a detriment of pay under EC sex discrimination law, in particular Article 	141 EC. Mary is a waitress working for a restaurant on a 6-month temporary contract of employment.  Just as her contract is due to expire, she discovers that she is pregnant.  On informing her employer, he decides not to renew her contract on its 	expiry.  As Mary would be taking maternity leave in the following 6 months, he feels 	she would be unable to fulfil the terms of a new contract. Advise Mary as to whether the refusal to renew her contract under these 	circumstances is contrary to EC sex discrimination law. OR Consider whether the Brussels Convention of 27th September 1968 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters, as now largely replaced by Council Regulation (EC) No. 44/2001 Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters (“the Brussels I Regulation”) establishes clear jurisdictional rules for determining which courts have jurisdiction in civil and commercial matters involving inter-state elements. In your answer refer, in particular, to the scope and operation of the principal jurisdiction rule under Article 2(1) of Regulation No. 44/2001 and the special rules laid down by Article 5(1) and (3).

questions.description: “[...] Price competition is the essence of free and open competition which is the objective of Community policy to estab…
questions.text: “[...] Price competition is the essence of free and open competition which is the objective of Community policy to establish on the internal market.  It favours more efficient firms and it is for the benefit of consumers both in the short and long run.  Dominant firms not only have the right but should be encouraged to compete on price” (Opinion of Advocate General Fennelly in Case C-395/96 P Compagnie Maritime Belge v Commission [2000] ECR I-1365. Discuss this statement with particular reference to the case-law of the Community judicature relating to the constraints placed by Article 82 EC on the pricing activities (such as concerning predatory pricing, price squeezing and excessive prices) of dominant firms.

questions.description: Assess whether the EC Treaty, as interpreted in the case-law of the Community Courts, establishes a discernible separati…
questions.text: Assess whether the EC Treaty, as interpreted in the case-law of the Community Courts, establishes a discernible separation of legislative and executive powers between the Commission, the Council of Ministers and the European Parliament. What reforms, if any, would you suggest in this regard?

questions.description: With reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case-law, outline the composition, mode of appointment and jurisdiction…
questions.text: With reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case-law, outline the composition, mode of appointment and jurisdiction of the Court of Justice and the Court of First Instance.  How has the Courts’ Treaty-given role of “ensuring that in the interpretation and application of the Treaty, the law is observed” influenced the development of its jurisdiction?

questions.description: “Fundamental rights are treated in a paradoxical manner.  On one hand, EU law stresses the indivisibility of fundamental…
questions.text: “Fundamental rights are treated in a paradoxical manner.  On one hand, EU law stresses the indivisibility of fundamental rights.  Civil, political, economic rights are to be equally valued and protected.  On the other, it does not protect their indefeasibility.  Fundamental rights are only to be protected insofar as they fall within the field of EC law” Chalmers et al, European Union Law (Cambridge, 2006), at pp. 268/9. Evaluate this claim in light of the protection of fundamental rights in relevant Treaty provisions and in the case-law of the Community Courts.

questions.description: Write a note on any three of the following cases, appraising, inter alia, the significance of each chosen case for the e…
questions.text: Write a note on any three of the following cases, appraising, inter alia, the significance of each chosen case for the effectiveness of EC law: (i)	Case 6/64 Costa v ENEL [1964] ECR 585; (ii)	Case 41/74 Van Duyn [1974] ECR 1337; (iii)	Cases C-6/90 and C-9/90 Francovich [1991] ECR I-5537; (iv) 	Case C-91/92 Faccini Dori [1994] ECR I-3325; (v)	Case C-377/98 Netherlands v Parliament and Council (Biotechnology Directive) [2001] ECR I-7079; (vi)	Case C-105/03 Pupino [2005] ECR I-5285.

questions.description: The Attorney General of Ireland has asked you to advise on the compatibility of the following proposed measures with EC …
questions.text: The Attorney General of Ireland has asked you to advise on the compatibility of the following proposed measures with EC law, in particular Articles 25 EC, 28 EC, 49 EC and 90 EC: (i)	A scientific advisory body has recommended to the Government that all bread manufactured or marketed in Ireland should be required to be fortified with folic acid.  The body believes that such a measure would reduce the number of babies born in Ireland with neural tube defects, such as spina bifida.  Evidence from a number of third world countries suggests that such a mandatory fortification policy is effective.  The Department of Health and Children is considering the introduction of such mandatory fortification. (ii)	The Bord na gCon (Irish Greyhound Board) has proposed to the Department of Agriculture and Food that a 1% levy be introduced and imposed on the sale, both for the domestic and international markets, of greyhounds raised in Ireland.  60% of the revenues generated from the levy are to be used by Bord na gCon to provide financial assistance towards the upgrading of facilities at greyhound racing stadia around the country on foot of approved development plans submitted by their owners, while the remaining 40% is to be used to finance the promotion of the sale of Irish greyhounds in other Member States, especially in the United Kingdom. (iii)	The Department of Justice, Equality and Law Reform, concerned at what it perceives to be increasingly aggressive behaviour by young people, has proposed to the Minister for Justice, Equality and Law Reform that he consider introducing a prohibition on the sale, rental or exploitation (in games halls, amusement arcades, public houses and youth centres) of any computer games that involve the participants mimicking violence, in particular those that involve the mimicking of shooting with firearms. Set out your advice in three separate and succinct opinions.

questions.description: A partner in your law firm asks you to provide legal advice for her perusal in respect of each of the following three cl…
questions.text: A partner in your law firm asks you to provide legal advice for her perusal in respect of each of the following three clients.  Set out your advice in three separate and succinct opinions. Heidi, a German national who has been living as a new age traveller in west Cork, joins a sect called the ‘Screeching Loolahs’.  All members of the sect are required to work for about 12 hours per week in the sect’s coffee shop in Bantry.  She spends the rest of her time practising meditation and screeching.  The sect encourages Heidi to apply for a training allowance available to workers who retrain on returning (after an extended absence) to the workforce.  Advise Heidi on whether she is eligible for this grant. Ella, an Italian national, has been living and working in Ireland for over two years.  She recently married Vladamir in Moscow.  Vladamir is a Russian national and an old flame of Ella’s from her days in the 1990s as a student of Slavic languages in St Petersburg.  Advise Ella and Vladamir on whether Vladamir has a right under EC law as Ella’s husband to enter and reside in Ireland. Jacques is a French national who moved to Dublin after the death of his parents in a car crash when he was sixteen to live with his grandmother, who is also a French national and a professional freelance musician.  After completing his leaving certificate two years later, Jacques applied for a local authority maintenance grant to attend university from Dublin City Council.  Although his grandmother’s income and his own income from his inheritance were lower than the cut-off threshold for the grant, Jacques was refused a grant on the basis that he did not satisfy the residency requirement, pursuant to which five years continuous residence is required on the date of application.  Jacques has heard that the requirement is occasionally waived by the City Council, in its absolute discretion, for applicants of Irish nationality who have resided abroad for fewer than three of the five years required, provided the move abroad occurred because one or both of the applicant’s parents (or guardians) moved to work abroad.

questions.description: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)	Ellas Rapid Constructions is a Greek construction company that h…
questions.text: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)	Ellas Rapid Constructions is a Greek construction company that has won, through an Irish subsidiary, a tender project to build a new motorway in County Galway.  Through its Irish subsidiary, Rapid Constructions (Dublin) Ltd, it seeks to recruit most of the skilled workers that it will need to carry out the project for the National Roads Authority.  Its Irish subsidiary is coming under “pressure” from certain local trade unions to recruit two Irish workers for every one non-Irish EU citizen on the project.  The unions have also indicated that they will organise an industrial boycott of the project if, as proposed in Ellas’ successful tender, 50% of the skilled construction workers needed are posted to Ireland from Greece.  According to the call for tenders, at least 50% of the general labourers required to complete the project should “as far as practicable be drawn from the workforce of the West of Ireland, or from persons on the liver register and registered as resident in the West of Ireland region” , which region is defined as including the counties of the province of Connaught”. As a specialist in EC law, Rapid Constructions (Dublin) Ltd has sought your advice as to whether: (i)	if it were to limit the number of skilled positions available to non-Irish EU citizens on the project, it would infringe any provisions of the EC Treaty or secondary legislation; (ii)	the threatened boycott of the trade unions could be successfully challenged on the basis of the EC Treaty before the Irish courts; (iii)	it could legitimately refuse, on the basis of Articles 43 EC and/or 49 EC, to respect the tender stipulation regarding the engagement of general labourers. Set out your advice. – OR – (b)	“Article 141 (1) EC lays down the principle that equal work or work of equal value must be remunerated in the same way, whether it is performed by a man or a woman [...and] that principle, which is a particular expression of the general principle of equality which prohibits comparable situations from being treated differently unless the difference is objectively justified, forms a part of the foundations of the Community” (see Case C-17/05 Cadman [2006] ECR I-9583, at paragraphs 27 and 28). Discuss the contribution of the Court of Justice in its case-law on equal pay under Article 141 EC (formerly Article 119 EC) and related legislation to the goal of equality between men and women.

questions.description: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)	“There is nothing, in principle, to prevent two or more independ…
questions.text: Answer part (a) or (b).  Do not attempt both parts. (a)	“There is nothing, in principle, to prevent two or more independent economic entities from being, on a specific market, united by such economic links that, by virtue of that fact, together they hold a dominant position vis-à-vis the other operators on the same market.  This could be the case, for example, where two or more independent undertakings jointly have, through agreements or licences, a technological lead affording them the power to behave to an appreciable extent independently of their customers and ultimately of their consumers” (Joined Cases T-68, T-77 and T-78/89 Italian Flat Glass [1992] ECR II-1403, paragraph 358). How has the Community judicature developed the abovementioned principle of collective (or joint) dominance since the judgment of the Court of First Instance in Italian Flat Glass? – OR – (b)	It has been argued that the Community Courts have “apparently been willing to apply Article [82] EC where there is no effective abuse by undertakings of a dominant position, but where a State measure produces the same effect” (Bacon, “State Regulation of the Market and EC Competition Rules” (1997) ECLRev. 283). Having regard to the case-law of the Community Courts interpreting Article 86 EC, assess critically the limits to the scope of Article 86 EC.
